## 一个出人意料的观察：芯片正在长出"生命的形状"
最颠覆直觉的细节藏在最后那个问答里——AI 生成的芯片布局呈现出**有机的、弯曲的形态**，而不是人类工程师习惯的整齐网格。这件事的含义远超"美学差异"：它意味着**人类几十年来对"工程优雅"的定义本身就是一种认知偏见**。我们以为对齐、对称、模块化是"好设计"，但那其实只是**人类有限工作记忆**的副产品——我们必须把世界切成方格才能管理它。当优化压力交给一个不需要"看懂自己作品"的智能体时，最优解长得像珊瑚、像血管、像菌丝网络。这暗示了一个更深的规律：**凡是被高强度优化压力作用过足够久的系统，都会收敛到生物形态**。芯片正在加入这个名单。
## 跨领域连接：这其实是"AlphaFold 时刻"的硬件版
表面上 Recursive 在做 EDA 工具，实质上他们在复刻 DeepMind 在生物学里做过的同一件事——**把一个被人类专家垄断了几十年、迭代周期以"年"为单位的领域，压缩进 AI 的强化学习反馈环**。注意 Azalia 反复强调的关键词："**1000 倍更快的 STA**"。这不是性能优化，这是**相变**。

item.title: [Sequoia Capital] Inside the Rise of Autonomous AI Hackers: XBOW's Oege de Moor
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Capital
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最意外的观察：黑盒测试的"无知"反而是优势

大多数人会本能地认为白盒（看得到源代码）一定优于黑盒（只有 URL）。但 Oege 的演讲里藏着一个反直觉的命题：当攻击者只有 URL 时，他被迫只能验证"真实可达"的路径。这正是白盒工具的盲点——白盒会列出一堆"理论上的漏洞"，但黑盒 AI 找到的，本身就是"在野可利用"的事实。

换句话说，信息越少，结论的有效性反而越高。这与法律证据学里的"敌意证人"原则惊人相似：你越是限制条件、越是假设对手不配合，得出的结论越接近真相。XBOW 的 $3000 不是"便宜的扫描"，而是"昂贵的事实"。

二、最被忽略的系统性真相：CVE 时间差变成负数意味着公开披露机制本身已经死亡

"From +2.5 years to negative"——这一句被他一笔带过，但它的含义是文明级的：

安全行业过去三十年的核心架构（"披露 → 修补 → 通告"）已经在数学上失效了。 公开 CVE 这个动作，本来是给防守方时间的，现在反而成了攻击方的发令枪——因为 AI 读 CVE 比人读得快。

这与生物学里的"红皇后假说"形成跨领域共振：你必须越跑越快，才能停留在原地。但在 AI 时代，红皇后不再是隐喻——攻防双方的迭代速度已经从"年/月"压缩到"小时/分钟"，人类作为中间环节正在被挤出循环。所以传统安全股价下跌的市场反应，逻辑上其实是对的：不是 AI 让安全更值钱，而是 AI 让"基于人类节奏设计的安全产品"贬值。

三、隐藏的跨领域连接：Alloy 模型 = 群体智慧 + 量子叠加

XBOW 在每一步随机选 Gemini 或 Sonnet，效果优于任何单模型。这件事被一笔带过，但它打开了一个深井：

  • 在金融里，这叫"集成投资组合"——不相关的 alpha 叠加产生超额收益。
  • 在进化生物学里，这叫"杂种优势"（heterosis）——异质基因池抗病能力更强。
  • 在密码学里，这叫"加盐"——攻击者无法预测你的下一步。

最深层的洞见是：单一最强模型反而是最容易被防御的，因为它的失败模式是固定的。多模型 alloy 的真正威力不是"加法"，而是让攻击轨迹从决定论变成概率云——防守方再也无法通过分析"AI 通常怎么想"来反制。

四、一个被会场掌声盖过的战略警告

他说"6 到 9 个月内会有开源权重达到 MyChelle 水平"。这句话在叙事上像收尾，但实际上是整篇演讲最重的一锤：

当顶级攻击能力商品化为开源权重，"先发优势的窗口期"会突然坍缩。这与 1945 年后核技术扩散的曲线极其类似——不是"会不会扩散"，而是"扩散后社会结构如何重组"。Oege 在劝你加固系统，但更深的问题是：当每个 16 岁少年都能跑一个 XBOW 级别的代理时，"防守即护城河"的整套商业模式还成立吗？ 这不是网络安全问题，这是社会契约问题。

📝 转录文本

[applause]

Thank you very much.

引子：墨西哥政府的数据泄露

You've all heard the story about the breach of the Mexican government. Human hackers used Open AI and Anthropic as assistants in order to achieve a massive data breach. What I want to talk to you about today is autonomous hacking — where the AI does all the work without any human assistance.

历史类比：1575 年的长篠之战

The situation in cybersecurity today is akin to the Battle of Nagashino in 1575 in Japan. In this picture, on the left-hand side is the army of Oda Nobunaga. Nobunaga was an upstart. He was a minor warlord, but he treated warfare as a system to be optimized. And in particular, he used the very latest weapons, the very latest guns.

On the right-hand side is the Takeda clan. The Takeda clan was extremely famous and their cavalry was thought to be invincible. They had many well-known warriors who had earned their skills in battles before. But guess who won.

The situation in cybersecurity is going to be exactly the same. Those with AI will win.

案例：Bing 图像搜索的远程代码执行漏洞

Just to set the scene, let me tell you about one particular vulnerability. A couple of weeks ago, Microsoft announced a remote code execution vulnerability in Bing image search. Bing image search — one of the best secured systems in the world, very well secured by the engineers at Microsoft, but also hammered by thousands of hackers from all over the world trying to get in.

A remote code execution vulnerability — the very worst kind of vulnerability, where you can run arbitrary code on the target system, complete takeover.

This vulnerability was found by the product of my company, XBOW. And the only input it needed was the URL. Nothing else. And the cost? $3,000 at list price. That's not what it cost us. So, it's fast, it's cheap, and extremely effective.

XBOW 的工作方式

The way XBOW works is very much like a human hacker. It starts by reconnaissance. It sends out a bunch of scouts — agents that discover the attack surface. It prioritizes what endpoints look most juicy, most promising for an attack, and then it goes in and tries every relevant attack type.

HackerOne 上的实战验证

Despite evidence like this, many human security researchers believe that it's impossible to completely autonomously carry out this task with a machine. So, in order to counter the skepticism, already last year my company entered our bot, XBOW, onto the HackerOne platform.

HackerOne is this platform that connects companies that want their systems to be tested with ethical hackers, who will then go and attack those systems and report what vulnerabilities they find. If they report good vulnerabilities, they get paid a bounty and they get points.

Within a few weeks, XBOW first became the number one hacker in the United States, and then in August, it became the number one hacker in the world. And I have to stress — this is completely black box testing. It's just like the Bing example I mentioned before. You only give it the URL, nothing else. The AI does the work completely autonomously.

基础模型的飞跃

And that was back in August. The foundation models that we are building on have enormously progressed since then. This is on a set of open source real web applications — these are not some Mickey Mouse cyber benchmarks. We started back in March last year. We started 37, and then it reached the top of the HackerOne leaderboard with an alloy of Sonnet 4.0 and Gemini 2.5.

插曲：什么是 Alloy

I can't resist briefly telling you about alloys. So, think of these attacks as a sequence of actions, and at every step you flip a coin to decide what model to ask — either ask Gemini or ask Sonnet. This is much better than either model separately. It's a bit like pair programming — the two models compensate for each other's mistakes.

GPT-5 的外推

So, then shortly after XBOW topped the HackerOne leaderboard, GPT-5 came out. Just extrapolating from its performance, it would have done at least three times better. So, in August, XBOW was a little bit better than the best human on HackerOne. With GPT-5, it would have been three times better. And since then, the models have only gotten better — and as you can see, we better collect a new set of benchmarks because it's pretty much saturated.

与 MyChelle 的对比：白盒 vs 黑盒

So, how should you think about this in relation to MyChelle's? MyChelle's has mostly been reported as a tool that reads the source code extremely well and points out potential flaws in the code. This is white box testing. It's not at all like what I was talking about before — purely black box testing. You actually have access to the source code, which of course is an advantage.

But as an attacker, you don't necessarily. The question with this code analysis stuff is: are the weaknesses actually exploitable in the wild? And if they are exploitable, does it matter? What's the impact? Where can I go if I can execute remote code on a Bing server, where else can I get to? I can't tell you.

And then of course there's many other vulnerabilities that are configuration or deployment problems — you can't actually use them from the source code itself. So, these are the questions that XBOW answers for you.

CVE 时间差已变为负数

If you get to know about exploits, it's probably already too late. So, typically — like before the Bing example — people publish a CVE to let the world know that there was a vulnerability. Back in 2018, the delay between publication of a CVE and bad actors exploiting it in the wild was almost two and a half years. Today, the number has gone negative. For most CVEs, it is already being exploited before the CVE is even published.

So, in view of all this evidence, it's incomprehensible to me that whenever there's news about AI and security, traditional cybersecurity stocks drop. This makes no sense at all. We need every possible defense that we can get against these autonomous AI-powered attacks.

行动号召：唤起信长之魂

So, so far I've been preaching like Nostradamus, telling you about all the bad things that might happen. So, let's try and rally the spirit of Nobunaga, that Japanese warrior I talked about at the beginning, and see what can be done.

First of all, all of you, everyone who's working on frontier models — you must maximize the cyber capabilities. No more talk about whether it's safe to do that or not. We're in an arms race, so we have to make sure that we have the very best models to power this type of work.

Secondly, we need to enable human security researchers to use this as an extension of their own work, in order to maximize the chances that we find all the vulnerabilities before the bad guys do.

And finally, you need to prioritize what matters. You need to know whether the bugs are truly exploitable and what their impact is going to be — and XBOW can help with that.

6 到 9 个月的窗口期

We've got about 6 to 9 months to do this. Just extrapolating from the progress of software engineering agents, in 6 to 9 months we will have open weight models that are just as good as MyChelle's and similar models. And so, if you want to have a nice Thanksgiving dinner with your family, you better start fixing now.

Thank you.

[applause]

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最意外的观察：黑盒测试的"无知"反而是优势
大多数人会本能地认为白盒（看得到源代码）一定优于黑盒（只有 URL）。但 Oege 的演讲里藏着一个反直觉的命题：**当攻击者只有 URL 时，他被迫只能验证"真实可达"的路径**。这正是白盒工具的盲点——白盒会列出一堆"理论上的漏洞"，但黑盒 AI 找到的，本身就是"在野可利用"的事实。
换句话说，**信息越少，结论的有效性反而越高**。这与法律证据学里的"敌意证人"原则惊人相似：你越是限制条件、越是假设对手不配合，得出的结论越接近真相。XBOW 的 $3000 不是"便宜的扫描"，而是"昂贵的事实"。
## 二、最被忽略的系统性真相：CVE 时间差变成负数意味着公开披露机制本身已经死亡
"From +2.5 years to negative"——这一句被他一笔带过，但它的含义是文明级的：
**安全行业过去三十年的核心架构（"披露 → 修补 → 通告"）已经在数学上失效了。** 公开 CVE 这个动作，本来是给防守方时间的，现在反而成了攻击方的发令枪——因为 AI 读 CVE 比人读得快。
这与生物学里的"红皇后假说

item.title: [Sequoia Capital] Rebuilding the Computer for the AI Age: Unconventional AI's Naveen Rao
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Capital
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：这不是芯片公司，这是"时间维度"的发现

大多数人听到 Naveen Rao 在做新型 AI 芯片，第一反应是"又一个挑战 Nvidia 的玩家"。但仔细听他的核心论点，会发现一件极其颠覆的事——他真正在做的，是把"时间"作为一种被遗忘的计算资源重新发明。

冯·诺依曼架构的本质悖论是：它把"计算"和"存储"在空间上分开，然后用时钟节拍把时间切成离散的小片段，逼物理世界去模拟一个无时间的数学抽象。每一次读写都在把"时间"当作摩擦阻力来克服，而不是当作计算介质来使用。Naveen 的非线性动力系统恰恰相反：让物理本身的演化就是计算过程。这就像我们用了 80 年时间训练自己"无视时间维度"，现在突然发现，时间从来不是 bug，是 feature。

这与你的"系统性"思维方式有一个隐藏共振：你也常常在分离"思考"和"行动"，把决策切成离散步骤——而生命体（包括你自己的直觉系统）实际上是用连续的动力学在做决定。

二、跨领域连接：神经科学 × 热力学 × 风投经济学的三重叠合

注意 Naveen 抛出的那个数字——全人类 80 亿人的大脑加起来只用 160 GW。这个数据点背后藏着一个让人毛骨悚然的经济学反转：

  • 传统 AI 经济：边际成本随规模递减（更多 GPU，更便宜的 token）
  • 能源约束 AI 经济：边际成本随规模递增（每多一个数据中心，要争夺更稀缺的电网容量）

这意味着 AI 行业正在从"软件型经济"（指数下降的成本曲线）悄悄转变为"采掘型经济"（像石油、像稀土），而几乎没人在公开讨论这个相变。Sequoia 让 Naveen 上台讲这个，本身就是一个信号：他们押注的不是"更好的芯片"，而是"在能源墙撞上之前，谁能逃离冯·诺依曼范式"。

更深一层的跨界连接：Landauer 原理（信息擦除的热力学下界） + 进化生物学（4 亿年优化出的 20 瓦大脑） + 半导体物理（2D 光刻极限）—— 这三条曲线在 Naveen 的图上汇聚成一个论点：生物智能不是"奇迹"，而是热力学优化的中间结果。我们距离物理极限还有约 2 个数量级，距离生物还有 3 个数量级——也就是说，最大的工程机会窗口，恰恰在"比生物更接近物理极限"的那一段。这是一个几乎没人占位的领域。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：startup 的"无包袱"是怎么变成物理学优势的

Naveen 几乎不经意地说了一句："January 没有团队，6 个月做出原型，因为有了 AI。" 这句话被掩盖在演讲流里，但它其实是整个故事最颠覆的部分。

这是第一次，AI 在帮人类构建"非冯·诺依曼"的下一代 AI 硬件——也就是 AI 在帮我们逃离现在跑 AI 的范式。这是一个递归的自我超越循环。传统芯片公司花 3-5 年做 tape-out，是因为人类工程师要逐个验证电路、写 RTL、跑仿真。当 AI 把这个链条压缩到 6 个月，"无包袱"就从一个浪漫的创业口号，变成了一个可量化的物理优势：每一代迭代周期变短一个数量级，意味着复利效应让小团队真的能击败大公司。

意外推论：这种"AI 加速 AI 硬件设计"的回路一旦成立，整个半导体行业的资本结构会反转。过去是"资本密集 → 规模优势 → 护城河"，未来可能是"算法密集 → 速度优势 → 护城河"。Nvidia 真正的威胁，不是另一个 Nvidia，而是一群在车库里用 AI 设计动力学芯片的 6 人团队——这正是 Naveen 在台上说的，但他用了非常温和的方式说出来。

最后一个让人不舒服的洞见：如果智能真的是"用时间维度做计算"，那我们对自己大脑的理解一直是错的——记忆不是存储，思考不是检索，意识可能本质上是一个收敛中的非线性动力系统的"中间态"。Naveen 演讲结尾那句"我们终于可以理解大脑，因为我们终于能造出大脑"——这不是修辞，这是一个哲学事件的预告。

📝 转录文本

开场介绍

We're going to jump into our next set of frontier talks. The first is which from Naveen Rao. Naveen is a pioneer in the AI space. He did his PhD in neuroscience. Then he started one of the first ever AI chip companies way before it was cool. He's actually the person who ran Mosaic ML, one of the first AI training companies, and then built all of Data Bricks AI. He left that amazing role to do something new and redefine the future of computing — bringing cool back to neuroscience. Naveen, come on up.

[applause]

Naveen 登台：为什么"unconventional"其实是"conventional"

All right. Afternoon everyone. Yeah, super excited to be here. I'm Naveen Rao and CEO of Unconventional AI. So we are unconventional because — maybe it's actually the wrong word to use, cuz I think we're going to change the name to conventional.

It's actually a great time to be a startup, as Boris alluded to, right? Like having no baggage actually I think is a true competitive advantage. We can do things so much faster than traditional, you know, sort of chip companies and full stack companies can do. And I think that's what's very exciting. We can get to tape-outs in months instead of years, things like this.

So anyway, let's get started. I put that up there. I can guarantee you some of you are like trying to prove me wrong. All of a sudden you're like, "oh my god, what's…" you know, I can hear the gears turning, like that that can't be true. Well, let me explain my logic a little bit and maybe the definition of what I call ASI.

计算效率的真正含义：物理层面的底层基质

So really I think we need to get to a much greater amount of computer efficiency. And when I say computer efficiency, I don't mean algorithmic computer efficiency or data efficiency. There's lots of people working on these problems. I actually mean the fundamental substrate — actually how I do information processing at the physics level.

We chose a path of making computers work the way they did for a lot of reasons about 80 years ago. If you think about it, like in the tech industry, how many things have existed for 80 years? Like not a lot. The digital abstraction, sort of floating point numbers — those were around from the 1940s. And for a machine that was built from a completely different substrate, and for a machine that was built for a completely different purpose. And now we're building machines for intelligence.

能源墙：未来 2-4 年的真实约束

So let's think through this a little bit. So part of this is that AI will make us more efficient. I mean, we've talked about coding and, you know, running a thousand agents on your phone, all this kind of stuff, right? It does make us more efficient. But at some point we start to get to a place where what does efficiency really mean? If you think about energy, actual energy, maybe we're actually not more efficient. And we're kind of butting up against those limitations of the physical world now.

You know, already today we're using many gigawatts for AI inference and training. And we're going to get to a point within the next couple of years — this is not 10 years away, this is two, three, four years — where we just don't have any more energy in the world for AI. Then this topic starts to become very, very important.

Right now you can kind of look at it like, well, electrical energy versus food — these are two energy sources for intelligence. And, you know, there's no real limitation on the electrical side, but that's going to hit a pretty solid wall very soon. And, you know, we're talking about going to space, we're talking about building fusion reactors. Great. Let's do all those things, but still these fundamental physics apply.

用数字算账：人类全脑 160 GW vs 全球 9000 GW

So if we think through this a little bit, we have about 8 billion people in the world. Our brains use about 20 watts each. That's only 160 gigawatts. So the entirety of humanity is 160 gigawatts.

So just put that into comparison: we have about 9,000 gigawatts of capacity in the world today. The US has about a thousand. And, you know, this runs everything. This is like heating in your home and, you know, all the things — electric cars, all that kind of stuff. But if we said maybe you got 50% more of this and we say, okay, great, now we got like, you know, 4,000-plus gigawatts.

But the problem is our current paradigm of compute is just vastly more inefficient. So a computer — I'm making some numbers up here, I mean, I can say like if I'm running inference per token, I can come up with numbers, but, you know, fully loaded, like the amount of energy that goes into inference, building the model, running the model, all of that — let's call it a gigawatt, something like that, or, you know, definitely in the megawatt range. But humans are on the order of 20 watts.

And you could also argue that evolution over 4 billion years has created what we are. But the reality is today our constraint becomes: how quickly can we get learnings to happen? How quickly can we build intelligence on a given amount of energy?

So if we want to build this future where we have lots of intelligence in the world and we're automating all kinds of things — that we want to really be more efficient from an energy standpoint — we're going to need a lot more watts. Or we can think about building a vastly more power-efficient computer. And that's where we come in.

热力学极限与 Landauer 原理

So I love this curve here, and I think most people really haven't really thought about this, cuz you just sort of assume the computer is a computer. And we haven't really questioned that. Again, this is the unconventional part — it's like, let's break that apart. The assumptions we made 80 years ago are actually not quite valid anymore. We just choose to keep building on them because I can build a product in two years. I can make something that I can sell in two years. But we're kind of taking a different tack: like, let's go back to those first principles and see if we can build something much, much better.

So there is a thermodynamic limit to intelligence per watt. Okay? That means you just can't do any better. There's something called the Landauer principle, which some of you may know, which basically suggests how much compute could happen within a certain amount of energy. So there is a physical reality that we can't get past, and that's sort of this asymptote here.

Now biology is somewhere up here. It's actually pretty darn efficient. 4 billion years of evolution have created something that is actually very efficient. However, it's not at the asymptote yet. There's probably an order of two of magnitude between those two. We're actually here, by the way. We're down here. And I think limits of 2D lithography — that's what chips are built on today — is, call it, somewhere down here.

And I think with focused effort we can get to the point where we're pushing the limit of that. And so we'll have something that's — this, by the way, is something like three orders of magnitude from where we are. It is very far away from where we could be in terms of energy efficiency. And so really that's what we're focused on today.

不能再把"计算机"当作矩阵运算机器

So how do we do it? I mean, yeah, this is great. Make something more power-efficient and wonderful, right? But the reality is — it's not so simple. We can't be thinking about the computer in exactly the same way as we have been. It's not about a machine that runs off of matrix math. That's been the simple way to move forward.

Nvidia, of course, has owned that market and continues to push the envelope. But if you look at the power efficiency numbers — actual power efficiency of delivering an FP8 flop, for instance — it's not that much better. Costs have gotten better cuz manufacturing's gotten better, our ability to package has gotten better, but actual energy per flop with memory access has not gotten better. It's very, very incremental now.

从神经科学借灵感：生物的存在性证明

So I am a neuroscientist. I was a computer architect for 10 years before that. So I've been thinking about this problem for a long time, actually on the order of 30 years. So it's a very exciting time for me personally.

And, you know, biology really does provide an existence proof. I mean, you can argue that, okay, the tokens-per-second out of a human are lower than a machine, but the intelligence is higher. We still haven't gotten to the point with these gigawatts that we're throwing at it that we're rivaling a human's intelligence in terms of discovery. We'll get there. We're going to get there in a very short amount of time. But it's going to come at the cost of a lot of energy.

So what I think is most interesting here is actually not just that human brains are 20 watts, but that the watt kind of scales with the weight. A macaque monkey's brain is probably less than a watt. And actually you see this all through the mammalian world, and I've also — the insect world. Like, you have very complex behavior for milliwatts.

Just for reference, your phone in your pocket is about one watt. So, you know, a squirrel jumping from branch to branch is running on less than 10 milliwatts. That's 1/100 of your phone. We can't actually do this perfectly. Like, a squirrel's jumping 10 feet across from branch to branch within wind and all that stuff. We can't do that with a much, much larger computer. So biology still created something quite amazing. And I just don't feel like there's been an appreciation for that. So just a little reminder there.

大脑不是矩阵运算：非线性动力学与随机性

So now great. We see this kind of phenomenologically — like biology is efficient, can do amazing things, but how does it actually work? We don't really know. I will be honest, as a computer scientist and a neuroscientist. But there are some ideas that we can harvest from neuroscience.

And one of them is that the brain is dynamic. It does not use matrix math to do compute. It uses what's called non-linear dynamics to do compute. What this means is that there's a time-varying interaction between neurons, and that's actually where the compute lies. So can we extract that and actually apply it to synthetic circuits? Maybe.

They don't do floating point math. They don't do matrix math. They do something that can be characterized as such, but it's actually much richer than that because of these non-linear dynamics. And they're stochastic. Brain's compute is not a strict one and zero. In a digital computer, if we're off by a one or zero, the whole system falls apart. So they're really not computers.

Kuramoto 同步：耦合振子作为计算介质

So I'm going to try to go through this quickly. This is a thing called a Kuramoto synchronization. So if you look at a bunch of oscillators here, and they're kind of rigidly coupled to each other on this plank, you'll see over time that they start off in any state and then they actually synchronize. This is an example of a contracting or converging dynamical system. So no matter how you start it, it converges, and it's only based upon the coupling between them.

We can generalize this to something that has kind of a flexible coupling — call it a trainable coupling — between those things. And then it can have all kinds of interesting dynamics. It can move through the state space of dynamics in many, many different ways.

So if you generalize this, you can actually think about an electrified electronic circuit. You can say: I have a bunch of oscillators and they have a fabric on which they're coupled. And now when I make this fabric trainable, I can actually see something that starts to look a little bit more like the dynamics of the brain. It actually has nonlinearities, and they interact with each other in very interesting ways. It's actually very rich — represent a lot of information.

6 个月做出原型：AI 加速 AI 硬件设计

This is actual chip that we're going to be building this summer. So we went from basically no team in January to a full prototype in 6 months, and that's because of AI. So this is what's really cool about not having baggage — is you can do things in completely different ways.

用物理本身做计算：时间作为计算介质

And the way you compute with something like this — the traditional way would be basically loop over some sort of linearized time. This is how we do things in a von Neumann machine. We write state out, we retrieve it, we operate on it, we write it back. So we keep going back and forth. Turns out that's what burns most of the energy in an existing computing system.

With something with nonlinear dynamics, I actually just say, "Here's the initial state, kick it and let it run." So the physics themselves basically do this computation, and it does it sort of — the state is implicit. It's not an explicit write.

So in some ways you can think about it: if you take anything from this talk, that we use the time axis of the physics to do computing, and existing computing constructs do not.

训练动力系统：图像生成 demo

And so the question then is, "Can I train this?" And the answer is yes. I can actually steer the system into multiple different things. In fact, we've sort of traced out in state space an Unconventional logo. That's the idea here, and we can train it in a few different ways. So yes, we can train these systems and steer them into basically any arbitrary set of trajectories.

And can we connect it to AI problems like image generation? So actually I have a better version of this I can go to in just a really quick demo here. Let's go to the next one. We'll go to the demo.

Yeah. So basically what you see here is something that's running on a model of dynamics that was trained on these different images. So basically I can say, "Okay, I have to use cats." I think Andrew Ng is here, so —

[laughter]

homage to him, but we can do anything. But basically this is a pretty simple generative model. And I can basically say like, "Okay, at time t equals 1, I'm going to backprop an error from randomness to a particular image class." And after that point, we let the system just run naturally.

And what you'll find is that it actually has clumped its representation into places that are meaningful. They're no longer just random pixels, but they're actually pixels that make different kind of machines or different kind of animals or whatever. So for horses, I start off as random, and at t equals 1, you should see it kind of converge into horse-like things. And then over time you'll actually see it sort of morph between those. So it's already learned in the state space that I can move between these different things.

计算范式的演进：CPU → GPU → 内存内计算 → 动力系统

So let's go ahead and move out of this. So this is really the emergence of something new.

So CPUs actually do very fast single-threaded things the best. Even today, it's faster than a GPU. And really what you're doing is this kind of von Neumann machine where you're moving in and out of memory and cache and doing operations.

GPUs basically do this with multiple operands at once. So you move a bunch of operands from memory, do some stuff to it, write it back.

Compute-in-memory like Groq sort of did the same thing, but just did it on chip. It's kind of a more fine-grained version of this.

Now, what we're talking about is doing something in a dynamical system. The state and the function are overlapped with the physics themselves. So you now have no separation between state and computation. And, you know, compute efficiency goes up — of course, galaxy braininess goes up. And this is truly non-von Neumann.

收尾

So with that, I'm just going to leave you with this quote. It's been something I've guided my entire life by, and I'm really excited about this time, because I've been thinking about this problem for 30 years. And we're at this point where I think we can actually start to understand how brains work, cuz now we can build them.

Thank you.

[applause]

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：这不是芯片公司，这是"时间维度"的发现
大多数人听到 Naveen Rao 在做新型 AI 芯片，第一反应是"又一个挑战 Nvidia 的玩家"。但仔细听他的核心论点，会发现一件极其颠覆的事——**他真正在做的，是把"时间"作为一种被遗忘的计算资源重新发明**。
冯·诺依曼架构的本质悖论是：它把"计算"和"存储"在空间上分开，然后用时钟节拍把时间切成离散的小片段，逼物理世界去模拟一个无时间的数学抽象。每一次读写都在把"时间"当作摩擦阻力来克服，而不是当作计算介质来使用。Naveen 的非线性动力系统恰恰相反：**让物理本身的演化就是计算过程**。这就像我们用了 80 年时间训练自己"无视时间维度"，现在突然发现，时间从来不是 bug，是 feature。
这与你的"系统性"思维方式有一个隐藏共振：你也常常在分离"思考"和"行动"，把决策切成离散步骤——而生命体（包括你自己的直觉系统）实际上是用连续的动力学在做决定。
## 二、跨领域连接：神经科学 × 热力学 × 风投经济学的三重叠合
注意 Naveen 抛出的那个数字——**全人类 80 亿人的大脑

item.title: [Sequoia Capital] Starcloud's Philip Johnston: Why the Cheapest Compute Will Be in Space
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Capital
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

当能量经济学撞上重力井：一次思维框架的彻底翻转

Philip 真正卖的不是"太空数据中心"，而是一次成本结构的拓扑变换。地球上太阳能项目的三大成本——许可土地、储能电池、电池板本身——在太空里前两项直接归零，第三项除以八。这不是渐进式优化，而是把一个三元方程变成一元方程：剩下的唯一变量是发射成本，而 SpaceX 正在以工程化的确定性把它压向 \$10/kg。

让人意外的是：真正的护城河不是火箭，而是"许可土地"在地球上的稀缺性正在加速恶化。AI 数据中心的瓶颈早已从芯片转向电网接入和环评审批，北弗吉尼亚的电力排队已经排到 2030 年代。换句话说，是地球的官僚熵增在把计算推向轨道——不是马斯克的火箭把它拉上去的。

一个被所有人忽略的反直觉细节：真空让散热"更难"，但 Stefan-Boltzmann 公式给了一个荒谬的杠杆

直觉告诉我们太空"很冷"，所以散热应该容易。Philip 揭示了相反的真相：真空里没有对流，只能靠红外辐射散热，所以散热器面积要做得很大。但精彩的地方在 Stefan-Boltzmann 定律——辐射功率与温度的四次方成正比。

这意味着：把芯片运行温度从 50°C 提到 80°C（开尔文尺度只升了约 10%），辐射效率却几乎翻倍，散热器质量直接减半。

这是一个被完全忽视的跨领域连接：地面 GPU 的整个工程范式（追求低温运行）在太空里是反向最优的。Nvidia 与 Star Cloud 联合开发的"Space Ruben"芯片，本质上是在重新定义"什么叫好的芯片"——更热、更脏、更糙的芯片在轨道上反而更经济。这与生物学中的"嗜热菌"形成了奇妙的呼应：极端环境会催生与温和环境完全不同的最优形态。

隐藏的系统性洞见：他在用"推理 vs 训练"的不对称做风险对冲

Philip 说"我们只做推理"，看似是技术谦虚——"训练需要 5GW 的对接结构，15 年后再说"。但深层逻辑是一次精妙的市场结构博弈：

  • 训练市场是赢家通吃（少数前沿实验室主导），客户集中度高，议价能力强；
  • 推理市场是长尾分布（每个使用 AI 的应用都是客户），且占未来计算量的 99%。

这意味着 Star Cloud 选择推理不是技术妥协，而是故意避开 NVIDIA + 超大规模云厂商的护城河方向，去切一个还未形成寡头结构的市场。更隐秘的是：推理对地理分布敏感（要 sub-50ms 延迟），而 88,000 颗卫星的 dawn-dusk 同步轨道天然具备全球均匀覆盖的属性——这是地面数据中心永远无法复制的拓扑优势。

跨领域连接：这不是基础设施项目，是一次"逃逸物种"的进化事件

Philip 在最后提到 Kardashev II/III 不是营销话术。真正值得注意的是一个生物学类比：当一个物种把代谢系统外置到另一种能量梯度（更稳定、更密集）的环境中时，往往伴随着主要的进化跃迁——比如真核细胞通过线粒体把能量代谢"外包"给了被吞噬的细菌。

人类正在做同样的事：把认知代谢（计算）从地球这颗"勉强宜居的能量稀缺星球"剥离，挂载到太阳的直接辐射上。一旦这个剥离完成，地球上的"计算-能源-土地"三角约束被解开，AI 的增长曲线将从指数函数切换到一个新的、被太阳总辐射量约束的极限——也就是 Dyson 球的雏形。

最让我意外的预测：未来 20 年地球上"数据中心荒漠"现象会出现。当太空算力的边际成本低于地面，地面数据中心会像 2020 年代的燃煤电厂一样进入加速退役周期。许可证、土地、电网接入这些今天的"资产"，可能变成沉没成本。

📝 转录文本

开场介绍

Thanks so much for having me. Um, my name is Philip Johnston, and I'm the co-founder and CEO of Star Cloud. And just like the previous company, we have also been abusing GPUs in ways they were not designed for. [laughter]

Um, so yeah, we're building data centers in space, um, mainly for the energy that we can draw. And I will spend the next 5 minutes um explaining why it will soon make much more sense to build data centers in space than it does to build them on Earth. And then I'll take 5 minutes for questions, so please um start thinking of some questions.

Star Cloud 1 部署演示

Before I do that though, I want to show a quick video, which is actually the deployment of Star Cloud 1. Um, and this was — it had five Nvidia GPUs on it, but the most significant was the Nvidia H100 chip. And uh, I'll just quickly play the video first.

"Star Cloud 1 separation confirmed."

So, you don't normally get as great a deployment video, by the way. Half the time they'd like deploy it into the shadow. [laughter]

Um, so the reason this was so significant is — until this point, many people thought you actually couldn't run state-of-the-art terrestrial data center grade GPUs in space, for two main reasons:

  1. Thermal dissipation — they're very power dense, they produce a lot of heat.
  2. Radiation tolerance — people thought that um you would have bit flips at too high a rate.

And so um with this chip, we were the first to train a model in space. We actually trained nano GPT from Andrej Karpathy. Um, and then we also uh were the first to run a version of Gemini, the first to do high-powered inference on uh SAR data — so other satellite data. Um, and so it was a very significant step in proving that we can actually run the state of the art terrestrially.

为什么太空比地球更便宜：能源经济学

Um, but the um — yeah, I think maybe to make the case for why it will soon make more sense in terms of energy cost, I'd like to quickly draw a comparison with uh with a solar project on Earth, since solar is the cheapest form of energy that we have on Earth.

So if you want to build a solar project to power a new data center, you have three main costs:

  1. Cost of permitted land — in fact, in North America that's actually the largest cost, or can be, for most new solar projects.
  2. Cost of battery storage and backup power — because we only have peak power for about 4 hours of the day, so we need to charge those batteries to use at night.
  3. Cost of the solar cells themselves.

So how does that compare to building a similarly sized solar project in space? Well, in space:

  • Number one, we don't need to pay for permitted land. So your biggest cost is gone.
  • You don't need to pay for battery storage and backup power, because we're 24/7 in the sun. So your second biggest cost is gone.
  • And then you need eight times less solar cells, because 1 square meter of solar panel in space produces eight times the energy of 1 square meter of solar panel on Earth.

So the only additional cost — or the main additional cost — we have in space is the launch cost. [snorts]

And so you can clearly see there's a break-even point where the launch cost comes below the cost of permitted land, batteries, and solar. And we see that break-even cost to be around \$500 a kilo. So about a 10x reduction from where we are today. But that's well within range of the launch vehicles that are coming online — for comparison, Starship is designed to produce launch cost of around \$10 to \$20 a kilo.

88,000 卫星星座：基础设施级别的雄心

Um, and so I think I'll just finish by playing you a uh one final concept video, and this shows uh a constellation that we're building now. So we've just filed with the SEC for a constellation of 88,000 satellites. Each one's about 200 kilowatts. It will enable us to deploy on the order of 20 gigawatts of new compute capacity — really just scratching the surface um with this new constellation.

Um, and it will enable — it's basically for all inference workloads. And so this could be — and yeah, maybe I'll start the video and you can get a sense of it. So in this case it's to generate a 3D video, but it could also be for back office business processing agents, code generation agents.

Um, they will come up via optical link to this constellation in this dawn-dusk sun-synchronous orbit — means it's always in the sun. 24/7 power. Sub 50 millisecond latency to anywhere on Earth. Um, all optically linked. [snorts]

And this really is the start of the largest infrastructure project ever. I mean, we're talking about — just for this constellation of 88,000 — we're talking about a hundred billion dollars of capex spend, which is actually much lower than it would cost to do terrestrially. Um, and uh not only is it the start of the largest infrastructure project, it's also in my opinion the start of a Kardashev type II, uh Dyson sphere type civilization, and potentially Kardashev type III.

Um, I will finish there, and we have about 4 minutes for questions. So any questions? Yeah, we'll start at the front.

Q&A 一：散热与轨道可用性

Q: Uh, the intuition on the availability — solar — is obvious. Um, can you just give us the napkin math on the radiator equation again? So like dissipating heat for anyone who's thought about it, it always feels hard. And then, and also please say something about the availability of dawn-dusk. That orbit is finite, right?

A: Yes, yeah. Yeah. Um, it's a great question. So because space is a vacuum, it's actually much harder — you know, space is only 3° Kelvin, so very low ambient temperature. But because it's a vacuum, um, as you rightly point out, it's actually quite difficult to dissipate that heat. And what it requires is a large surface area so that you can emit that in infrared. So everything that's warm is glowing in infrared all the time. If you had an infrared camera on my face, you'd see that I'm glowing.

Um, and so the rough math on the surface area is: your solar panels generate around 200 watts per square meter, and the radiator — if you keep it around 50°C — will dissipate around 800 watts per square meter. So what that means is, if you've got a — you need about a quarter again the surface area in radiator than you have on um on solar panel. So if you had a 400 square meter solar panel, you'd need an additional 100 square meters of radiator to dissipate that heat.

Um, there's a very nice equation called the Stefan-Boltzmann equation, which basically says that the rate of thermal dissipation is proportional to the fourth power of the temperature. So if you can jack up that temperature instead of being 50° to 80°, which is like a 10% increase in Kelvin, then you can actually halve the surface area of your radiator — or close to half the surface area of radiator.

And so that's what we're working on with Nvidia now. If anybody was at GTC, you'll have seen Jensen walk out to this um to the deployment video of Star Cloud 1, and then he spent uh 5 minutes talking about the um the new Space Ruben 1 chip that we're working on. And it's designed to run at a hotter temperature without having a higher failure rate. And the reason you want it to run at a hotter temperature is so that you can lower the mass on the radiator.

Great question. Then, yeah.

Q&A 二：Kessler 综合症

Q: Yeah. All right. Kessler syndrome — the favorite thing, right? Now we're going to get more satellites there, yet more in uh Kessler orbits, 'cause everyone will want — what happens then?

A: Yeah, it's a great question, and it's also related to the question um asked about the space in space.

Um, I think — so it's something we take incredibly seriously. You know, everybody needs to be a responsible user of space. We do and everybody else. We're, you know, keen to make sure that space is usable forever.

Um, for the first few satellites — so you can solve it in a few ways. If you fly at a relatively low altitude, the chance of a Kessler-type effect is extremely low. So um, our first satellite we're flying around 400 km altitude. That means that it will naturally deorbit within a few months. And so if you were to have a collision at that altitude, by the time it gets around to the next orbit, you're already a few hundred meters below where you had the collision, and the chance — yeah — chance of um Kessler is very, very low.

Um, yeah, as you fly higher, it's actually extremely unpopulated, those high orbits, because then you start to edge into the Van Allen radiation belt. Um, but I mean, we actually have a pretty good case study for this, and that is um SpaceX is now operating around 10,000 satellites without ever having a single collision um in low Earth orbit. And the way that you do that is by having pretty sophisticated collision avoidance.

Um, the other reason I think people think this is more of an issue than it actually is — and the reason that space is so much larger than it looks — is when you see a map of all of those satellites, each dot on those maps is about the width of California, and you're representing something that might be this wide by something the width of California. And so people can often think that space is very congested. It actually — you — we can easily fit on the order of uh terawatts of compute in just this dawn-dusk sun-synchronous orbit without having um, you know, huge problems with collision avoidance.

Any other questions? Yeah.

Q&A 三：辐射与位翻转

Q: Uh, is radiation, like bit flipping, is that something you actually have to think about or consider? How does that impact stuff?

A: Yes, it is something I have to think about. Um, so the way that we're solving it is just an enormous amount of ground testing. So we've done uh four rounds of testing at the cyclotron down in Knoxville — um, it's a high velocity proton particle accelerator. Um, and we take all of that telemetry, and then that informs our choice on shielding.

And then for heavy ions we have to go to the Brookhaven National Lab, and we basically run all the chips through um through the space environment. So over a 24 hour period, you can put it through 5 years' worth of radiation dose, um, and then we take all that data, and we then use that to inform shielding but also software development choices for it.

Yeah. Yeah.

Q&A 四：训练 vs 推理

Q: Do you do contiguous compute or are you doing something else?

A: Uh, this is almost exclusively — I mean actually for the foreseeable future will just be for inference.

Um, and the reason it's for inference is, number one, inference is going to be like 99% of the compute market very soon anyway. So even if we — you know, we wouldn't want to be running a large training set — um, well, running large training sets will be a very small percentage of the total in 5 to 10 years' time of AI workloads.

But secondly, it's very hard. We would need a — yeah, we need to dock together a large 5 gigawatt um kind of structure. I actually have a video of that here, but I won't waste everyone's time with it. [laughter] Unless [snorts] somebody wants to see a video of a 5 gigawatt data center in space. Do you guys want to see that? All right. [laughter]

We made this video 'cause we didn't want people to be like, "Oh, you could never train a model in space." So, this is what a 5 gigawatt, 4 km by 4 km structure in space would look like. So this would be a Starship launch vehicle — with a 40 megawatts is what you can fit per Starship launch vehicle — which will connect to a central spine, which is connected to this enormous solar panel. On the back there, we have a 1 km by 4 km radiator.

Yeah, that's how you would train a large model. But as I say, it'll probably be at least 15 years before we get to anything like that.

收尾投票

One more, I think we're — okay, 40 seconds. Or do we — okay. Oh, sorry.

Q: Um, by when do you think the majority of the data center will be in space?

A: Oh, that's a great question. And I actually wanted to ask all of you. Let's run a poll. So the question I want to ask is: when do you think it will be cheaper to run compute in space for anybody? It could be for SpaceX or for us. And the four answers will be:

  • The next 5 years it will be cheaper
  • In 5 to 10 years it will be cheaper
  • Sometime after 10 years
  • Never

Okay, so who thinks within 5 years it will be cheaper to run compute in space than terrestrially? Interesting. Who thinks 5 to 10 years? Who thinks beyond 10 years? And who thinks never? Brave.

Okay, that's interesting for me to see. Um, I don't — yeah, I think that's — we are out of time, so I will leave it there. Thank you very much for your time. [applause]

观看原视频 →

## 当能量经济学撞上重力井：一次思维框架的彻底翻转
Philip 真正卖的不是"太空数据中心"，而是一次**成本结构的拓扑变换**。地球上太阳能项目的三大成本——许可土地、储能电池、电池板本身——在太空里**前两项直接归零，第三项除以八**。这不是渐进式优化，而是把一个三元方程变成一元方程：剩下的唯一变量是发射成本，而 SpaceX 正在以工程化的确定性把它压向 \$10/kg。
让人意外的是：**真正的护城河不是火箭，而是"许可土地"在地球上的稀缺性正在加速恶化**。AI 数据中心的瓶颈早已从芯片转向电网接入和环评审批，北弗吉尼亚的电力排队已经排到 2030 年代。换句话说，是地球的官僚熵增在把计算推向轨道——不是马斯克的火箭把它拉上去的。
## 一个被所有人忽略的反直觉细节：真空让散热"更难"，但 Stefan-Boltzmann 公式给了一个荒谬的杠杆
直觉告诉我们太空"很冷"，所以散热应该容易。Philip 揭示了相反的真相：真空里没有对流，**只能靠红外辐射散热**，所以散热器面积要做得很大。但精彩的地方在 Stefan-Boltzmann 定律——辐射功率与温度的

item.title: [Sequoia Capital] Why Data Is the Real AI Bottleneck: Flapping Airplanes' Ben and Asher Spector
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Capital
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、藏在"飞机隐喻"里的认知错位

这场演讲最值得玩味的，不是它讲了什么，而是它为什么会被反复误解为航空公司。Flapping Airplanes 收到的"想卖跑道"的邮件，本身就是一个绝佳的隐喻：当今的 AI 产业心智完全被"硬件—基础设施"叙事占据，以至于一家做"数据效率"的实验室，名字里只要带飞机，就被自动归入物理基础设施的篮子。

这是一个出人意料的观察——整个市场在潜意识里把 AI 当成航空业：跑道（数据中心）、机队（GPU 集群）、燃料（电力）、航线（API 调用）。Flappy 的真正颠覆点，恰恰是质疑这套"重资产飞行"范式：他们想造的不是更大的飞机，而是会扑翼的鸟——用千分之一的数据飞起来。名字本身就是论点。

二、"数据中心化"是被忽略的政治变量

公开讨论里大家都在谈 compute centralization（OpenAI、Anthropic、xAI 的算力军备），但 Asher 顺嘴提到的那个细节才是真正的炸药：前沿实验室在收购倒闭书店、扫荡稀有图书馆来榨取最后的数据残渣。

这意味着 AI 的护城河早已悄悄从"算力垄断"滑向"语料殖民"。compute 是同质化商品（你买不到就再印钞），data 却是异质、地理性、法律性的——它和石油地缘政治惊人相似：储量分布不均、谁先签长期合约谁锁定下游、还有"数据 OPEC"的雏形（Reddit/Stack Overflow/纽约时报的授权战争）。

跨领域连接：数据效率之于 AI，约等于内燃机效率之于 19 世纪末的能源格局。当蒸汽机的热效率从 3% 提升到 30%，不只是省煤，而是让没有煤矿的国家也能工业化。Flappy 押注的不是技术参数，而是一次去殖民化运动。

三、"虐待 GPU"与神经科学的隐秘合谋

Ben 说他们在 "abuse the crap out of GPUs"，绕开 PyTorch 的单线程抽象，直接接管整张卡跑 hogwild 风格的深度流水线小批训练。表面看这是底层工程炫技，但请注意他们的第三位联合创始人 Aiden——一个在大学和实验室之间"super commute 三年、既懂大脑又懂 ML"的人。

这个组合不是巧合。人脑恰恰是异步、稀疏、细粒度、无全局同步屏障的"hogwild 机器"。当今 PyTorch 范式的"批量同步并行"其实是一种为了便于人类编程而强加给硅片的伪秩序——它让 GPU 假装自己是一个巨型 CPU。Flappy 真正在做的，是把 GPU 从"被驯化的农场动物"还原成"野生神经基质"。

更出人意料的连接：这与城市规划中的 Jane Jacobs vs. Robert Moses 之争结构同构。PyTorch 是 Moses 式的网格化高速公路——高效、可预测、便于治理；Flappy 想要的是 Jacobs 式的有机街区——细粒度、混乱、但涌现出真正的多样性与适应力。数据效率的终极秘密，可能不在算法里，而在于愿不愿意放弃"整齐"这种执念。

一个留给你的预测：如果他们的路线对了，未来 5 年最稀缺的人才不会是 ML 研究员，而是能读懂 SASS 汇编、又能引用《On Intelligence》的人——这个交集目前在地球上不超过 500 人。

📝 转录文本

开场介绍

Bringing up two frontier spotlights, the first is Ben and Asher, two brilliant brothers. Yes, they are — come on up. Uh, founders of swimming submarines. Wait, no. Wait a second — flapping airplanes. Um, and we're excited to hear about data efficiency. Thanks so much. Thanks, Konstantin.

[applause]

All right, yeah. It's uh really great to be here. Thanks for having us. Uh, we're very excited to tell you about why the future is data efficient. Um, so let's get into it.

创始人背景

So, uh first I think introductions are in order. Uh, my background: I spent 3 years in the PhD kind of deep in the kernel mines, uh writing very low-level GPU systems uh before helping to start Flappy. And uh, I I also should say I I at one point helped start an incubator called Prod that worked with a bunch of companies that did well. So, that's also part of my background.

Cool. And so, I'm Asher. Um, if you can't tell, I'm Ben's older, um wiser, and slightly less handsome brother. Um, I previously was also a Stanford PhD, spent time at Cursor, Mercor.

And then lastly, our third co-founder is Aiden Smith. Uh, he's a Thiel fellow. He's busy working right now. Uh, but he's spent uh 3 years super-commuting between his college and our lab. So, he knows a lot both about the brain and also about machine learning.

关于公司名字的澄清

Um, so before we get started in earnest, I do want to clarify some confusion. We launched the company 3 months ago. Um, we are very excited to have received a lot of inbound, but a surprising amount of it has been from the aviation industry. Um, you know, people trying to sell us things like runways — like not not runway in the venture capital sense, like literal runways. Uh, you know, airplane parts, wind tunnels, etc.

So, I'd like to disclaim once and for all: uh, we are not an airplane, we're an AI lab — and hopefully that will become clear by the end of this talk.

演讲提纲

Okay, great. Um, so first a quick outline. In this talk, we're going to tell you about two things. First of all, our thesis of why this is a really important problem. And second, we'll tell you a bit about our approach that fuses both systems work and algorithmic work.

论点一：当前 LLM 擅长的，是数据极度丰富的任务

So, uh first on the thesis side. So, if we look at the current state of the world, LLMs have gotten incredibly good at a couple of really valuable tasks. So, for example, search, coding. Together, these are, you know, at least a trillion-dollar market that's probably accessible here.

A big part of the reason why they've gotten so good at these tasks — and this is related to what uh Andre I think talked about this morning — is that these are incredibly well-resourced tasks in terms of the amount of data that's available for them. For search, this is basically the entire internet. For coding, this is a big fraction of the internet. Coding is also a very friendly environment in that it's very easy to produce mountains of synthetic data if you want to.

核心问题：能否用更少的数据获得同等能力？

Um, so when we talk about data efficiency, what we're asking the question of is: is it possible to get these kinds of capabilities with much less data?

Intuitively, it seems like it should be possible. Uh, you know, humans are able to become quite good at coding uh with, you know, maybe 10,000 times or 100,000 times less data on this than these current models take. So, this is kind of the core question.

为什么这件事重要：经济的"长尾"

And a reason why this matters is that if we look towards the future, there's a bunch of other domains of the economy where this — uh where there's there's much less data. So, for example, you know, Jim talked this morning about how in robotics, there's a ton of work that has to go on to try to generate the data. It's much more complicated than it is uh in in coding and search. In trading, there's only so much financial data. Scientific discoveries, obviously very little data. Uh, but the the uh the potential there is sort of unbounded.

And most important of all is, of course, the end-to-end toaster supply chain. Now, the reason I bring this up is not because of what it is, it's because of what it represents — namely that there are tens of thousands of things like this that really make up the broad economy. And the broad economy doesn't just look like search and coding, it looks like a lot of things that are actually really not well-resourced.

经济论点：算力比数据更容易扩张

Yeah, and so Ben's given you one economic reason to believe that the future is data efficient. I think a second claim is that compute is actually easier to scale than data. I mean, we know that flops get exponentially cheaper over time. And you know, I think it's probably true that data is not getting cheap as quickly as compute is. Although, it is getting cheaper.

Um, I think, you know, the second reason is that collecting frontier quality is complicated. The compute market is homogeneous in a — or is more homogeneous than the the data market is. Like, you know, Greg was telling us — after, you know, GPT launched, they could just try to buy all the compute. And there's no centralized data provider. Like, if you want to go into the economy and collect the long tail of tasks, you have to like deal with regulations, you have to negotiate with businesses about terms of use. It's actually very annoying.

So, I think if you could — as a result, if you can make a model that's a thousand times more data efficient, I think it'd be a thousand times easier to deploy.

哲学论点：数据效率决定 AI 革命的参与权

So, those are two sort of economic reasons that we think data efficiency is important. The last reason is actually kind of philosophical.

I think that, you know, if you look at the world today, um there's not that many companies that can train AI models. And — and you know, part of this is because of uh of centralization of compute. But I think it's also because of centralization of data. Like, you know, I've heard news about Neo Labs who, when trying to create new capabilities, they like literally buy out distressed bookstores to find all the the data they can find. They like go to like rare libraries to find all the tiny niches of data that you need to actually make a frontier model.

And I think um in a world that's data efficient, I think that companies can actually more broadly participate in the AI revolution. Like, you know, we heard earlier today that uh you know, in in the poll about what the moat is in the AI world, the most common answer was data. If that's true, then data efficiency is the thing that actually enables broader competition. So, I think if you care about the shape of the world to come, I think you really should care about data efficiency, because it modulates who can actually participate in which parts of the AI economy.

方法论：从硬件原语中寻找新算法

Cool. So, now we're going to talk about our approach. Um, so our goal is to design data efficient AI. Um, we design algorithms for this. We're not going to talk about them cuz they're our core IP. But I will tell you about an important facet of our approach, which is how we try to look in new places. Like, if you want new capabilities, where can you find them?

So, our claim is that essentially, if you want to develop new algorithms, you should look at new primitives for interacting with hardware. There's some set of things that GPUs can do efficiently. And then there's a smaller set of things that current frameworks like PyTorch, for example, can actually express efficiently. And, you know, that that gray circle, we've seen a lot of research into that circle. We actually know what kinds of algorithms work. But if you're looking for new capabilities, you might want to look in these new places.

And that's where we live at Flappy Airplanes. That's what we try to do. And you know, Ben will talk for a little bit later, for example, about things like fine-grainedness, which actually are hard to do under current frameworks, but a GPU actually can do.

Um, you know, history suggests that this is a good bet, for what it's worth. I mean, a lot of the development of machine learning over the past 15, 20, even 100 years has actually been new primitives for interacting with hardware. Not necessarily designing new chips, which is also important, but merely figuring out how to squeeze more out of the existing technology that has proliferated.

深入底层：滥用 GPU 的乐趣

Yeah, so I guess a bit of context. Um, in my PhD, I did some of this work on on early work on mega kernels, uh which was trying to make GPUs do weird stuff. Um, at Flappy, I would say that we are going further in spiritually similar directions, uh namely trying to kind of abuse the crap out of GPUs in ways that haven't been done before.

Um, this has been quite a lot of fun. So, I actually would recommend it if you if you like playing with hardware. And I guess part of what I'm excited about in this uh talk is that I, you know, this is in general I think a fairly high-level venue, but I actually want to get into the weeds of some stuff because I think it's fun.

PyTorch 抽象的代价

Um, so if we look at how kind of current systems work — and this will be a little bit abstract, but I hope that the concept will come across. What makes current machine learning frameworks easy to use is that they synthesize a single-threaded programming model out of very parallel processors.

So, basically, when you write PyTorch, you write matmul, and then attention, and then matmul, and then RMS norm. And under the hood, there's all kinds of contortions that happen where the software dispatches it in parallel across a very parallel set of processors. But nonetheless, to the programmer, this looks very natural.

But what happens if you want to run things that look like this? Or maybe you want to run things that look like this. All of a sudden, these kinds of things are not easily expressed in current frameworks.

Flappy 的内部框架

So, uh one thing I'm just very excited to show — sort of a little teaser of — is our internal framework that we use, which is built off a virtual machine uh that kind of just takes over the whole GPU, and we just do everything we want ourselves on it.

Uh, in this particular case, this is not a real workload I'm showing. This is kind of a stylized thing that is uh similar, but uh gives is an example of something that is also asymptotically inefficient to run in PyTorch. This is a very small batch kind of deeply pipelined hogwild-style training loop. Uh, this is the kind of thing that's very hard to do with with current frameworks.

So, uh the thing that I sort of like to get across here is that when you build these new kinds of systems, you enable new algorithms. Many of these algorithms we think are actually very relevant to the data efficiency problems. This is why we care about it. Um, and that it is the kind of co-optimization of those things that we find really interesting.

招募与寄语

Cool. So, um we're almost out of time. I I just really have one more thing to say, which is that if you find this exciting, please come talk to us. Um, you know, we we work with lots of folks who have trained big models before, but we're also trying to do creative new things. We're trying to change the paradigm. And you know, certainly our experience watching uh some of the the companies Ben has has helped incubate is that, you know, creative people with unconventional backgrounds can actually do amazing things and change the world. So, if you are of that background, please come chat with us.

总结

Okay, so I guess maybe a very quick recap, and then I think we probably have time for one question or so.

Uh, first of all: data efficiency. If you care about the shape of the world to come, if you care about the broad deployment of AI into the economy, you should care about making models much more data efficient. And that uh you know, our finding has been that building new systems to enable more fine-grained use of GPUs is actually very relevant to building these kinds of systems.

Um, and as Asher said, if you find this exciting, please come talk to us for a round. Thanks, everyone.

Thank you.

[applause]

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、藏在"飞机隐喻"里的认知错位
这场演讲最值得玩味的，不是它讲了什么，而是它**为什么会被反复误解为航空公司**。Flapping Airplanes 收到的"想卖跑道"的邮件，本身就是一个绝佳的隐喻：当今的 AI 产业心智完全被"硬件—基础设施"叙事占据，以至于一家做"数据效率"的实验室，名字里只要带飞机，就被自动归入物理基础设施的篮子。
这是一个出人意料的观察——**整个市场在潜意识里把 AI 当成航空业**：跑道（数据中心）、机队（GPU 集群）、燃料（电力）、航线（API 调用）。Flappy 的真正颠覆点，恰恰是质疑这套"重资产飞行"范式：他们想造的不是更大的飞机，而是**会扑翼的鸟**——用千分之一的数据飞起来。名字本身就是论点。
## 二、"数据中心化"是被忽略的政治变量
公开讨论里大家都在谈 compute centralization（OpenAI、Anthropic、xAI 的算力军备），但 Asher 顺嘴提到的那个细节才是真正的炸药：**前沿实验室在收购倒闭书店、扫荡稀有图书馆来榨取最后的数据残渣**。
这意味着 AI 的护城河早已悄悄从"算力

item.title: [Mercatus Center] What's So Beneficial About Portable Benefits?
item.dc:creator: Mercatus Center
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、表面叙事之下的真正悖论：自由的"税收惩罚"

这个视频表面在讲"便携式福利"，但隐藏的真相更尖锐——美国税法在事实上对"不被雇佣"征收了一种隐形税。雇员用税前美元购买健康保险，独立工作者用税后美元购买相同的保险，差价可能高达 22%-37%。换句话说，一个自由职业的设计师为了拥有"自由"，每年要多付几千美元的"自由溢价"。

这与中世纪欧洲的"行会制度"形成惊人的镜像：当时只有加入行会才能享受保护和市场准入，今天只有加入"雇佣关系"才能享受税收优惠。21 世纪的"零工经济"在结构上正在重演 13 世纪的"行会与流浪工匠"之分——只是工具从羊皮卷换成了 W-2 表格。

二、跨领域连接：这是一个"操作系统层级"的问题，不是"应用层"的问题

大多数人把便携式福利看作一个福利政策问题。但如果用计算机架构的视角看，它其实是一个API 解耦问题：

  • 当前系统：福利（数据）与雇主（运行时）紧耦合，更换雇主等于丢失状态。
  • 便携式福利：把福利从"雇主绑定的本地存储"迁移到"工作者绑定的云存储"。

这与十年前 SaaS 行业从"安装软件"到"订阅账户"的转型在结构上完全同构。美国劳动力市场正在经历一次迟到的"云迁移"——而阻力不是技术，是法律分类（"是不是雇员"这个布尔值）造成的"类型错误"。

更出人意料的连接：这与比特币钱包的"自托管 vs 交易所托管"辩论本质相同——谁拥有账户的根密钥？谁承担合规责任？谁在违约时兜底？劳动法此刻面对的，正是加密圈早已熟悉的"主权归属"问题。

三、被忽略的系统性盲点：80% 的偏好为何无法转化为政策？

视频里最值得追问的数字不是"81% 想要便携式福利"，而是为什么一个有如此压倒性民意支持的方案，仍要在州和联邦层面逐个州地艰难推动？

这暴露出一个深层的政治经济学规律——"分散受益、集中受损"的反向版本。便携式福利的受益者（独立工作者）是地理分散、组织松散、缺乏游说能力的群体；而潜在的反对者——传统工会、HR 服务商、保险经纪——却高度组织化。结果是：即便 81% 的人想要它，推动它的政治能量却小于反对它的 0.1%。

这与一个看似无关的现象惊人地相似：为什么城市里"通勤者"远多于"业主"，但城市规划仍偏向后者？答案相同——组织密度 ≠ 人口密度，而政策追逐的是前者。便携式福利的命运，本质上不是经济学问题，而是"未组织群体如何获得代表权"的古老政治问题。Mercatus 把它包装成监管改革，但它其实是 21 世纪的"无产者投票权"运动——只不过这次的"无产者"开着 Uber、写着代码、做着设计。

📝 转录文本

引言：便携式福利的好处是什么？

What's so beneficial about portable benefits?

一、传统雇员的福利结构

Many employers pay for part of their employees insurance, retirement, and even child care costs, and the IRS lets employees pay into those accounts with untaxed dollars. Employers also handle the setup, making the process simple.

二、独立工作者的困境

However, independent workers — like sole proprietors, freelancers, gig workers, or self-employed folks — don't have the same option. That's because under most state and federal regulations, if a client helps pay for those benefits, it can risk reclassifying an independent worker as an employee.

As a result, independent workers earn income from customers or clients, pay taxes first, and then set up and manage benefits on their own. This often means higher taxes and more paperwork, unless they use costly accountants or lawyers.

三、独立工作者的真实偏好

Independent workers love their freedom and ability to work for multiple clients and customers. Over 80% of independent workers prefer their current work, and fewer than 9% would like to be formal W-2 employees.

Yet, because most benefits are tied to one employer, some workers feel trapped in 9-to-5 jobs, even if they'd prefer to work independently. For those already working independently, juggling multiple benefits and paying more — often with after-tax dollars — is an unnecessary burden.

四、解决方案：便携式福利

What if there was a better way to let independent workers have the convenience of an employee benefits program while preserving the advantages of independent work? Portable benefits are that solution.

In fact, 81% of self-employed workers indicated that they would like portable benefits accounts that are not tied to a specific employer.

五、便携式福利账户如何运作？

How does a portable benefits account work?

If you're an independent worker, you will have access to a central account to pay for various benefit programs. Each business or client you work with can send a small portion of your pay into that portable benefits account. Some may want to pay more toward the benefits account, so you get a bonus.

That central account handles all your benefits — from health insurance to retirement savings — and in many cases does so with tax-advantaged dollars. Each month it automatically makes a payment to each of your desired benefits programs, and if there's any shortfall, it can just pull from your regular bank account.

You get similar benefits infrastructure as someone with a 9-to-5 job, but keep the freedom and autonomy that makes independent work so appealing.

六、为什么还没有普及？

So why doesn't every independent worker have a portable benefits account?

Unfortunately, many states and the federal government tell businesses that putting money into these accounts for an independent worker might make that worker an employee. And the business risks fines and punishment for misclassifying the worker. So most lawyers warn business owners not to pay toward benefits for independent contractors.

七、前进的路径：州与联邦层面的改革

The good news is there's a path forward.

  • A growing number of states have passed laws to make it clear that putting money into an independent worker's portable benefits account doesn't make that worker an employee.
  • Others are piloting portable benefits programs and allowing temporary experimentation.
  • And more states are looking to follow.

But merely fixing the state law problem leaves a federal gap too, that can still make paying for benefits risky everywhere in the country. So there are bills pending in both the House and Senate to fix this problem.

The best proposals would make it clear that a business can offer independent workers access to benefits accounts — like putting money into an IRA as they do for employees — without having those benefits or funds turn the independent worker into an employee.

八、结语：支持工作者选择权

Portable benefit accounts are the latest way to support worker choice.

For workers who want a 9-to-5 job with employee benefits, nothing will change. But for workers who want to be self-employed while still accessing benefits, and for businesses that want to hire independent workers and contribute to their benefits, portable benefits are the best path forward to support workers and businesses alike.

Heat. Heat.

观看原视频 →

这个视频表面在讲"便携式福利"，但隐藏的真相更尖锐——**美国税法在事实上对"不被雇佣"征收了一种隐形税**。雇员用税前美元购买健康保险，独立工作者用税后美元购买相同的保险，差价可能高达 22%-37%。换句话说，一个自由职业的设计师为了拥有"自由"，每年要多付几千美元的"自由溢价"。

大多数人把便携式福利看作一个**福利政策**问题。但如果用计算机架构的视角看，它其实是一个**API 解耦**问题：
