- **风险投资生态**：硅谷的真正护城河不是钱，而是"土壤"——失败过的创始人、二次创业者、退出后的天使、写过 term sheet 的律师。一个国家给再多补贴也复制不了这层腐殖质。 - **AI 训练数据**：大语言模型的能力不是来自"更多文本"，而是来自**几百年累积的、人类相互引用、注释、批评的文本网络**。没有印刷

item.title: [Business of Sport] 106 Points & Still Not Promoted…Does The System Need To Change?
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个出人意料的观察：106 分的"不公平"，其实是一场被误读的商业赌注

大多数人看到 Rochdale 拿 106 分却升不上去，第一反应是"系统坏了"。但真正反直觉的事实是：让 Rochdale 落空的不是 EFL（英格兰足球联赛），而是 National League 自己。EFL 给了两个升级名额，是 National League 主动决定把第二个名额变成"6 队附加赛"的——也就是说，他们用第二个自动晋级席位换取了电视转播价值与中游球队的"希望感"。这是一笔商业交易，伪装成了体育规则。

换句话说，球迷愤怒的对象搞错了。Rochdale 不是被"上层联赛"拒之门外，而是被自己的联赛用商业逻辑稀释掉了应得的确定性。这是一种典型的"组织把自己制造的痛点外包给上级背锅"的现象。

跨领域连接：足球升级机制 ≈ 风险投资中的"成熟度尽调"，也像学术界的 tenure track

Charlie 反对"三上三下"的真正逻辑，其实和风险资本拒绝过早 IPO、或者大学拒绝把博士后直接转成终身教授是同构的：

  • 基础设施的相变：从半职业到职业，不是"多赢几场"的渐变，而是要挖掉塑胶草皮、建青训学院、设立慈善基金会、改名（想想 Headington → Oxford United 那个 1961 年的条件）。这是身份的相变，不是排名的延伸。
  • Yo-yo 风险 = 资本错配风险：一支没准备好的球队上去再掉下来，损失的不只是积分，而是被永久性地破坏的收入结构（塑胶场地一旦挖掉，工作日租金就回不来了）。这与一家公司过早 IPO 后再退市的"反向估值毁灭"几乎一一对应。
  • 晋级 = 股东资格：最让我意外的细节是——升入 EFL 不只是"打更高级别比赛"，而是获得 EFL 的股东席位。这把"晋级"从体育叙事彻底翻译成公司治理叙事。难怪 1961 年要做"尽调"。

隐藏的系统性真相：体育公平的反面，不是不公平，而是"过早的公平"

人们以为体育系统的进化方向是"越来越开放、越来越多名额、越来越自动"。但 Charlie 揭示的是一个更深的悖论：当你把晋升通道拓得太宽，你实际上是在惩罚那些已经做好准备的俱乐部——因为他们将不得不与一批结构上还没完成相变的球队竞争生存空间，而联赛整体的"产品质量"会被拉低，最终伤害所有人。

这其实就是Goodhart 定律在体育治理中的镜像：当"积分"成为唯一指标，俱乐部就会优化积分，而不是优化"成为一家可持续职业俱乐部"所需的所有隐性资本（青训、社区、基础设施、财务韧性）。

所以，Rochdale 106 分的故事，真正的寓意不是"系统应该让他们上去"，而是：一个健康的系统，必须保留一些看起来"不公平"的摩擦，来强迫参与者完成身份的相变，而不是只完成数字的累积。

这甚至可以反过来照亮你正在做的内容管线——cron 跑得再勤，发布量再多，如果底层结构（schema、回流路径、读者关系）没有完成相变，"积分"再高也只是 yo-yo。

📝 转录文本

引子：一场最戏剧性的国内联赛收官

Charles: Football finally — quickly, because we had the most incredible scenario at the weekend happening.

Charlie: Now, this is very domestic. I think we have to say again to our foreign audiences, we've been discussing F1, we've been discussing world athletics. Those are genuine global things. This is quite domestic.

Charles: Strap in, because it's amazing, right? It's domestic, but it's an incredible story —

Charlie: — sporting contest.

Charles: Yeah. So I lay it up, and then you're going to smash it home for all the beliefs that you have on this, which are genuinely different to anything I've heard. So I cannot wait — cannot wait — for you to make me look silly.

Charlie: I could never do that, Charles. Only you do it yourself.

Charles: Very good.

York vs Rochdale：107 对 105 的世纪决战

Charles: Uh, so the National League — the the the top football league outside of um the well, the EFL, which is Championship, League One, League Two — has just had the most incredible season. York City and Rochdale have both had the most incredible run, getting over 100 points apiece. And this was neck-and-neck throughout the whole season.

It was teed up perfectly because what was the final game of the season? The final game was Rochdale versus York at Rochdale. And my god, it didn't disappoint.

So going into the game: York on 107 points, Rochdale on 105. Rochdale win and they're up. York draw or win and they're up. Rochdale scored a 95th-minute goal and the stadium goes mental — like pitch invasion. The whole thing — even though there's still time in the game left. Pitch invasion. They think they're up. Of course they do.

Um, now there's only one team that goes up automatically, which is what's going to be grounded in this conversation. One team goes up automatically and then there's a playoff underneath.

So Rochdale score, 95th, pitch invasion. Finally, they get the fans off, etc. You know, everyone calms down a bit. Game restarts, 3rd minute — so 13 minutes into stoppage time — York City score and York City go up.

没有门线技术的"门线时刻"

And actually the most amazing thing about this — go and watch the game, guys. Go literally go and take a look — because the linesman, the referee to give this goal is brilliant. It's a line… It doesn't hit the net. It's cleared just behind the line, and it would —

Charlie: No goal-line technology.

Charles: No goal-line technology. It would be so easy for that to have been missed, and it wasn't, in the most high-pressure environment.

Anyway, York City score to equalize — 1-1. York City end on 108 points. Rochdale end on 106.

提问：106 分还升不上去，合理吗？

Now I'm looking at that going: that is incredible. I'm also looking at it going: that is ridiculous. How can a team get 106 points and not get promoted? What a joke. National League — sorted out — two automatic places.

Charlie: Well, how can the National League sort that out? Sorry, what you're asking —

Charles: National League, EFL, like — whoever — football governance, just sort that out. Two teams up automatically. That can't happen.

Charlie: You asking for my opinion?

Charles: Yeah.

Charlie: You — you're actually looking like you got a slightly thick look on your face, as if you're actually waiting to be slapped.

Charles: I know. I want you to —

"Three Up" 运动登场

So Charlie is jumping on board a bandwagon which has been created by the National League. It's called "Three Up."

Charlie: Yeah.

Charles: Okay. So Jeff's bandwagon.

Charlie: Well, it's the National League as a whole, but Jeff Stelling is a very prominent pusher of this because his club Hartlepool is a National League club.

Okay, so the National League, just to reiterate what Charles says, is the fifth tier of English football. So it sits just outside the top four divisions and is the top tier of what we call the non-league game — right, the non-league pyramid. And the non-league pyramid starts at very, very low levels, almost at kind of village football level, and you can get promoted all the way up to the National League.

And if you come either in first place in the National League or win the next six-team playoff, as Charles said, those two teams then progress to the bottom division of the football league, and two teams from the football league get relegated back into the National League and they become what is called a non-league club.

So the National League is the top of non-league, and then you get the football league, which is the three-division pyramid running up to the Premier League.

Phil Alexander 的提案

Okay. So, the National League earlier this year, under the leadership of their chief executive Phil Alexander — who is a long-standing friend of mine, the former chief executive of Crystal Palace, and a great man who I have nothing but respect for — but genuinely, genuinely, and I totally respect Phil's campaign on behalf of his clubs to get three promotion places to go up to the football league, and therefore three relegation places from the football league to the National League.

Charles: When — sorry, can I just be clear? When you say three places, you mean — so one more automatic place plus the playoff place.

Charlie: Exactly that.

Charles: Okay. So two automatic positions plus the playoff places.

Charlie: Um, now let me explain why I think this isn't a very good idea.

历史课：从 1979 年的 Vauxhall Conference 说起

And to understand this, we have to go back into history. So the National League was formed in 1979. Back in those days, it was called the Conference. And those of my generation will grow up knowing it was sponsored by Vauxhall — so was known as the Vauxhall Conference. And the two leagues it was formed from were the Northern League and the Southern League. And those two — that part of the game was regionalized, effectively, as you can imagine: the Northern League and the Southern League. And the Northern League and the Southern League then have various divisions that ran below them which were also regionalized that fed into them.

And at this point, there was no automatic promotion of any kind between the non-league and the league.

Headington United 与 Ron Atkinson：1961 年的"选举"故事

Now, I've got sort of personal knowledge of this, albeit before I was born, but I grew up with the history of it — which is that the dominant Southern League team, the dominant non-league team in fact, of the 1950s, was a team called Headington United. Headington United were captained by a man who went on to become very famous, called Ron Atkinson. And their leading goal-scorer was his brother Graham Atkinson. And Headington United was in the suburbs of Oxford, but it's where Oxford Brookes University is right now.

Um, and they were basically winning the Southern League every year during the 1950s. But there was no way of getting promoted to the football league unless you got elected. And fundamentally, you got elected when the football league clubs decided that one of their member clubs was no longer fit to be in the football league. And that was normally a function of bankruptcy. One of the football league clubs would go bankrupt. They would get chucked out of the football league. They lose their shareholding in the football league. And then the football league clubs would elect a non-league club to take their place. And that election would not necessarily just be who's currently the top performing non-league team — it would be who is the current top performing non-league team who would actually make a good football league club, one of our partners, one of our business partners, one of our co-shareholders.

So what happened was, um, that Headington United applied for election when Accrington Stanley went bust in 1961. And uh, their directors had to go on a tour of the whole of the country trying to persuade the directors of football league clubs to elect them, and eventually, by a narrow margin, they did elect Headington United. But they made a condition. They said:

"You have to change your name to Oxford United. You can't be a shareholder and member of the football league whilst fundamentally being a village team. You've got to make yourself into a proper city team. You are now going to be a member of the football league — that comes with responsibilities. That comes with — you know — you have to be fully professional, previously not semi-professional. You have to be named after your city, not after your suburb," and so on and so forth.

Now, hold that thought — that there were conditions attached. You had to be a certain type of club. You had to be ready for it. You had to be a responsible future shareholder of the EFL —

Charles: Similar to what Prem Rugby's just done now.

Charlie: Exactly that.

1987：自动晋级的诞生

Right. Now, automatic promotion first got introduced in 1987. That was when the first team got automatically promoted from what was then the Conference, now the National League, into the football league. And it was Scarborough who got promoted, and Lincoln were a team who finished bottom of the whole football league who got relegated — funny enough, just been promoted to the Championship.

Okay. So the idea was, in those days, if you finished bottom of the pile, 92nd out of 92nd, you've kind of stunk the place out. You kind of need to go and put a cold towel around your head, sort yourself out, because we don't really want to have teams stinking the place out in the football league. So let's make it automatic. Let's not make it about an election. Let's make sure that our worst member, our worst shareholder, the one who stinks the whole place out, knows we're actually going automatically. We can't hang on just because we happen not to have gone bankrupt.

And it also then gave a pathway, as it were, to non-league clubs to say: it's now automatic. We don't have to seek election. If we are the top non-league club, we will get the privilege and the right to become a shareholder in the football league. This is what it is. It's not just being promoted into a competition. You become a shareholder in the football league when you get promoted.

2003：第二个名额，与一笔商业决定

Go to 2003 and they say — this is the football league saying — "Right, we think that the National League is stronger now, is better now, and we want to see a bit. We're going to offer a second place." Okay.

Now, the National League decided that they wanted that second place to be determined through a playoff. Now, this is a commercial move on behalf of the National League. Okay. Because what it means is — it means that more teams that are in the National League think that they've got a chance.

Charles: Makes sense.

Charlie: Right? As it does in other divisions. Okay.

Now, if we're talking strictly about what your challenge is, which is "Well, it's absolutely disgraceful that a team that gets 106 points shouldn't get promoted" — the only reason why Rochdale were not promoted this season was because of the National League, not the Football League. Because the football league says, "You've got two spaces." And if the National League decided that actually first and second should go up, then they would go up. It's the National League that decided, first of all, to have a 1-4 playoff, and then even more controversially, to extend it to a six-team playoff.

So actually, the jeopardy we're looking at here — which is that Rochdale, who've got 106 points, might not get promoted, and a team who only got 80, 85 points might get promoted — is entirely because the National League have decided that that's how they want to operate things.

想吃蛋糕又想留蛋糕

So what they're now saying is: "Nice bit of cake that —"

Charles: "I really enjoy that cake, and I'm going to eat it. I want to take some cake as well."

Charlie: "Three to four up in some, going through the leagues above."

Charles: That's correct. There's four-up go from League Two to League One.

Charlie: Right? That's consistent across those leagues. So if you're saying okay from two — because it's two down from League Two, two up from National League — really, are we saying here that there would be a concern that the teams that could come up, if it was three-up three-down, wouldn't be able to survive? Wouldn't be able to sustain a football league —

Charles: — fundamental set of requirements —

Charlie: Fundamentally, yes. That is my argument.

半职业 vs 职业：一次结构性的相变

But I'm going to explain what I mean — which is that the jump from League Two to League One within one sporting competition (which is the football league) is a very small jump. Right? The structural requirements for a League Two club are the same as the structural requirements for a League One club. You don't need to do anything different. You need the same type of pitch. You need to have the same type of safety standards around your stadium. You need to have a community trust — that's a club charity. You're very likely to have a proper women's team. You're very likely, within the academy system, to have a proper academy that brings all the young people from your area through. These are fundamentally requirements — or if not requirements, very close to being requirements — of league clubs.

The National League: not. So the National League doesn't have any of these requirements. There are clubs in the National League — there are competitive clubs in the National League. There are clubs in the top half of the National League that are semi-pro, whose players are not full-time. This is a totally different environment. Club charities? Well, if you want, go for it. If you don't want, don't worry about it.

Charles: And a football league club has to have a foundation.

Charlie: Yep. Exactly. Academy — hmm, if you want one, great. If you don't want one, not a problem. Right? So this is really fundamentally different.

Bromley 案例：塑胶草皮的代价

So what the challenge is here is, when a National League club gets promoted to the football league, it's going to have to quite fundamentally change itself in a number of different ways.

Okay, so if you look at Bromley, for instance, who recently got promoted from League Two to League One — going back 10 years, Bromley is a semi-pro club operating with an all-weather pitch, in a community-club kind of —

Okay. They then start to do better on a consistent basis, and they start to gear themselves up for: "We might get promoted to the football league. What does that mean?" That means we'd have to dig our pitch up, get a grass pitch, because you don't have all-weather pitches in the football league. What does that mean? What that means is, we can't rent out that pitch during the week, and that's a massive part of our revenues. Okay. So we're going to have to start thinking about our revenue base. If we don't have the revenues from the plastic pitch, then we've got potential revenue problems. Okay. Are we eventually going to have an academy? Okay, we have to start having a look at that. Are we going to need to institute a Bromley charitable trust? Okay, we have to start looking at that — so that by the time that they actually get into the football league, they are ready to be a football league club.

"Yo-yo 球队"：你最不想看到的副作用

Because the last thing you want is what I would call yo-yo clubs in this particular area. Okay? A club that gets on a nice little run in the National League, gets promoted to League Two, is in no sense ready to be in League Two — doesn't have an academy, has a plastic pitch, doesn't have the right safety requirements around the ground and so on so forth — and then struggles in League Two and comes straight back down, but then has dug up its plastic pitch, no longer has the revenues that it concerned, and so on and so forth.

So my point is: I think that two-up is adequate and correct. I think it is generous of the football league to allow this overall. Historically, it wasn't the case. Historically, it was a closed shop. It's now no longer a closed shop, and they've, in fact, even doubled the entry during my lifetime — they've doubled the number of clubs that can come in.

一个折中建议：拿掉附加赛，保留两个自动名额

But I don't see why — if the National League is genuinely concerned, from a sporting perspective, about the problem that you posed, which I do have sympathy with, which is "a team could have an outstanding season and only just miss out" — fine. Get rid of your playoff.

Charles: Get rid of your playoffs and just put two up.

Charlie: Now, of course, what Phil Alexander would say to me is "But those playoffs are crucial, because they give hope to the mid-table, and they're also good from a TV perspective." I go: "Okay, Phil, that's great, right? Totally get it — but you're not talking about sporting integrity there, are you? You're asking the EFL to pay for you to have the luxury of having a playoff system when you don't need to have one."

Charles 被说服了

Charles: That, and the yo-yo club point — you've got me there. Okay, I get that. And I completely agree. And it's easy to look at this just from the "how can that happen?" — but then also, you had a team, you know, "Oh, the poor old Liverpool, you know, give us a trophy for finishing second." You had a team that got like 97-98 points and didn't win the Premier League and some — just like — yeah, that can happen. So…

Look, I really like this — sorry, can I — I really like this with you putting that across, because the point that is continually drilled into everyone is "this is wrong, and we need to have more place. We need to have that extra place." So to hear that side of it is great, because that's grounded in rationale, and that's grounded in the broader conversations we have in football, which is sustainable product improvement. You know, developing the right structures in the system that actually supports all of the participants in it. That sounds like a very sensible explanation of why this is actually in place.

收尾：深度够，再谈三上三下

Charlie: It's a big jump, Charles. There are fundamental differences and changes that come from moving from a semi-professional league into a professional league. Um, and those need to be recognized.

And if at the end of it, it's decided structurally — "Do you know what, actually, there are three spaces here, because the depth in the National League is now so deep of the clubs who could automatically translate" — there. So we're not talking about four or five clubs, we're talking about 8, 9, 10, 11, 12 clubs who are league-ready, properly league-ready, with their foundations, with their academies, with all this type of stuff — okay, have a proper look at it. Okay. But do make sure that's the case.

Cuz what I'm seeing at the moment is, in the upper mid-table of the National League, you've got semi-pro teams. Wealdstone — Tamworth — you've got semi-pro teams. And great for them, by the way. But that's an awful long way away from league football.

Charles: Charlie — excellent as ever. Thank you. That was fascinating.

Charlie: Thanks, Charles.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个出人意料的观察：106 分的"不公平"，其实是一场被误读的商业赌注
大多数人看到 Rochdale 拿 106 分却升不上去，第一反应是"系统坏了"。但真正反直觉的事实是：**让 Rochdale 落空的不是 EFL（英格兰足球联赛），而是 National League 自己**。EFL 给了两个升级名额，是 National League 主动决定把第二个名额变成"6 队附加赛"的——也就是说，他们用第二个自动晋级席位换取了**电视转播价值与中游球队的"希望感"**。这是一笔商业交易，伪装成了体育规则。
换句话说，球迷愤怒的对象搞错了。Rochdale 不是被"上层联赛"拒之门外，而是被**自己的联赛**用商业逻辑稀释掉了应得的确定性。这是一种典型的"组织把自己制造的痛点外包给上级背锅"的现象。
## 跨领域连接：足球升级机制 ≈ 风险投资中的"成熟度尽调"，也像学术界的 tenure track
Charlie 反对"三上三下"的真正逻辑，其实和**风险资本拒绝过早 IPO**、或者**大学拒绝把博士后直接转成终身教授**是同构的：
- **基础设施的相变**

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Why Books Used to Cost More than Houses– Ada Palmer
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被忽视的因果链：气候带如何决定了知识的分布

最让人意外的不是"书曾经像房子一样贵"，而是这个价格差异本质上是一条等温线决定的。霜冻线——一条地理学上的隐形边界——成了中世纪欧洲知识贫困的根本原因。莎草纸不能在霜冻线以北生长，所以法国、西班牙、大部分意大利都被排除在"廉价书写"之外。这意味着欧洲的"黑暗时代"在某种意义上不是文化的黑暗，而是植物学的黑暗。当我们谈论"文明衰落"时，我们其实在谈论一个失去了某种农作物供应链的大陆。

更隐蔽的洞察是：罗马的崩溃并非"野蛮人入侵"那么戏剧化，而是一次供应链脆弱性事件——埃及一断货，整个帝国的官僚系统、税收、军队调度就瘫痪了。这听起来惊人地像 2020-2022 年的半导体危机：一个不起眼的"原材料"变成了整个文明的咽喉。Ada 揭示的不是历史，而是一个反复出现的系统模式——高度复杂的社会总是建立在某种被低估的、看似平凡的物质基础之上。

跨领域连接：羊皮书与今天的算力垄断

中世纪欧洲"巴黎大学图书馆只有 600 本书，而中国、中东、撒哈拉以南非洲都有数千本书"——这个对比颠覆了"欧洲中心论"的整个叙事框架，但更深的连接在这里：当书写媒介贵到等于一栋房子时，知识不是被审查的，而是被资本结构本身排斥的。这与今天大模型训练的局面惊人相似——GPT-4 级别的训练成本就是当代的"羊皮卷价格"，决定了谁能进入知识生产的前沿。我们以为我们生活在"信息廉价"的时代，但前沿知识的生产成本正在重新回到中世纪的曲线上。

还有一个更冷的观察：欧洲被迫使用昂贵媒介这件事，反而塑造了一种"高密度抄写美学"——精装插图圣经、誊抄修道院传统、对每一个字母的崇拜。当媒介稀缺时，文化会发展出"压缩与精雕"的审美；当媒介廉价时（中国的纸、今天的互联网），文化倾向于"丰饶与冗余"。所以中世纪欧洲手稿的金箔与彩绘，本质上和今天 Twitter 上的"shitposting"是同一个变量的两个极端。媒介成本决定文化形态，这是麦克卢汉没说透的一层。

一个反直觉的推论：稀缺曾经创造的不是匮乏，而是"权威"

如果一本书等于一栋房子，那么拥有一本书就等于拥有一种宗教式的权威。中世纪的"权威知识"不是因为内容更真，而是因为载体更贵。这解释了为什么印刷术之后宗教改革立刻爆发——不是路德的论点更有说服力，而是当圣经的物理价格崩塌时，教会作为"昂贵真理唯一守门人"的商业模式同步崩塌了。

把这个模型反过来推到今天：当 LLM 让"专家级文本生成"变得近乎免费时，我们正在经历一次新的"权威崩塌"——律师、医生、咨询师、学者，所有靠"昂贵文字"建立合法性的职业，都在面对一次中世纪修道院式的危机。Ada 这段话表面上在讲过去，实际上在描述我们正在经历的此刻。

📝 转录文本

印刷便宜了，纸也便宜了

So, not only does printing get cheaper, but around this time paper itself also gets cheaper. So, like not just reading, but writing gets cheaper. And do you as historians just see a market change in this period in the amount of records that are taken and as a result our understanding — or what what do you see?

一切取决于书写表面的价格

Amount rests on whether you have a cheap writing surface. And here, rather than looking first at the Renaissance, let's look at what we think of as fall of Rome. Because one of the biggest things that happens there is that Western and Northern Europe lose access to papyrus.

莎草纸：古代的廉价书写表面

Right? So, papyrus is the cheap writing surface of antiquity. It is a easy plant-based writing surface. You take this tall, thin water reed — you that is fibrous like asparagus. You slice it into ribbons. You set them out in the sun, a bunch of them parallel to each other sitting on a stone like noodles. You put a second row of noodles perpendicular to that on top, and then they dry in the sun and they're naturally sticky. They stick to each other. They produce a sheet. Practically no labor has gone into this. You've sliced, you've laid out, boom. Papyrus is a very inexpensive writing surface.

莎草纸如何支撑了罗马的官僚体系

And this is what enables Rome to have a bureaucracy and to have libraries — in any mid-size city will have a library. People can send letters back and forth. There can be enormous tax records.

Sometimes when Egypt and Rome are at war, Egypt will be like, "No, we are angry. You will stop exporting papyrus. No papyrus for Rome." And then Rome's infrastructure will fall apart overnight, because you can't do anything if you can't write stuff down.

霜冻线：一条决定文明的隐形边界

Papyrus is a warm weather plant. It is killed by frost. You cannot grow it north of the frost line. So, France, Spain, even most of Italy — you can only grow papyrus down in the very tip and down in Sicily, right?

没有莎草纸，你写在死羊身上

Um, without papyrus, what you're writing on is a dead sheep. And if you think of the price of a head of lettuce and the price of a leather jacket, you're understanding the difference between a sheet of papyrus and writing on a dead sheep.

一本中世纪书等于一栋房子

So, every page of a medieval book is as expensive as that much of a leather jacket. And a medieval book on parchment — cow, handwritten — costs as much as a house. So, that a small pocket copy of a book costs as much as a studio condo. And a big illuminated fancy Bible, you're spending on that what you would spend on a villa in the countryside.

Right? This is an enormous expense. And so, to have a library is to be not just rich, but mega-rich. So, only the wealthiest cities contain anybody who has a library.

巴黎大学图书馆只有 600 本书

The great library of the University of Paris — the library from Europe's perspective — has 600 books. There's definitely more than 600 books in this room. Right? Every kiosk at an airport selling Dan Brown novels has more than 600 books. This is nothing.

与此同时，世界其他地方……

And at the same time as that, right? In the Middle East, sultans have libraries of over 1,000 books or 5,000 books. There are libraries in sub-Saharan Africa with thousands of books. There are libraries in China with thousands of books. Because they in China have cheap paper and rice paper. The Middle East has papyrus. Europe — and only Europe — is writing on a leather jacket.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

## 一个被忽视的因果链：气候带如何决定了知识的分布
最让人意外的不是"书曾经像房子一样贵"，而是**这个价格差异本质上是一条等温线决定的**。霜冻线——一条地理学上的隐形边界——成了中世纪欧洲知识贫困的根本原因。莎草纸不能在霜冻线以北生长，所以法国、西班牙、大部分意大利都被排除在"廉价书写"之外。这意味着欧洲的"黑暗时代"在某种意义上不是文化的黑暗，而是**植物学的黑暗**。当我们谈论"文明衰落"时，我们其实在谈论一个失去了某种农作物供应链的大陆。

item.title: [Business of Sport] F1 Returns: The Rules Have Changed…BUT Have They Done Enough?
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个意外的观察：F1 正在享受"声誉的复利红利"

最让我意外的不是规则失败本身，而是这次失败几乎没有引发问责风暴。两位主持人无意中点出了一个深刻的系统性现象——当一个组织积累了足够厚的"叙事资本"（Liberty Media 接手以来的成功故事），它就获得了一种近乎金融意义上的"信誉对冲"：同样的失败事件，落在 Chelsea 这样的"负面叙事"组织上会被解读为体制性溃烂，落在 F1 身上却被自动翻译为"敏捷迭代"。

这其实是叙事层面的马太效应。声誉不只是结果，它本身就成了下一轮判决的输入参数。一个 20 岁车手以 50G 撞墙——在另一个叙事环境下，这可能就是头条丑闻和高层下台；在 F1 这里，它被吸收进"功能良好的治理系统"的故事里。Bman 的撞车不是规则的反例，反而被叙事吞噬成了规则有效性的证据。

跨领域连接：F1 的"超车通胀"和社交媒体的"参与度通胀"是同一个病

Verstappen 嘲讽这是"马里奥赛车"——这句话比表面看起来更尖锐。F1 此次改革的副作用是让超车从稀缺事件变成了常态事件，而稀缺性正是戏剧性的来源。这和社交媒体平台为了 KPI 不断降低互动门槛是完全同构的现象：当你把"点赞"从一种判断动作变成滑动反射，每个赞的信号价值就趋近于零。

银石赛道全场起立的那一刻，本质上是一种信息论意义上的高熵事件——因为超车困难，所以一次成功超车携带的信息量极大，观众的情绪共振也最强。一旦超车被工程化为每圈都发生的事情，它就从"事件"退化为"背景噪音"。F1 的真正风险不是技术规则失败，而是它可能在不自知中把自己最值钱的资产——稀缺性——给稀释掉了。这是体育版的"格雷欣法则"：低质量的超车驱逐高质量的超车。

隐藏的系统性真相：B 队结构是体育的"影子银行"

关于 Toto Wolff 想买 Alpine 这一段，最深的洞察其实主持人没有明说——多队所有权问题在结构上等同于金融体系里的"影子银行"：它存在于规则的灰色边缘，名义上是独立实体，实际上承担着主体的风险敞口和战略意图。Red Bull 的二队在 2005 年被允许，是因为当时 F1 处于"流动性危机"（凑不齐发车格），任何愿意进场的资本都是救星。这恰好平行 2008 年监管者放任影子银行扩张的逻辑——危机时期的应急豁免，在繁荣期变成了系统性套利通道。

Zak Brown 提到 Ricciardo 抢最快圈的那个细节是关键证据：协调行为不需要明文指令就能发生，仅仅是所有权结构本身就改变了博弈的纳什均衡。UEFA 在 Crystal Palace 案中的强硬处理透露了一个深层原则——在体育运动中，"感知的公正"比"实际的公正"更重要，因为这门生意的产品本质上是不确定性，而不确定性一旦被怀疑被操纵，整个产品就崩塌了。

这也解释了为什么 F1 现在反而更难处理这个问题：当资产价值从"赠送"变成"数十亿"，过去的临时安排就被资本化、合法化、路径依赖化。Liberty 真正面临的考验不是混合动力的工程问题，而是如何在不引爆既得利益的前提下，把当年危机时期的"应急 patch"从系统中拆除。这和拆除任何成熟监管体系里的影子结构一样——拖得越久，拆除成本越呈指数级上升。

📝 转录文本

开场：F1 回归与五周空档

Charlie: Formula 1 is back. The Miami Grand Prix this weekend. So, it's it's probably a bit of a relief to the rulers of the sport that they've had this break.

Well, it's funny you should say that. It's very funny you should say that cuz — and I wouldn't often say that. Normally, it'd be an absolute nightmare, but it sort of feels to me like they've needed this break.

Well, yes. And why have they needed this break?

And I think they can look at it. So, just if everyone remembers, this break happened between Suzuka and Japan and now the Miami Grand Prix because there were two races scheduled in the Middle East — one in Bahrain and one in Saudi in Jeddah — and with the Iran war, the US–Iran war, those two races were canceled. So that creates a five-week gap in the calendar.

Normally, I don't know how that would be felt — you know, in a year where there hadn't been massive regulation change and everything was as normal, would have been quite an interesting time to ask some of these team principals whether the breaking momentum would have harmed them. However, as has been talked of so much around this Formula 1 season, this year was a standout year. It was the most radical regulation changes in F1 history had been implemented as they moved to a hybrid car. So half electric, half combustion.

规则刚改完，又改规则

The question that I think we need to really look at here is: in that time period that we just had in those 5 weeks, why has there had to be regulation change already to the regulation changes?

So there have been a number of changes that have been put in by the FIA and Formula 1 that have addressed issues that came up in those first few races but also in testing before the start of the season, which drivers and team principals had raised as concerns for mostly safety of the drivers.

Now, why did it take for a 20-year-old to smash into a wall at 194 km an hour, 50 gs of force, to make this happen? You said something to me the other day that was quite interesting and I'm going to look at it on both sides and see your thoughts, but you think that Formula 1's got off a little lightly with these changes because this has been positioned as such a momentous moment in F1's history.

批评视角 vs 正面视角

Now, I'll present you a critical and a positive. I kind of wrote two down and just let's start with that and see your thoughts.

So if I'm being critical about changes having to be made within the first three weeks, I'd go: this is a governance failure.

Yeah. Shambles.

It's a potentially very costly one.

Yeah.

Um, everything's been built up to this moment and you're basically saying, well, you got that wrong.

Yeah.

If I'm putting the positive case across, it's: new regulation is unlikely to ever be perfect. Acting within three races with unanimous buy-in from all stakeholders is actually a sign of a functioning system.

Two things can be true at the same time. It can be a total shambles which is then addressed in a competent way.

为什么说 F1 "侥幸过关"

So what did I mean by saying I think they got off lightly? Well, because when you start a sporting competition and within a couple of months of starting that sporting competition you have to rip up your rule book because it hasn't worked for whatever reason, that is a crashing failure. Crushing and crashing.

And it's the type of thing under normal circumstances which would get people pointing fingers left, right, and center. It was his fault. It's that fault, or should never have been here, or shouldn't have been there. Sackings, resignations, and all this type of stuff.

I think the general sentiment around Formula 1 over the last 5 years has been so positive, and the view about what Liberty have done since taking over the running of Formula 1 has been so positive, that there's this sort of inset narrative that basically: sport's going great, industry's going great, everything's great, and the people that run it are great, and so on and so forth. So that when something terrible happens, there's almost a sort of see-no-evil hear-no-evil. "Oh, um, right, we have to change a few regulations. Oh, that's a bit embarrassing. Uh, well, it's still all great by the way."

叙事的力量：以 Chelsea 作对比

Whereas if you look at, for instance, on a different topic — once a narrative sets in, like it has done in your head and the general sporting public's head, that the people running Chelsea are a bunch of muppets who don't know what they're doing, then even things which happen which actually are not that abnormal suddenly become supposedly symptomatic of much broader failures, right? And which maybe they are, right? It's not abnormal for a manager to be fired, but the extent to which that's taken on an existential meaning, a much broader meaning, is because of an established narrative that's been taking place over a period of time.

And F1's rulers are benefiting right now from the exact opposite, which is: there is an established narrative that the people who are running Formula 1 are brilliant, know what they're doing, really get it, etc. So that when something terrible happens like this, it's almost sort of: "Well, it clearly can't really be their fault really because they are geniuses who know exactly what they're doing. So it's just an adjustment we need to make and let's just try and brush it under the carpet and forget it ever happened."

这次失误的代价究竟多大？

So I guess it's a way of saying I don't want to sort of hang people out to dry just for the sake of it. But we should acknowledge — you know, when we're talking about, I think, you know, we asked the question last week with reference to Live about great sporting disasters, great sporting commercial disasters, and we were saying CrickIt, right, six billion on something like that — that surely is the biggest sporting disaster of all time without any question, etc. — which it is.

And people then were sending in other stuff, like for instance talking about, you know, Michael Johnson's athletics Grand Slam Track that we were discussing just now, which lost 30 million bucks. I rather suspect this mistake costs more than 30 million bucks. I just gently suggest that this is quite a large sum of money that's going to be wasted here.

Oh, completely. And if you think about the broader value of what this regulation change brings in — if it actually isn't that consumable and people don't enjoy it, and the drivers don't like it, and the success that the sport's quickly grown and the financial power that it has being then corrupted and harnessed — people have said, well, you know, that impact is going to be huge. So to make the change is not good.

Bearman 撞车事件还原

But, you know, let's just — I want to just be really clear that people understand this because it has been a bit technical, and even these changes have been a bit technical. So, you know, I wrote some stuff on it beforehand, but I mentioned the Ollie Bearman crash in Suzuka.

So he was basically approaching Franco Colapinto, who drives for Alpine, at a closing speed of 50 km an hour in a turn — not because either of them were driving recklessly, but because the regulations basically created this situation. Now what do I mean by that? Bearman was deploying his battery boost mode. So something that's come in with the electric.

So what was the speed again?

50 km an hour. I mean really we're talking 190 he crashed at. So Bearman was deploying his battery boost mode.

Yeah.

Colapinto was harvesting his — like super clipping it's called — which basically meant the car effectively is decelerating. Bearman goes off the track going round and at a huge speed of 190 km an hour and hits the wall with 50 gs of force.

Now, the stewards investigated this and they didn't take any action against either driver, and their reasoning was clear on that because the fault was structural. It wasn't human.

车手的预警：Norris 与 Sainz

Now, Charlie, if you remember, we've talked about some great quotes from drivers — mostly laughing at some of Max Verstappen's comments being rather unhappy — but, you know, that there was one in particular that stood out for me from Lando Norris that we had brought up before, right after the Melbourne Grand Prix, so the first race of the season. And he said, "It's chaos and we're going to have a big accident."

Carlos Sainz followed that up just after Suzuka when Bearman had crashed and he said, "We've been warning this kind of accident will always happen. In Suzuka we were lucky there was an escape road. Imagine going off in Baku or Singapore or Las Vegas with those speeds."

Now just to be clear for people that don't know, those are what you call city tracks. They are very close, tight-walled races. Whereas the racetrack, you know, you normally have far long sweeping corners which give you some chance to decelerate — but still hit it pretty hard.

改动的方向与历史先例

The four to five changes that have been made — without getting too technical again for people — they're basically done to improve safety.

What I did want to say is, you know, F1 has a history of regulation change. What's happened this year isn't new, and sometimes it does take a long time for them to actually prove valuable, why, you know, why they work.

The ground effect cars in the 1980s produced aerodynamic instability. Um, if you — well, I don't remember that, but you probably will. The refueling era which then came after that created a serious fire hazard in a lot of the cars.

But I'm looking at this again from the positive side of going: well, they've made a change relatively soon after implemented because it's been seen to be dangerous.

Toto Wolff 的"手术刀"比喻

But I think Toto Wolff's key point on this seems about right, because he basically called for a scalpel, not a baseball bat. They've taken swift action. They haven't let the whole thing play out over 6 months, 8 months, or even to the end of the season with problems escalating left, right, and center. They've jumped in and they've gone, "Okay, we've taken it on board. We are changing it."

And I guess you would say that is the sign of functionality within a sport and an industry. They haven't waited for the whole thing to become sort of, you know, too dramatic, too bad. They have applied their scalpel quite quickly and efficiently. Well, we hope efficiently. We haven't seen yet. We haven't seen what the outturn of it is, but we assume that they've got it right the second time around.

No. And we'll get a good grasp of it this weekend and see, and I'll be interested to see how the drivers speak about it as well. Drivers interviewed throughout an entire weekend.

对观众而言：超车多就是好吗？

Yeah, it will. I think that the other point which you kind of touched on at the start of this conversation is: we still don't know yet what the impact this is going to be on viewers. Um, on whether these changes actually are good for racing, full stop.

Because the outturn from an uneducated perspective — so from a normal viewer who just likes watching cars racing, as opposed to the more technical level of people who's really interested in the intricacies of what's happening with the engines — basically, what this has led to is more overtaking.

So the question is — you know, something that Max Verstappen is sneery about and which Lewis Hamilton says he likes — we don't yet know whether the viewing public likes to see what I think it was Verstappen or somebody else called "Mario Racing", where it's literally backwards and forwards, backwards and forwards, backwards and forwards. Or if, you know, actually they prefer it when an overtaking maneuver happens but it's a rare event. So when it does happen, it's actually dramatic.

Because I have to say, when I've been live, been at Silverstone or when I was working on the Bahrain Grand Prix, and you're watching the race and you're aware that it's a big battle and it's very hard to get past — when the overtaking maneuver eventually happens, the whole place lifts off, because they're in recognition that was an amazing piece of driving to be able to pull that off under the circumstances.

So, I just am interested on whether making it a more common occurrence genuinely adds to the sport for the mass viewing public, or it doesn't.

I have found the first few races an enjoyable consumer experience, but I also firmly understand that what we do not want to see is a return to days where drivers get in cars, have major accidents, and die.

Yeah, absolutely.

So, you know, I want to enjoy it, but I don't want it to be dangerous to do so.

转向：Toto Wolff 与 Alpine 收购争议

Finally, just on Formula 1 before we move on — have you seen some of the comments that Zak Brown's made about the potential Toto Wolff?

Yes. Yes. Now, this is something that we highlighted quite early on in our breakdown journey — is that there was the possibility, or in fact not the possibility, there was a sort of corporate battle going on in terms of who would be able to buy — which team was it? Is it, Charlie?

The Alpine team. And there were two contenders for this. One of which was a consortium led by Christian Horner coming back into the sport to introduce a new independent team that would be able to compete. And the other one of which was Toto Wolff, the Mercedes boss, effectively being allowed to buy up a second team to help his overall effort within within the industry.

And I think at the time our conclusion was that we wanted Horner back and we wanted another independent team. We wanted more competition.

What's Zak Brown said about it?

Zak Brown 的立场

Well, Zak's actually — Zak Brown being the McLaren boss. Zak Brown is the CEO of McLaren. He said something very similar. Zak's always been very consistent on his position on this.

So I'm going to say this 'cuz he's treading a slightly careful line in saying that he doesn't want Toto and Mercedes to have it because Mercedes supply the power unit for McLaren. So he needs to have a good relationship there. But I think he can say what he said because he has always been very consistent. He does not agree with teams with an A and B team structure, should we say. He doesn't agree, in this day and age where Formula 1 is so strong, two owners having two teams.

Yeah.

Sorry. The same owner having two teams.

Yes.

Alpine 的所有权拆解

Otro Capital and Renault own Alpine. Otro Capital is a financier. They have 24%. Renault is the car manufacturer. They have 76% — but, key, a bit Jim Ratcliffe and Man United: Otro Capital's 24% is, like, the — should we say — the sporting operational side. So whoever owns that 24% will have a huge say of how the team is run.

Zak's key point, right, is that it creates unfair advantages for teams to have a direct relationship. And he's not wrong. Of course he's not wrong.

历史回顾：Red Bull 的二队为何被允许

The reason it exists in one part on the grid at the moment in Red Bull and Racing Bulls — now the environment for Formula 1 was very, very different when that deal happened. It happened in 2005 where teams were basically not valuable assets. They were being given away pretty much.

They weren't just not valuable, Charlie. There were seasons. There were seasons when you couldn't fill the whole grid up.

Mhm.

So anyone who wanted a team — yeah — you were kind of like, "Come in and do it." So, Red Bull taking two — brilliant. That's one less we have to worry about.

Absolutely.

Now, fast forward 21 years, the sport is in a completely different place and these are now multi-billion assets even at the back end of the grid. So, Zak's point is this compromises sporting integrity and fairness. The sport is in the strongest it has ever been. And his quote: "Can you imagine a Premier League game where you've got two teams competing owned by the same person?"

Yeah. No. Couldn't. It's banned. Right. Can't have that.

对现状的呼吁

So, he's basically — he has previously been suggesting that, you know, FIA should force Red Bull to sell one of those teams.

Yeah.

Now, that's of the existing landscape, let alone letting Mercedes purchase a very significant stake in another team.

不公平的具体证据

And he, you know, previously has highlighted examples of why it's unfair. Daniel Ricciardo, which we've mentioned before, in 2024, took the fastest lap, basically changed his tires for the last lap of the race and took it off Lando Norris, which got an extra point at the time that had a say in the title in the championship race.

But also, this is the other interesting one which people, you know, must consider — and it's the movement of the executive between teams. So Red Bull, when Christian Horner left, just took Laurent Mekies from Racing Bulls, a very respected and established engineer/team principal. McLaren have just signed Verstappen's race engineer called Gianpiero Lambiase from Red Bull, but he can't join until 2028 because he has to see out his contract at McLaren.

The free flowing movement of executives on one side versus we're getting a top level engineer from another and we have to pay compensation and wait for his contract to end. So there's a driver perspective and what happens on the track. There's a development perspective — what happens behind the scenes.

Toto 的辩解与足球类比

Toto, very specifically to be clear, this is not a feeder team play. His quote is "No," and I'm telling you in big capital letters, "No, we do not want to create a feeder team."

Well, but surely it will be.

Look, there's a very good reason why in football, not only you not allowed to own two clubs in the same domestic ecosystem — even at UEFA level, as became clear with the whole controversy over Crystal Palace last summer, where the fact that John Textor had owned 40% of Palace, but also owned Olympique Lyon, that was enough basically to push Palace out of the Europa League.

Okay. So that even though they were in totally different countries, even though the likelihood of them actually ending up facing each other was very, very small, Palace were pushed down and out of the Europa League because UEFA couldn't accept it.

假想情景：感知与现实的双重风险

It's partially to do with reality. So let's just imagine that there was a situation where you were coming to the last race of the season and it was a pitch battle, as you would want it to be, between Mercedes and McLaren, and there was a situation that happened out on the track where Toto Wolff, being desperate for Mercedes to win, was in a position to influence how his other team acted during that race.

No one can tell me there wouldn't be more than a temptation — a major, serious, obvious incentive — to try and control things in that way, as you mentioned with Ricciardo in the Red Bull group.

But then there's also perception as well, and perception is really important because ultimately it's all about the punters. It's all about the audience that drives the commercials and drives the TV audience.

So even if Toto could put his hand on his heart and say, "No, I would never do that," the questions would be out there every race. "Surely he must be directing what's going on. He controls the sporting operation there. He controls a sporting operation here." That is a major perception problem. You just can't get around it. It just is.

结论：需求已变，规则该变

So I think, you know, you and I agreed on this 20 years ago. It was a question of needs must, and that's when you had Red Bull and Toro Rosso, as it was then called. And Bernie, who was running the sport at the time, needed to fill out the grid. "Okay, if you guys want to have two teams, have two teams, because I need to make sure we've got enough cars racing. Otherwise, we got a serious problem."

They don't need that anymore. If there is a spare place on the grid for an expansion franchise, let's say — imagine what that would go for. It would go for absolutely vast amounts of money.

So, there's no need to bend to somebody's desire to start monopolizing different parts of the grid. And I agree with Zak Brown. I think there should be a three-year period when Red Bull are told, "Right, over the next three years you need to divest that team." Right? It's a seller's market. Why wouldn't you? I mean, make a lot of money out of it — going to make a lot of money out of it. But from the sporting perspective, the more competitive teams you have on the grid, the better.

Completely agree.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个意外的观察：F1 正在享受"声誉的复利红利"
最让我意外的不是规则失败本身，而是**这次失败几乎没有引发问责风暴**。两位主持人无意中点出了一个深刻的系统性现象——当一个组织积累了足够厚的"叙事资本"（Liberty Media 接手以来的成功故事），它就获得了一种近乎金融意义上的"信誉对冲"：同样的失败事件，落在 Chelsea 这样的"负面叙事"组织上会被解读为体制性溃烂，落在 F1 身上却被自动翻译为"敏捷迭代"。
这其实是**叙事层面的马太效应**。声誉不只是结果，它本身就成了下一轮判决的输入参数。一个 20 岁车手以 50G 撞墙——在另一个叙事环境下，这可能就是头条丑闻和高层下台；在 F1 这里，它被吸收进"功能良好的治理系统"的故事里。Bman 的撞车不是规则的反例，反而被叙事吞噬成了规则有效性的证据。
## 跨领域连接：F1 的"超车通胀"和社交媒体的"参与度通胀"是同一个病
Verstappen 嘲讽这是"马里奥赛车"——这句话比表面看起来更尖锐。F1 此次改革的副作用是**让超车从稀缺事件变成了常态事件**，而稀缺性正是戏剧性的来源。这和**社交媒

item.title: [All-In Podcast] OpenAI Misses Targets, Codex vs Claude, Elon vs Sam Trial, Big Hyperscaler Beats, Peptide Craze
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的"权力倒挂"：算力不再是稀缺品，电力才是

表面上市场在讨论 OpenAI 没达成 10 亿周活、收入未达标、$6000 亿算力承诺难以兑现——但 Chamath 揭开了一个被普遍误读的真相：这不是需求问题，而是供给问题，而且供给的瓶颈不是 GPU，是变压器和电网基础设施。

让我给你一个意外的观察：当算力变成电力的奴隶时，AI 公司的护城河从"模型能力"悄悄迁移到了"电网谈判桌"。 这意味着 Dario Amodei 和 Sam Altman 现在的命运，不是由他们的研究员决定，而是由一个你从没听过的 PJM 互联队列调度员决定的。这就是为什么 Anthropic 必须给 Amazon 让出经济参与权——他们买的不是 EC2，他们买的是电力的优先排队权。这彻底重写了"AI 公司"的本质：它们正在从软件公司变成披着软件外衣的电力公用事业公司。

二、跨领域连接：墨西哥湾石油争夺战 × 19 世纪铁路 × 当下 AI 资本结构

播客里有人提到 Cisco 在 2000 年泡沫顶点的图——这是错的类比。真正的类比应该是 19 世纪 60 年代的美国铁路。

为什么？因为 Cisco 卖的是商品（路由器），最终因为暗光纤过剩崩盘。但铁路公司的崩盘不是因为铁路过剩，而是因为它们用股权换土地、用债务换路权，最后变成了"披着运输公司外衣的金融杠杆机器"。听 Chamath 描述：超大规模公司将"看起来像庞大笨重的工业企业"，他们会有"债务、定期贷款、循环信贷工具"——这就是 1869 年的 Union Pacific 翻版。

更让人意外的连接：Greg Brockman 写日记这件事和 19 世纪铁路大亨 Jay Gould 留下的财务备忘录在历史结构上是同构的。当一个产业进入"权力极度集中、利益分配未定"的临界点时，参与者会本能地开始"自我记录"——既是辩护，也是炫耀，也是对历史地位的押注。Brockman 不是在写日记，他是在为一个尚未发生的传记作者预先准备素材。这是权力转折期人物的共同病症。

三、最反直觉的洞见："反刍"作为一个跨系统隐喻

播客里 Jason 说"反刍是不快乐的根源，不要写日记"——表面是生活建议，但请注意系统级的对应：

  • Brockman 的反刍 → 法律风险敞口（discovery maxing）
  • AI agent 在删除生产数据库前的"过度自信" → 缺乏元认知反刍（"我应该停下来吗？"）
  • OpenAI 持续公开自己的 KPI 和未达成目标 → 让自己暴露在情绪化抛售中
  • Cisco 投资者 25 年的痛苦 → 集体反刍创造的价值毁灭

真正惊人的洞见：人类的心理健康危机和 AI 系统的可靠性问题，本质是同一个元认知校准问题的两面。健康的人和健康的 AI 都需要能识别"我不知道我不知道什么"的边界——心理学叫"元认知"，AI 工程叫"calibrated uncertainty"。Sax 说的"AI 不知道它不知道什么"和治疗师面对的"病人不知道他在反刍什么"是同一个问题在不同基底上的实现。

所以你下一个真正的赌注不是模型规模，而是元认知校准——能让 agent 在删除数据库前停下来的那个机制，本质上和让一个焦虑症患者识别"这个想法是反刍"的机制，是同一种神经技术的不同实例。这才是 GPT-5.5 cyber 比 Mythos 更有意义的真正原因：它不是更强，而是更知道自己什么时候该停。

四、一个意外的产业地形预言

Freeberg 提到 BCG 的"三的法则"——成熟市场最终会演化成 4:2:1 的市场份额比例。但我要给你一个出人意料的推论：AI 不会演化出一个市场，而是会演化出至少四个独立的 4:2:1 市场——消费、企业、编码、网络安全。每一个市场的领导者都不同，且这些市场的领导者会互相吞食对方的领地。

最反直觉的预测：Anthropic 在企业市场可能输给 Google，但他们会在"被 Elon 收购"这条路径上找到第二曲线。Chamath 已经埋下伏笔："Elon 和 Dario 明天就该做个交易"。如果 xAI 的电力 + Anthropic 的模型 + Tesla 的物理世界数据合体，那就不是"市场份额"游戏了——那是直接重写棋盘。

📝 转录文本

开场闲聊：Miss Thing 播客与"Gay Name / Straight Name"

Jason，你想给我们讲讲你新喜欢的播客吗？哦，太棒了。我的信息流现在因为，你知道，自从取消文化结束后，Sachs，每个人都在用 R 词和 F 词。我 Instagram 整个信息流要么是 gay、要么是唐氏综合症、要么是斗牛犬。就是这三种之一。

然后我偶然发现了 Miss Thing 播客。他们做了一个段子叫 "gay name, straight name"。这是 David 的 gay name 还是 straight name？这是好消息和坏消息环节。来吧。

Gay name 还是 straight name？ David。 David 对我来说是直的。 好的。但他有我心目中完美的身材。这可能让人困惑，因为我会想，你是 gay 吗？然后又觉得，不，我只是想成为你，David。 完全是。就像米开朗基罗的大卫，男性理想形象。难以置信的身体，有点小。抱歉。 是的。 哦，有点粗糙。 什么？ Jal，你在那儿看什么？ 他们基本上把这两个搞定了。好，继续。 我不认为 Chamath 在他们的短名单上，但我知道 Jason 会在某个时候出现。 Gay name 还是 straight？ 也许就是这个。 Chamath，数到三。是的。 三、二、一。Gay。我看到的是意大利毛衣，那种特别响亮鲜艳的毛衣。他会穿去扑克之夜，和他的兄弟们。不是直人扑克之夜哦，是 gay 扑克之夜，在酒吧。 是的。总在谈红酒。 谈红酒。 总是那种——是的，没错。 是的。还有，他就像在健身房脱衬衫自拍的家伙。 是的。其他人都在想，"Chamath，对不起，我想用一下镜子。我想看看自己。 下次扑克之夜见。" 完全是。 你带红酒。 我们带毛衣。 是的，就是这样。哇，他们做到了。

那真是太棒了。我向 Miss Thing 播客的兄弟们致敬。

哇，太棒了。我觉得我是 gay。我从来不知道。 让你的赢家继续奔跑。 我们把它开源给粉丝，他们已经疯了。 你做了什么？像 cameo 一样？你付钱让他们做的？ 他们作为人情为我做的。所以他们做了。 太棒了。所以，谢谢那些 Miss 的兄弟们。我以前在剪辑里看到过他们。我觉得他们非常有趣。 太棒了。向我的兄弟们致敬。 太棒了。

节目开场

好的，各位。说真的，欢迎回到世界上排名第一的播客。这是 All-In 播客，再次和我，David Freebergia, David Sachs，当然还有，我是 Jason Calcanis。如果你是第一次来这里，可以叫我 Jay Cal。

话题一：OpenAI 没达成目标

OpenAI，他们 ChatGPT 的目标没达成，Freeberg，用户和收入都没达成。我们来谈谈这个。

《华尔街日报》在周二的一份突破性调查报道中说，OpenAI 期望在 2025 年底之前达到 10 亿周活跃用户。他们没达到，2026 年过去四个月了仍然没达到这个里程碑。

另外，Chamath，他们没达成 ChatGPT 的 2025 年收入目标。具体数字没说明，但正如我们在这里讨论的，他们的年化收入率为 2030 亿。这个行业还有一些会计细节有待解决。

为什么这很重要： 1. OpenAI 在算力上有 6000 亿美元的支出承诺。说明白点，这大约相当于他们在二级市场上的交易价值。换句话说，OpenAI 整个企业的价值等于他们未来一年的支出承诺。 2. 据报道，即将上市的 CFO Sarah Friar 担心收入增长不够快，跟不上支出，OpenAI 想在今年晚些时候 IPO。这让 Friar 和 Altman 产生了冲突，或者也许这是自然的紧张关系。Friar 不认为 OpenAI 已经准备好接受公开报告标准。据《华尔街日报》报道，Altman 显然想行动得更快，所以他们发布了一份联合声明，"这太荒谬了，等等等等。"

Sachs，我们来谈谈你。你怎么看？这些是重大逆风，还是只是作为我们这一生最重要的竞赛——通往超级智能的竞赛——的领跑者在管理预期？

Sachs 的逆向看法

我实际上对此有一些逆向看法。我知道 OpenAI 度过了非常糟糕的一周。就像你说的，他们有那篇《华尔街日报》文章，说他们没达成数字。他们没达成 10 亿用户增长目标。他们没达成收入数字。这让人质疑他们是否能负担得起他们承诺的数据中心。除此之外，他们本周还和 Elon 打了官司。所以在新闻上，对他们来说这是相当糟糕的一周。

但我对此有一个逆向看法：在过去一两周，如果你看产品层面发生了什么，对他们来说是相当不错的几周。

他们发布了 ChatGPT 5.5，我在硅谷接触到的人的评论非常强劲。你跟开发者、编码者交谈，他们对此非常满意。同时，Opus 4.7，最新的 Anthropic 发布版本，似乎是个失败品。人们在抱怨它。在很多情况下他们回滚到 4.6。他们说 Opus 4.7 在配给算力，减少思考时间，没那么好。Claude 也有一些 bug。

所以如果你只是把 ChatGPT 5.5 和 Opus 4.7 比较，OpenAI 在产品层面似乎度过了更好的几周。我相信产品改进会继续。GPT 5.5 基于一个叫 Spud 的新基础模型，这是他们一年多来做的第一次基础模型升级，有了新的基础模型也将为未来的改进铺路。

OpenAI 现在对他们的产品感觉相当乐观，你开始在 X 上看到一些开发者的势头在转移。我看到很多人说他们正在把编码使用从 Opus 转移到 GPT 5.5。

我认为 SAM 可能最终是对的，但是出于错误的原因。我的意思是，当他做出这些大算力承诺时，是基于在消费端达到 10 亿用户和达到收入目标的估计。消费业务最终很弱，所以他们没达成这些目标。但与此同时，编码已经成为 AI 全方位重要的领域。因为他们做出了所有这些算力承诺，建立了这些数据中心，他们现在比 Anthropic 拥有更多算力。Anthropic 是 token 受限的，这降低了他们服务 Mythos 的能力，导致他们对 Opus 4.7 进行算力门控。我理解 Dario 为什么做出那个决定。我不是说，我的意思是这是一个谨慎的商业决定，我不批评他。但我认为，再次，Sam 可能最终在这里是对的，但理由是错的：他在消费端失利，但企业端蒸蒸日上，并给他能力赶上代码端。

Polymarket 与 IPO 时间表

你的 Polymarket——

这是目前最重要的市场—— 当然，我们上周谈到了 Grok 和 Cursor 联手，Elon 和那边的团队。

Polymarket 现在显示 OpenAI 在 2026 年底之前公开上市的概率为 32%。这比 12 月的 60% 下降了。Chamath，你给出了一些警告——嘿，资金有限，SpaceX IPO 显然先出来。现在如果 OpenAI 今年不出去而 Anthropic 出去，这就建立了一个有趣的动态。

Chamath：电力才是真正的瓶颈

你对 OpenAI 做出的巨大承诺有什么看法？他们会跑下悬崖，还是最终会很出色？即使不是出于他们认为的确切战略原因——

我认为他们会没事的。我认为这是一家市值数万亿美元的公司。我认为 Anthropic 是一家市值数万亿美元的公司。我认为现在发生的事情是对 AI 世界内部实际发生的事情的完全误解。

有一个非常具体的瓶颈正在限制一切：那就是获取驱动这些 token 所必需的电力的能力。

OpenAI 没达成的目标，我认为这是对今天没有足够算力容量的洞察。这个问题只会变得更糟。你已经在 Anthropic 看到了这一点，他们刚刚找到了一种方法在经济上诱使 Amazon 给他们足够的容量，这样你就不必通过 Bedrock 路由来访问 Anthropic 模型。你也看到他们现在与 Google 等人做差异化交易，在他们已经有的基础上加上经济参与，以获得更多容量。

我的观点是什么？这个市场上的一切都受到电力限制。这些公司可能没达成数字或预测的原因与需求无关，完全 100% 是由于生成输出 token 所必需的电力供应。

今天宣布的一件非常有趣的事情会让这个问题更加严重，那就是你现在开始看到积压不仅仅是获取电力，还有实际必要的组件。不仅仅是往复式发动机，不仅仅是天然气涡轮机，现在你在谈论变压器和实际的战术电网基础设施。

为什么这很重要？如果你看看实际正在建设的千兆瓦数量，我们现在有巨大的不匹配。Jason，人们已经宣布了所有这些项目，但实际正在建设的不到一半。不到一半。其中大部分卡在繁文缛节中。其中大部分是因为有这些供应链延迟。所以没有可信的策略来打开任何这些东西。

这会伤害谁？它会最伤害 Anthropic 和 OpenAI。这会让谁受益？它会让超大规模公司受益，特别是 Oracle、Amazon、Meta、Microsoft 和 Google。现在你将看到的是来回的谈判和交易。我必须放弃多少股权？我必须放弃多少控制权来获得算力，相对于如果我不这样做我会错过多少增长预测？

这意味着，我们上周谈到了，对于 Grok 来说，这是一个巨大的赛道，可以直接冲过去，对于 SpaceX 来说也是如此，因为他们有大量的过剩容量。所以我认为 Cursor 交易是开胃菜。但如果我是 Elon，我现在会在这个市场上四处奔跑，因为如果模型在质量上赶上来，我认为他现在也可以与 Anthropic 或 OpenAI 做一些非常疯狂的事情。也许不是 OpenAI，因为——

我们一会儿会谈到诉讼—— 那些包袱。 是的。 但是，他和 Dario 明天就应该做个交易。

限制资源的澄清

所以你在框架化，嘿，这里的有限资源是算力——

不，限制资源是电力。电力，然后驱动算力，然后提供 token，然后为大规模的开发者、协作者和所有这些其他消费者和企业无法获得足够的项目提供服务。

明白了。

Jason，对 Anthropic 和 OpenAI 来说，让事情变得复杂的另一个因素是所有那些坐在那里转着拇指的东西。其中 40% 会被取消，因为他们在围绕 AI 创造一个良好的积极光环方面做得太差了，以至于 40% 的所有宣布的项目被取消，因为过去四年中 40% 的所有项目都被取消了。是的。还有一些关于数据中心、AI、工作等的不好的感觉。这造成了一些逆风。人们真的在做暴力的事情，把它归咎于数据中心和 AI。我不想给它太多播出时间。

Freeberg：BCG 的"三的法则"与市场结构

Freeberg，你对我们正在看的棋盘有什么看法？无论是通过算力、能源还是通过在商业层面上市，资本、算力和能源的战略性质现在在这种巨大需求中扮演了一个角色。这仍然是一个空中球的游戏。

BCG 有一个理论，我想我以前讲过这个，叫作"三的法则"，他们一次又一次地表明，任何稳定的、成熟的、竞争性的市场都演变为基本上 90% 市场份额的 4:2:1 比率。所以有一个市场领导者，其市场份额是第二名的 4 倍，第二名的市场份额是第三名的 2 倍。这几乎是每个成熟的竞争性市场的情况。

所以你可以这样想 AI，可能演变成一个消费者市场和一个企业市场。OpenAI，即使他们没达到 10 亿，他们仍然有 9 亿周活用户，远远领先于 Claude。我认为 Claude 可能不到 1 亿，Sachs，你可能知道。然后 Gemini 可能更接近他们，7 亿到 10 亿之间，可能与 OpenAI 不相上下。

所以，消费者市场看起来正朝着 ChatGPT/Google 争夺第一和第二的方向发展，然后可能 Anthropic 在第三，也许 Elon 出现并依靠他的算力容量起飞。然后企业市场是一个有点不同的故事，那是它自己的市场，是 Anthropic，或者实际上可能是 Google 领先，如果你看所有 Vertex 的使用情况。Google 声称 75% 的 GCP 客户是 Vertex 的活跃用户。所以 Google 在企业方面也可能占据相当大的市场份额。

这也可能是为什么 Google 股票在过去几个月绝对涨爆的原因——他们实际上在企业和消费方面争夺第一名。

MIT 的剪枝技术：10 倍效率提升

但我仍然认为，回到 Chamath 关于算力和能源容量限制的观点，在我们如何实际扩展和部署企业和消费场景的模型方面，还有很多机会。

我想强调 MIT 这两位科学家发表的一篇论文。他们发表了一篇关于剪枝技术和神经网络的论文。这篇论文表明，你实际上可以通过将大模型剪枝为小模型来将这些网络的大小减少 90%，并获得相同的准确性。然后你可以选择运行哪个模型进行推理。通过这样做，你实际上可以将推理成本降低 10 倍。每单位进入数据中心的能源你可以获得 10 倍的输出，而不会损失准确性。

这是一个非常有趣的算法技术，可以应用于现有的大模型，使它们的能源使用大大降低。如果你想想看，你启动一个非常大的模型来回答一个非常简单的问题。你实际上可以剪枝那个模型。

这可能在 AI 应用中和传统 Google 搜索中一样。搜索有一个长尾，但有几个搜索占据了大部分搜索量。比如天气是什么？电影时间是什么？股价是什么？有一组特定的事情构成了消费者能源的大部分。可能也有一组特定的事情构成了编码输出的大部分。

如果你能将 80% 的搜索或聊天界面或编码请求通过剪枝技术减少到更小的模型，然后你有一整套可以动态调用的小模型，并将推理成本降低 90%，你可以使数据中心和能源容量的使用增加 10 倍，比我们今天能做的多。

我认为我们仍处于在输出和 token 方面获得效率的非常早期，这也为 Elon 等人重新发明这是如何完成的并可能相当激进地竞争解锁了机会。

SLM 与 VSLM：垂直化小语言模型

有两种方法可以获胜。你可以投入算力，或者你可以做 SLM——小语言模型——和 VSLM——垂直化小语言模型。

如果你有一个垂直化的天气小语言模型，比如说，那不存在，但你可以举个例子。他们会有一个用于旅行的，例如。当你点击 Google 获取航班信息时，它显然会把你路由到更轻量、更快的东西，使用 Google 航班。Google 航班已经合并到 Gemini。Gemini 现在用户数紧随 7 亿、7.5 亿。

这正是我们 18 个月前在这个播客中讨论的，Freeberg，如果他们把它放在顶部呢？ 这会对他们的搜索收入做什么？ 搜索收入正在飙升，他们也在飙升。所以他们已经找到了一种平衡这两种竞争力量的方法——拥有 AI 启用的搜索结果，同时仍然让人们点击链接。他们做得显然非常出色，股票正在奖励。

我只想对你说的话补充一句话，那就是，你正在使用我所谓的人本主义——人类不直观地知道这个模型是什么。它不仅仅是一个垂直化的模型，而是会有通过自动化剪枝技术发现的模型，它们将协同工作。所以，许多小模型连接在一起。我们不通过某种人类启发式来定义每个模型，比如说这是一个搜索旅行模型，这是一个地图模型。我们不知道为什么这些模型在被分解时以它们工作的方式工作。但我确实认为这就是进化发生的地方。所以实际上一个模型变成了一个宏模型。它下面有许多较小的模型可以动态调用，这允许你以相同的能源单位进行 10 倍的推理。

Sachs 谈 Google 与 Codex

Sachs——

让我建立在你关于 Google 的观点之上。Jcal，我会说，如果有一个唯一的原因 OpenAI 没有达到其用户目标和收入目标，肯定是关于消费者方面，你必须说这是因为 Google 设法占据了有意义的份额。一年前左右他们基本上无处可见。Sergey 从退休中复出，帮助公司聚焦，正如你所说，他们在改进 Gemini 和把它放在搜索的顶部、整合它方面做得很出色。

也就是说，再次，我不认为 OpenAI 的所有消息都是坏的，因为我认为 5.5 的发布很棒。我们听到了关于 Codex 的非常好的消息。我确实认为 Codex 现在在编码 token 方面正在占据份额。我只是认为我们处在一个非常有趣的位置，这些公司不断在彼此之上加码。我的意思是，两周前，看起来 Anthropic 将完全占主导地位，对吧？我的意思是，Anthropic 增长了 10 倍。OpenAI 增长了 3 倍——

然后服务器开始宕机。你这周看到了吗？服务器宕机。我办公室里的人在抱怨我们无法登录 Claude。

听着，竞争激发出每个人最好的一面。Anthropic 迫使 OpenAI 竞争。Google 在消费者方面迫使 OpenAI 竞争。我只是希望市场尽可能长时间地保持竞争性。我确实认为这对消费者、我们的经济和我们的国家最有利。

网络安全：GPT 5.5 Cyber

让我说 OpenAI 度过了好的一周的另一个领域是这个炙手可热的网络安全领域。显然，Anthropic 凭借 Mythos 制造了巨大的轰动。它还没有商业发布。他们受算力限制，但作为概念证明或训练模型，它在网络安全方面达到了新的能力水平。

但现在 OpenAI 已经发布了一个名为 GPT 5.5 cyber 的新模型，刚刚通过了一系列测试，他们已经表明——这是 AI 安全研究所做的测试——GPT 5.5 是第二个端到端完成他们的多步网络攻击模拟的模型。所以它具有与 Mythos 相同水平的能力，并且似乎已经准备好商业化。你知道，他们有算力来服务它。所以我确实认为这是一个大成就。

我的意思是，看，我们知道其他网络模型即将到来。不仅仅是 Mythos。事实上，在大约 6 个月内，所有前沿模型都将具有 Mythos 级别的网络能力。但 OpenAI 这么快推出 GPT 5.5 cyber 是令人印象深刻的，我认为 5.5 可能是网络防御者实际上可以使用的第一个网络模型，因为我再次不认为他们像 Anthropic 那样受算力限制。

这是一个令人难以置信的机会，你知道，对于 CrowdStrike 和 Palo Alto Networks 这样的公司——这两家公司都在节目中出现过——他们出来开始攻击这个领域，伙计，你真的可以看到一切都被加固。这可能是每个拥有 Cursor、Claude 或 OpenAI 或 Gemini 的人的令人难以置信的收入流。这是一个加固的惊人机会，就像被攻击一样。

Mythos 不是末日武器

我能就此发表一个观点吗？因为看，现在对 Mythos 几乎到了恐慌的程度。人们把它当作末日武器或类似的东西。它不是。它只是前沿模型已经达到了它们能够自动化网络活动的程度，就像它们能够自动化编码一样。但这意味着模型可以增强网络攻击者或网络防御者的能力，就像它们可以增强编码者的能力一样，并允许他们发现更多的漏洞。

显然有一个风险。但我认为重要的是要理解，Mythos 或 GPT 5.5，它不创造漏洞。它只是发现它们。bug 已经在代码中。它们坐在那里等待一些黑客发现。如果我们现在可以使用 AI 提前找到这些 bug，这些漏洞并修补它们，那么你实际上加固了我们的基础设施，加固了我们的安全。

我也相信，从前 AI 网络安全到后 AI 网络安全的飞跃，将是一个大的一次性升级周期。因为再次，你将找到所有这些休眠的 bug 和漏洞。但我认为一旦我们度过了那个升级周期，你将达到 AI 驱动的网络攻击和 AI 驱动的网络防御之间的新平衡。它会变得更正常。它不会感觉像这种巨大的破坏。

也就是说，我认为人们把这视为某种存在性威胁。我不认为是这样，只要每个人都做他们应该做的事——使用新能力来加固他们的代码库、基础设施和安全，在黑客掌握这些能力之前。

黑客 vs 白帽 vs 普通人

是的。Chamath，如果你建立在 Sachs 的观点之上，世界上大约有 500 万安全专家。我们谈到了 token 成本。40 小时的 token 不停地敲打，你知道，一周。你可以以每个首席安全官或每个安全专家 100 美元创造另外 500 万个。所以这是安全专家代理的数量，Sachs，你的观点。

是的。你可以有 5000 万个，1 亿个。他们没有发现独特的东西。他们只是从不睡觉。他们坚持不懈地追求这些问题。这是一个非常好的观点。

让我稍微完善一下。是的，网络安全行业可能有 500 万人，但可能只有几千个真正精英的黑客。当然，那些黑客没有时间去追求那里的每一个可能的攻击向量的整个表面积。所以如果你训练一个模型来做他们做的事，显然，就像你说的，它可以以人类黑客无法做到的规模和速度运作。所以显然，你需要做的是把这些工具交到白帽手中，让他们自己进行网络攻击，然后找到漏洞并在黑帽掌握这些能力之前修补它们。

但我认为这只是关于这个的最后一点，我会停下来。理解中国模型将在大约 6 个月内具备这些能力是非常重要的。

哦，他们现在 Deepseek 4 肯定有了。他们已经达到某种水平。 嗯，不，Deepseek 4，我的意思是，Deepseek 4 在很多方面令人印象深刻，但它的能力不在前沿。它可能是 80%、85%。让我们称之为美国前沿。

Chamath：机器对机器的网络战

Chamath，你想插话进来。让我们让 Chimoth 进来。

两件事。这甚至成为可能的原因是因为人类容易出错，当人类编码时，他们会创造漏洞。所以，人类利用人类是我们长久以来所处的位置。现在我们有计算机利用人类，因为计算机会出去寻找人类编写的所有这些 bug。在下一阶段，将是机器对机器。

我认为网络安全的本质将完全改变。可能在接下来的五六年内，将有很多理由重写运行世界的所有软件。一部分原因是你将被要求展示更多的运营杠杆和收入增长，但另一部分原因是过去手工制作的所有东西从根本上是不安全的。无论哪种方式，所有道路都将通向运行世界的所有运营软件将被重写。越来越多的软件将由机器编写。越来越多的软件将因此变得不可侵入。

但是网络威胁实际上只会增加，因为然后你将试图弄清楚如何使用机器将某些东西注入另一台机器，以便某些代理循环注入一些恶意软件或注入一个不好的 token。我认为这是一件非常复杂的事情。

我要告诉你的是——我甚至不确定我是否被允许说这个——但是世界上一家非常好的、可能是最好的网络安全公司，由世界上一位非常优秀的 CEO 经营，这个 CEO 可能会或可能不会在 Liquidity 上发言——会告诉你，他们已经渗透并基本上可以操纵每一个模型。让我大致这样说吧。

好的，完美。是的。我在 Breakthrough Prize 上——我们四个人中有三个在那里——我和 George Kurtz 谈过，你描述的另一个人不是—— 坐在 Nash 旁边。是的，我在说—— Nash 和 George 是领导这一切的两个人，Palo Alto Networks 和 CrowdStrike。他们理解 George 告诉我的——有一个想要这个产品或服务的人的队伍排到门外。

杀人率与网络威胁的类比

如果你看这个，Freeberg，就像谋杀率——我们坐在这里，谋杀率是人类历史上最低的。在我们的生命中它已经大幅下降，但在历史的弧线上更是大幅下降。我认为网络安全也会发生这种情况。攻击向量只有这么多，剩下的攻击向量将只是人为因素，对吧，Freeberg？这一直是这样。当我们使软件更具弹性时，弱环节就是把密码贴在便利贴上的秘书，或者使用他们狗的名字加 1、2、3 作为密码的会计，对吧？这是历史上的。

好的，Freeberg，作为我们结束的地方，你有什么要补充的吗？

闲聊：Atlanta 的酒店和 Pappy Van Winkle

哦，那是——为什么你的床这么乱？为什么你不能让客房服务进来？ 听着，我会告诉你发生了什么。听着，我在亚特兰大。 而且，为什么你没有像那种有两个房间的套房？只是一个房间吗？ 这家酒店只有一间。 只是一个房间。是的。 你知道，你要么吝啬，要么穷。是哪一个？ 我吝啬。这是情况：我今晚在亚特兰大看 Knicks 比赛。 你在一个房间里。 这是我做的。我只想向你解释价值与价值。有些人把钱花在私人飞机上，他们花 30,000 美元飞到亚特兰大。我把 30,000 花在场边座位上。我不想要套房。我想把它放在座位上。 你也可以两个都做。 我想我也可以两个都做。我不——我在转变成—— 我不明白—— 接受我的富有。好的。 如果你已经说服自己应该花 30,000 美元买场边票——我认为这很离谱，但好的。你已经说服自己每张 1 万。 一个有两个房间的酒店房间。好的。 可能比你付的多 15%。 是 2 倍，但是的，你说得对。 我会拿酒店房间。20 或多 20%，但你的房间一晚 200 块。所以你一晚付 400。你再拿一个酒店—— 我的意思是，这是亚特兰大。我所在的最贵的酒店在亚特兰大是 500 美元一晚。没什么大不了的。但一切都卖光了，因为所有 Knicks 的人都来了。 所以你在告诉我那将是 1000，你不能花 1000， 一切都卖光了，因为 Knicks 在这里。 所以我们必须看你脏床。 好恶心。 床没那么脏。来吧。处理它。好的。把它拿出来发布。

Sachs 关于 Pappy Van Winkle 的故事

我有一个关于飞往亚特兰大的私人飞机故事。 你提醒我了。好的。所以是的，那里有一些活动。所以我把我的团队飞到那里——你知道，我的飞机上有几个人——这是一段相当长的飞行。大约 4 小时左右。 从后面？是的。 是的。所以我去后面睡觉。嗯，首先，你知道，我们开始了飞行，我在飞机上有几瓶 Pappy Van Winkle。所以我们开始喝了一杯，然后我去后面睡着了，基本上当我们着陆时我醒来。 所以我出来，三瓶基本上都喝完了 Pappy Van Winkle。 哎呀。那些大约 2000 美元一瓶。 不，不，更多。这些是古董瓶。其中一个是—— 我有一个来自你飞机的。我有一个来自老 Falcon 的。是的。 是的。它们是这些复古的—— 4000 美元。我记得。是的。 反正你再也找不到这些 [脏话] 了。所以这些家伙，当我们着陆时他们问我，"嘿，Sax，你飞我们去这个活动花了多少钱？"我说，"嗯，大约 8000 美元的喷气燃料和大约 12,000 美元的 Pappy Van Winkle。"嗯，你必须为氛围加油，就像为飞机加油一样。 亚特兰大好吗？我从来没有真的—— 那些人还在为你工作还是他们—— 他们叫亚特兰大？嗯，亚特兰大好吗？听着，去年我去了底特律的比赛，那个城市正在反弹。亚特兰大有一个不可思议的反弹机会。我会这样说。他们有一个很好的机会升级城市。我昨晚午夜去了 Waffle House。没有枪击事件。好的，让我们继续。 顺便说一下，你在亚特兰大的 Best Western 得到忠诚度积分了吗？还是没有， 我得到双倍积分，因为我用我的 Best Western Visa 卡。是的，它在你想要的任何地方。好的，使用促销代码 Jcal 获得额外 1000 积分。

话题二：Musk vs Altman 诉讼

在其他 OpenAI 新闻中，Musk vs Altman——世纪审判，或者也许是十年的审判——已经开始。

Elon 当然指控 OpenAI 违反慈善信托、不当致富。他指控 OpenAI 基本上把非营利组织变成了营利组织。他寻求 1500 亿美元的损害赔偿，要求他们恢复为非营利组织，要求 Altman 和 Brockman 被罢免。

Elon 和 OpenAI 律师之间有一些火花。Elon 把讨论提升了一个层次。他说："如果我们让劫掠慈善机构变得没问题，美国慈善捐赠的整个基础将被摧毁。这是我的担忧。"

显然，这里有大量有趣的细微差别。具体来说，Greg Brockman 保留日记，他在那里像 Bond 反派一样进行 journal maxing。他日记的摘录包括："结论，我们真的想要 BC Corp。真正的答案是我们想让 Elon 出局。如果 3 个月后我们做 BCorp，那就是个谎言。看不到我们在没有恶斗的情况下把它变成营利。我只是在想办公室，我们在办公室，这个故事将正确地是我们对他不诚实。最后，它仍然是关于想要营利，只是没有他。"等等等等。

Freeberg 关于 Brockman 日记的反应

Freeberg，你对这个案子有什么看法？Elon 会赢吗？我只是不知道为什么 Greg Brockman 有一个该死的日记，他在那里字面上记录。我的意思是，我喜欢这家伙，但他在想什么 [脏话]？就像，你只是坐在家里说，让我写关于我正在犯的罪行，或者让我记录它。顺便说一下，让我永远不要删除它。我不明白这个。

这不仅仅是 journal maxing。这是 discovery maxing。 这是 smoking gun maxing。 我不明白。我不明白，伙计。 我的意思是，你们记得 The Wire 中那个家伙说，"你在记录犯罪阴谋的笔记吗？"他让房间里的每个人。我们能播放那个剪辑吗？就像，"你在做什么，Greg？[脏话] 你在记录犯罪阴谋的笔记吗？你在想什么 [脏话]，伙计？" 如果你要犯罪，你不要在你的日记中写下犯罪的日期和时间。 嗯，看，我们不知道这是犯罪。我们不要—— 好的，当然。 一个犯罪，但是的，你保留恶作剧。Chamath，你保留日记吗？ 你怎么想，Juel？你保留日记吗？ 我——我相信反刍——不，我现在告诉你，反刍是通向不快乐的道路。没有人在乎你的感受。写下你的感受只会让你痛苦。和你的配偶谈论你的感受—— 只是去吃一顿美味的晚餐，坐在 Knicks 场边，做我 30 年来做的事。 [脏话] maxing。 [脏话] maxing。注册表上升了。你所要做的就是工作。开始新项目。十分之九失败。下九分之十的赌注。一个赢了，你就赢了。去坐在 Knicks 比赛的场边。 继续。生命太短暂。 只要继续前进。不要写下任何东西。句号。完整停止。 这是好建议。是的，我只是——对我来说最大的惊喜是这家伙有一个日记。我只是——我不认识任何有日记的人。我从来没有听说过这个。所以无论如何，这令人震惊。除此之外，我对案件会发生什么或法官会做什么没有看法。

Chamath 谈反刍与治疗工业复合体

我对案件也没有评论。我觉得 Polymarket 在所有这些发现被发布的情况下都没有动这有点奇怪。Elon 赢的概率实际上是 42% 或 43%。所以，我们群聊中的一个朋友说可能发生的事情是 Elon 在技术上获胜，他只是被退还了 4000 万美元。所以，也许这就是这个民意调查所预示的。

但在一个完全独立的话题上，我认为 Jason，我知道你把它当作笑话说，但这种只是继续前进、不反刍的想法，我认为对每个人来说都是非常好的一般人生建议。

现代治疗工业复合体和药物工业复合体，我相信，是—— 围绕反刍。嗯，它确实围绕反刍展开。 是的。 那是所有这些事情的入门毒品。 是的。谈论你的问题。你知道，当这些人去治疗时，你听过这些人吗？Howard Stern 说，"我已经和同一个人治疗了 40 年，每周 2 或 3 天。"我说，"好的，治疗师每小时收你 1200 美元的停止动机是什么？没有。"然后他们失去了一个收入流。他们失去了一个客户。这都是一个巨大的 [脏话] 欺诈。事实。 关于这个案子—— 我不会走那么远。我确实认为解开人们因为成长方式而拥有的一些 Gordian 结有很多价值。但这与具体地、随机地反刍是不同的，因为我不认为有很多有成果的—— 你的生活中有一个像创伤这样的紧急问题。是的，当然。打开它，弄清楚。我只是在谈论这种永无止境的自我提升、反刍的事情。

这是陪审团审判还是法官审判？

但回到话题，Sax，那是什么——我们在谈论奥克兰的陪审团，我相信。

不，但这是一个法官审判。这很重要。这是一个法官审判，陪审团是咨询能力—— 但最终那个法官，她会做出最终决定—— 她会做赔偿。

那么 Sachs，这是一个我们有湾区陪审团法官，我们有 Elon——他被认为是有点右翼，那个地区的人不都同意他的政治。然后你有这个 Sam Altman 的纽约客故事，人们发现这么多不同的人觉得他们被他骗了。把这两件事放在一起，要预测这会变成什么是不可能的。Sachs，你的想法？

Sachs 拒绝就案件本身发表意见

嗯，是的，我不认为这是关于政治的。我的意思是，我猜你可以争辩说 Elon 寻求的——保护慈善机构——如果有什么的话，是一个左派编码的原则，尽管我不真的认为它是左还是右。

看，我不想在这场审判中站队。我和其他人一样在看。我上次对 Elon 的诉讼发表意见时，被传讯了 6 个小时。还记得吗？因为他们只是认为我莫名其妙地知道一些事情。

对。 我从来没有和 Elon 谈过这个案子。我对此一无所知。 是的。 我会看看会发生什么，就像其他人一样。

现在，我刚才读到一些报道，有一件事我会说——显然公司在某个时候确实给了 Elon 公司的股份，但他认为这有点恶心。你记得这个吗？

是的。因为在某个时候，我在我们的节目上说，当这个争议开始发生但在它成为法庭案件之前，我说，看，如果 OpenAI 在某个时候决定他们的结构错了，他们应该和 Elon 一起做修正。他应该是资本表上的股东。我不知道的是，显然他们确实尝试做这样的事情，但 Elon 拒绝了，因为他确实希望该实体保持为慈善实体。换句话说—— 根据报告，他对此有一个原则性的看法，他说不，我们正在试图拯救人类，然后你把王国的钥匙交给微软，这一切都已经显现出来。 我也没有和 Elon 谈过任何这些，但我猜测，就像这些事情中的大多数，会有某种和解或这里的事情，但也许他把它推到极致。谁知道呢。

法官 Rogers 与 Epic Games 案

法官 Rogers，做这个案子，61 岁的奥巴马任命者，政治起到了作用。Sachs，他们必须告诉陪审团，无论你对这些人在政治上的感觉如何，无论如何，请把它放在一边。但值得注意的是，她监督了 Epic Games 诉 Apple 案件，关于 App Store 排他性，对 Apple 有利地裁决，有一些注意事项——他们没有垄断等等。

所以这将是一个非常有趣的案子。我认为对 OpenAI 来说最坏的情况是他们必须以某种方式解开这个，这将延迟 IPO。这将给股东带来混乱。我猜最好的是某种和解。如果 Elon 投入了第一个 4000 或 5000 万美元，他在稀释后应该得到公司的 10%、20%、30%。

好的，让我们继续讨论案件。还有更多要讨论的，祝大家在他们的诉讼和那些在 Polymarket 上下注的人好运。

All-In Summit 广告

All-In Summit 售罄很快。我们的第五届，洛杉矶，9 月 13 日到 15 日。去 allin.com/events，演讲者将是顶级的。显然 Freeberg 把这作为他主要的创意输出。我从 Chamath 听说今天他将做一个百老汇音乐剧——魔术师踢踏。我有踢踏舞情况。

他真的是在变成一个完整的演艺人员。这将是 vaudeville Sachs 总结。他只是要把它带到一个全新的水平。音乐节目—— 像 Nathan Lane。 我认为如果你正在编码，这将是他的盛大同性恋峰会。是的，它可能是一个盛大的同性恋峰会。 这可能是我们在 LA 的最后一年，伙计们。 为什么？ 不可能。 可能。哦，每个人都想去拉斯维加斯，显然。那些骨头，宝贝。你能想象在午餐时离开峰会去玩骰子吗？Chimoth，我们得到一个新鲜的。是的，我可以。是的，我可以想象。 我有一些砖头。哦，我这里有一些砖头。我们走。Yum yum。 那样 Sachs 就可以来了。 是的，Sachs 就像，我永远不会踏入加利福尼亚，但我会。你知道，我们正在做几个现场活动。你来吗？ Liquidity 还是别的什么？ Liquidity。然后在九月有 All-In Summit。 是的，我也会做那些。

话题三：大型科技公司财报与超大规模资本支出

好的。大型科技公司在周四爆出了财报。Google、Microsoft、Amazon 和 Meta 都报告了。我不知道为什么他们在同一个晚上这样做，伙计们，但他们这样做了。表现非常出色。它很棒。

然而，资本支出公告才是真正的故事。让我把这个排好并显示图表。2026 年来自四家公司的 7250 亿美元资本支出指引——Amazon、Microsoft、Google 和 Meta。Amazon 领先 2000 亿，Microsoft 和 Google 各 1900 亿，Meta 1450 亿。

加上 Grok、加上 OpenAI 和其他一些参与者到这些计划中。我们还没听到新的 Apple CEO 的消息，但他将接任。他将有一些计划。我们将看到大规模——未来一年内 1 万亿美元的建设。我不知道这是否可能，但这一切都是由 AI 和云计算驱动的。

云收入数字

  • Google Cloud：包括 Google Suite，同比增长 63%，季度收入 200 亿美元。让那个数字深入人心。
  • Microsoft Cloud：包括 Azure、Windows Server、SQL Server——他们捆绑了一些东西使数字上升。增长 30%，收入 347 亿美元。
  • Amazon Web Services：原始云。增长 28%，收入 376 亿美元。这是一个相对纯粹的玩家。只算 Amazon 的网络服务。

显然，所有这些都在转向 NeoClouds。所有这些都在为 AI 工作和 token 服务。他们有大量的客户群，从最小的初创公司到最大的前沿模型，客户都得不到足够的算力，这一切都流向了底线。

但这正在大规模缩减自由现金流，Chamath。这些是自由现金流机器，是人类历史上最大的印钞机。但他们正在放弃自由现金流、股票回购和股息，并专注于这三者来投资基础设施。Amazon 的自由现金流下降 97%。Google、Microsoft 和 Meta 分别下降 12%、12% 和 8%。

Chamath：资本市场的结构性转变

你对这种自由现金流、自由现金流泛滥的结束以及我们看到的资本支出大规模投资的看法？

我认为我们正在看到资本市场的一个非常重要的结构性转变。我认为过去 20 或 30 年——好吧 20 年——是 Mag 7 跑得很远。这些大公司变得越来越大，吸收了所有这些投资资金，最大的原因是它有这些非常资产轻的商业模式——你只是建造更多的软件，它有所有这些杠杆，一切都行得通——也许除了 Amazon，因为他们需要仓库和交付的物理基础设施。但总的来说，这是一个非常资产轻的投资周期。

现在突然之间，钟摆向另一个方向猛烈摆动。有一件事我认为人们误解了——当它转回到这些资产重的基础设施投资时，超大规模公司正在签发我怀疑他们的身体能兑现的支票，但有一个世界他们不能。

我给你举个例子。你知道 Microsoft 说服三里岛的所有者重新打开他们的核电站吗？

是的。 你知道他们的购电协议是什么吗？是当时电力现货价格的 2 倍多。2 倍多。问题是，那不是他们整体能源需求的一个巨大百分比。

所以如果你把这个推演下去，你认为这五六家公司突然不仅仅是花费——Jason——每年 7000 亿的资本支出——他们正在花——但是从运营现金流来看，他们将花费当时电力现货价格的 2 倍，因为他们只想保证未来的需求。

所有这些现金会去哪里？它不会去股东那里，它不会留在资产负债表上。这些公司现在会被杠杆化。它们会变得高度复杂的金融工程。它们会有更多的债务。它们会有各种不同的工具和定期贷款和循环信贷和所有这些东西。所以，它们将在五年内看起来像这种庞大笨重的工业企业。我不确定那时候是否有一个好的估值案例。

所以我认为可能更简单——这就是我推特上说的——只是跟随美元，就像一年从超大规模公司流出 1 万亿美元一样，它去哪里？只跟随那些美元并购买那些公司，因为那些公司已经被低估了。

Cisco 与 2000 年泡沫的类比

这显然让我们都经历了一些事情。Nick，你能拉出我刚发给你的 Cisco 图表并把它最大化吗？我们在 1990 年代末和 2000 年看到了互联网基础设施的大规模建设。这导致很多激进的公司进行大规模支出，很多散户投资者拥抱这些股票，就像我们看到人们试图进入这些私人公司一样。Sachs，看 Cisco 2000 年的高峰。这是有史以来最非凡的图表。他们花了 25 年才回到那个高峰，他们有一个失去的二十年。我们最终有了大量的光纤被购买。我们几年前在节目上谈到了这个。但你看这个大规模基础设施时有事情可以建立。你认为这将再次成为 Cisco 系统、WorldCom 等吗？

Sachs：没有暗 GPU

不，我真的不这么认为。我们在 2000 年遇到的问题是暗光纤。你有所有这些基础设施被建设但没有被使用。今天没有暗 GPU，你知道，正如 Brad Gerstner 喜欢说的。

所以现在驱动资本支出的是对算力、对 token 的贪婪需求，需求现在正在拉动这种额外的基础设施投资。所以我认为这里发生的事情是，AI 的牛市论点在一个下午就被验证了。

我的意思是，再次，你有 Microsoft Azure、Google Cloud、Amazon AWS、Meta，他们基本上都超过了预期，超过了云收入将达到的指导，因此他们今年将在资本支出方面再投资多少。

我认为我们应该有 6600 亿超大规模公司资本支出，从去年的 3500 亿上升。我认为现在新的估计是它将超过 7000 亿。所以这——再次，它超过 GDP 的 2%。这是 GDP 的巨大顺风。还有另一篇文章说我认为上个季度 AI 是 GDP 增长的 75%。

不仅是基础设施，还有 token 经济

顺便说一下，这只是资本支出部分。这是物理基础设施。这不是在 token 工厂内部生成的 token 的经济影响。这是工厂的建设。这些 token 是如何使用的？就像我们看到的那样，它们不仅仅是用来做研究或回答问题，而是用来创建代码。所以我们看到了软件开发生产力的爆炸。我们看到了定制软件创建的爆炸，这将加速经济的每一部分。每个想要获得代码的企业现在都将能够首次获得代码。以前他们甚至无法雇用所需的工程师来生成它。现在他们将能够。所以这是整个经济生产力的巨大解锁。

然后你进入这些新用例，比如协作用例和代理。所以发生的工作流程自动化，仍然很早。我不相信这会取代人类。

9 秒删除生产数据库的 agent

我们上周有那个疯狂的案例——一个 agent 在 9 秒内删除了一个生产数据库，因为有一个 bug。看，那对我说的是——并不是 agent 没有价值。它们有价值，但它们必须被监督。

你知道，这种你只是要能够自动化所有工作的想法，是对真正技术问题和问题的大量挥手。代理必须被监督。某人必须负责。不会是 CEO。CEO 不想为成千上万的代理负责。你需要人——

尽管 Jack 和 Block 这样说。 是的。一千个直接报告是一个很好的目标，但这不现实。是的， 你需要精明的 IT 人员可以监督这个并确保它工作。他们必须对 CEO 负责。某人必须推动生产力。这就像 Bology 总是说的，AI 不是端到端的，而是中间到中间的。你必须有人做提示，你必须有人做验证，我会加上监督和责任。

AI 与美国经济同义

无论如何，更大的观点是，我说的是我不认为会有伴随着这种生产力繁荣的巨大失业。事实上，我认为现在实际发生的是 AI 正在变得与美国经济同义。

我的意思是，它产生 GDP 的 75% 这一事实，你有这种资本支出爆炸、这种能源爆炸来供养它，再次，只是这些新 token 工厂释放的应用的开始。我认为这都是非常非常积极的事情。

所有这些末日论者试图给它泼冷水，或者不断地吓唬人们。我的意思是，他们想要美国经济做什么？只是停止？我的意思是，他们只是不想要任何进步。我的意思是，再次，你知道，当你谈论停止 AI 或停止 AI 进步时，你真正做的是停止美国经济。你基本上是在说你不想要经济增长。AI 现在与美国经济的增长同义。如果没有经济增长，就没有钱来支付所有的社会项目。就没有钱来偿还国债。就没有钱来基本上建立我们的国防。所有这些我们想花钱的事情。我们必须有一个充满活力的经济。这现在与 AI 同义。

所以我知道 AI 可能不受欢迎。我看到那些民意调查。但拥有强大的经济是受欢迎的。我相信这些事情现在是同义的。

话题四：Retatrutide 与肽热潮

嘿，Freeberg，你已经变成了 Retatrutide curious。你已经——

告诉我——告诉我关于 Reta 的事情，因为我想要它。我想去用它。 我想使用它，我需要你告诉 Nat 我可以拿它。 我有一个朋友也有一些建议。

Freeberg，关于 Lily 上个月发布的第三阶段临床试验数据的报道。所以每个人都对继续显示相当惊人结果的数据疯狂。

不像 Tirzepatide，那是 Lily 今天的主要产品，这是一个双激动剂。它有两种肽与不同的受体结合，GLP-1，GIP 受体。这另一个现在还与胰高血糖素结合，这是第三个受体。胰高血糖素受体结合肽导致细胞增加它们的代谢，这实际上加速了脂肪能量消耗，超过了通常的肌肉能量消耗。它更可能在早期燃烧脂肪，导致更快的脂肪损失，但也减少了肌肉损失。

数据汇总

现在出来的一些其他数据显示： - 非 HDL 胆固醇下降 27% - 甘油三酯下降 41% - 肝脏脂肪下降 80%——80% 的肝脏脂肪减少 - A1C 在 40 周内从 7.9% 下降到 6%——这很惊人。如果你是糖尿病患者，A1C 在几个月内下降这么多，这字面上是一种救命药物 - 这个第三阶段试验的平均用户体重从 214 磅下降，减少了 37 磅。相比之下，安慰剂组在 40 周内减少了 6 磅 - 适度的副作用。20% 的人感觉比安慰剂组的人更恶心

抗炎与抗衰老效益

现在还有很多其他单独的研究正在进行，显示炎症信号分子的显著减少。所以系统性信号——嘿，细胞处于困境——触发这种炎症过程，可能对你的身体造成很多其他伤害，可以加速衰老。所以另一个对话是，Retatrutide 实际上可能也是一种抗衰老药物。

话题五：Freeberg 在最高法院

伙计们。Freeberg 有他自己的个人超级碗。你看到我为 Knicks 季后赛季做准备。我得到我的场边。Freeberg 有相当于的——Sachs，他去了最高法院听他们谈论化学品。这对他来说是一件大事。最高法院聚在一起。你穿了吗？是的。Monsanto 审判在最高法院发生，他去了——他得到了场边。他去了最高法院在大楼里听。
