When I gave that talk in 2024, what I was saying was: later on, things like Meta glasses, like Ray-Ban glasses, those kinds of AI wearables, will have cameras, and generate a lot of this data. At the time I thought, OK, over the next five years, five years from now, there could be a huge amount of data. But I didn't expect that because human data took off, so many people would contribute it, so the whole timeline moved up.

Put it this way, you thought you'd be a supporting character, but in robot learning, human data became the main character.

Right. You were planning to ride the wave of wearables.

Right. You were originally waiting for that, but then the whole community got moving.

Right exactly.

数据规模的估算

So on that point, you just said you want to make it happen, and clone human behavior. What scale of data and funding do you think it takes? Because I think it definitely won't need less data than language models. If anything, it'll need more.

Only more. Only more. Well... Mm. My estimate is probably around 100 million hours of data.

A hundred million hours.

Right, a hundred million.

A hundred million.

And today, I think the largest dataset I've heard of is around 100,000 to 200,000 hours. If a very rich company bought up everyone's data, it could maybe scrape together a couple million hours. Maybe around 1 to 2 million hours of high-quality data. So we're still 100x away from that.

Still 100x away.

A hundred times doesn't even sound that crazy.

Not that crazy.

Right, it can be done.

Yeah exactly. After that, it's probably just a data-transfer bandwidth problem and a storage problem. Then there are management issues, like filtering and exploration, and infrastructure. But the issue is: whether it's language models or video models, they have standardized representations and formats like MP4. For human data, we still don't have that, right? We haven't even converged on the modalities or sensors. So if we just push for 100 million hours of data, that could ultimately be wasteful.

Right. Exactly. This whole thing feels to me right now like a train moving at full speed, and the people with money are laying the tracks.

Laying track, right.

That's basically the situation. Basically, a few labs are out front frantically laying track, and then all the capital behind them starts feeding the train more fuel.

偶然产生的人类数据

I've also noticed you often emphasize human data. You keep saying we want incidental human data, not data collected specifically for robot tasks. Why?

Because I think human physical intelligence, to a large extent, is something we don't think about. When we do things, like opening a door with my foot, or closing a drawer with my elbow, or picking something up without bumping something else. You can't really script that. In ordinary life, you do these things. But if you ask someone to go to a data collection center and do a bunch of assigned tasks, then when they do them, they'll probably end up with fewer interactions rich in physical intelligence. Because you need to optimize for task completion, right?

So I think in the end, the paradigm is probably something in between. We may need some intentionally collected data to show that something is possible. But most of the data collected will probably still just come from daily life. And eventually it becomes more like Tesla, where the issue is data filtering: which data matters, then pull that data out and train the model again.

But the tricky part is, today's human data providers are basically working for you. Right. If you ask them to collect incidental data, they don't even know how to collect it.

Right, and that's why the research matters. If we can show this year that incidental data is useful, a lot of people will start collecting that kind of incidental data. Right?

I was thinking, I have a lot of incidental data that might mean nothing for robot learning. For example, my neck isn't great, so I like to do this. Right. What do you do with that?

I'm stretching.

That just becomes a filtering problem, right? There are definitely filtering issues. For example, there are also lots of modeling questions. If you really think it through, how do you do behavior cloning on a human? That's extremely hard. Take me, for example. I'm cooking, and I suddenly glance at the clock. Why did I look at the clock? Right? Should that be cloned too? Because I don't know the time, but the robot does. Should we clone that action? Right? All of this is why behavior cloning an agent is extremely hard.

I think there's another difference from autonomous driving. In autonomous driving, you're not really putting sensors on a person. You're putting sensors on the car. It's a constrained task. You just need to get from point A to point B. That's what most people do anyway.

Right. Most people's driving actions are valid. People rarely collect inattentive driving footage. That would be dangerous.

Exactly.

We just talked about whole-body pose, tactile sensing, and all that. My feeling is that cameras may subsume many other sensor modalities.

Mm-hmm.

For example, with hand tracking, if nothing is occluded, video can track hands pretty well. Same with whole-body pose estimation. If your camera has enough focal length, you can estimate that too. Will it eventually become a very video-heavy model?

I think so. I think that's very likely. That trend is already underway. It depends how you frame the problem. Right? These are all partial measurements. Whatever gives you a measurement, you use it. Right? It's fidelity versus scalability. Cameras already give us excellent fidelity. And it's a mature industry with low costs and solid supply chains.

Right. Like that startup you worked at in college, the tactile sensing startup. You said it had temperature sensing and all kinds of other sensors. I can't imagine how much one setup costs.

$10,000 each. [laughs]

Looking ahead, I mean, we're on a train moving at full speed, with everyone pushing forward while laying the tracks as we go. So what kind of human data do you think, in the long run, will matter most?

I actually don't know the answer. But I think these data collection companies need to work very closely with research institutions. For example, at our Georgia Tech lab, we need data to do research. And they also need to know what kind of data is useful. So it's a mutually beneficial relationship. What does the data distribution look like? What data is actually useful? How do you help one person collect better data more efficiently? I think that's something everyone needs to work on together.

数据是壁垒还是商品？

You're in academia and in industry, working with ego human data. I'm sure you can see a lot of industry trends. What I'm curious about is, do you think human data, including the capture hardware and the whole pipeline, will become a moat for frontier labs, or as competition heats up and it becomes more widespread, does it become a commodity?

I think it becomes a commodity. Because even frontier labs don't know what data they want. So basically, frontier labs end up teaching these data vendors what data to collect. But they can't tell them to sell only to them. Those vendors will sell to others too. So it becomes essentially data vendors serving as a middle layer, a platform for exchange. With this kind of interaction between frontier labs and data vendors, communication becomes unavoidable. So what data you collected, what equipment you used, it all gets shared in the end.

Right. Right, so could there be mm, a company ambitious enough to do everything in-house and build it all itself? Would you prefer that path, or should a frontier lab do the research and buy from data vendors?

I'd want something like Tesla: own everything. But as an academic, I think research should be driven by everyone. So I think it may still end up as a frontier lab driving a few data vendors.

EgoVerse 与开放科学

You recently had a project called EgoVerse. That was also about turning human data into an open collective effort for academia? Yeah. Why do you still believe in that? Because it sounds like if you need 100 million hours of data, and each hour costs tens of dollars, that's astronomical for academia. Right.

I think it comes back to that train-and-tracks metaphor. Basically, our enthusiasm for data has far outpaced the foundational research.

Mm.

The train may already be moving, and not even on the tracks anymore. You know? It's already gone. So I think we need more people doing more fundamental research: what kinds of data are useful, how to use that data better, and how to use it for different things. For example, human-robot interaction, and other more interesting research. I think data as a platform is a very good way to enable collaboration.

Second, on the data vendor side, I want some centralization. Meaning I need, through my own or my collaborators' credibility, to earn their trust. If they give me data, and do some level of quality checks, our research can make their data more credible. Then it becomes a mutually beneficial relationship.

But do you ever think that if you really pull this off, if it actually works, the endgame will inevitably become what language models are today: highly commercialized and very closed.

Yeah, I think so. If you really need 100 million hours of data, there's no way an open-science model can work. Like those earlier open-science language models, they weren't especially successful either. It's basically the same story. You need highly centralized operations, something driven by capital, to make it happen. So even if the outcome isn't as good as I hope, I still think we need to try.

In a way, your success becomes your failure.

Right. Yeah exactly. If I succeed with "scale is all you need," open science loses.

Right. Ten years ago, everyone was working on self-driving. At the beginning, people wanted to explore all kinds of data sources. Some wanted to learn from YouTube driving videos. But after ten years, self-driving converged on data tightly tied to automakers and tightly tied to hardware. Do you think robotics will take a different path? Aren't you worried robot data will also end up tightly tied to robot manufacturers?

The reason it ends up that way is that they don't share the data. Right? They just don't want to make it public. That kind of company data would help other people too. Say your car has six cameras. If another car has a roughly similar camera layout, the data can actually transfer. But because of capital interests, it doesn't get transferred. Right? Human data works the same way. If someone really buys 100 million hours of data, and doesn't give it to anyone else, you'll get the same problem. So ultimately, it's not tied to hardware, it's tied to business, to business incentives.

如果失败：哪个假设是错的？

If I gave you a prediction that human data ultimately doesn't become the foundation of robot learning's GPT moment, which assumption do you think was wrong?

I think maybe for example, my understanding of simulation-based learning is fundamentally wrong, that its scalability is nothing like I expected. That's one example. But or maybe the gap between humans and humanoids, for example, at the second or third layer, is too large, and there's just no way to get zero-shot or few-shot transfer. Another possibility is that non-humanoid robots really proliferate and take off, making human-data transfer harder and harder.

Mm right. Maybe we end up building robots with three-finger claws, tall bodies, and unlimited rotation, right.

Right. Maybe but I still think this robotics boom is driven by data. Wherever data is needed, I think robot companies will build robot embodiments that are tightly coupled to the data.

十四、全栈与系统观

Lastly, I want to ask about some other topics. One is full-stack robotics. You emphasized before that today people overemphasize algorithms and underestimate systems a bit. Right. Why are systems so important in robotics?

Because systems are part of everything. I think it's like this: Take computer science. CS itself has a systems discipline, right? PL compilers databases distributed systems. Robotics, or robot learning, is still at a stage where people haven't studied the overall system deeply enough, or rather, we still don't have very convenient systems yet. In that situation, that means people don't understand it all that deeply. So that leads to some projects, or some directions, advancing more slowly. At least I think that's probably because the systems side still isn't mature enough. So I think people need to take seriously the question of why full-stack matters so much.

Does full-stack mean building everything yourself?

It doesn't mean building everything yourself. It means you need to know what actually affects your final outcome, so you need a deep understanding of every detail in the whole stack.

Right. A lot of teams, robotics teams today, whether startups or big companies, now that the robotics ecosystem is maturing, we can buy great hardware, and there are plenty of data vendors too. Right. For a team that's truly capable of solving robotics and achieving physical AGI, how should it make the buy-or-build decision? What should be bought, and what should be built in-house?

I think you can buy everything, but your integration has to be deep enough. Once you understand it well enough, you'll know for yourself whether to buy or build.

So looking at it today, what absolutely can't be outsourced, what has to stay in-house?

For example, the whole evaluation and training loop, that's something a team definitely has to build itself. Post-training data collection, and then pre-training data filtering, and you need to check for yourself how well the data was collected, and whether it matches your expected data distribution, and the closed loop as well. For example, what we do at school is the students collect the data themselves, train the model themselves, and deploy it themselves. Every researcher needs a solid grasp of what each kind of data does, and how each slice changes the system's behavior. That requires very deep understanding. If your data comes from a vendor, it's definitely more of a black box to you. Right? How did they collect it? How good is their synchronization? Right. Would that be a challenge if you want to get to 100 million hours?

Yeah. That's a huge, huge challenge. I think a lot of people are struggling with this.

十五、模型与建模

Lastly, I want to talk about modeling. This is really what the whole community has been talking about most today. World models or VLAs. But I want to discuss it from the angle of human data, because I think every kind of data brings its own inductive bias to modeling. If human data becomes hugely successful, what kind of modeling method would it favor?

I think long context is extremely important. Again, like I said, if you're trying to explain human behavior cloning, what you're really doing is using context to explain actions. That's the core problem. Right. So if you don't have enough context, you can't explain the action. You just treat it as a very broad distribution. You could do A, or you could do B, because you don't have enough context to understand why they did it.

The human model you just mentioned, the foundation model for human data, is it a System 2 model or a System 1 model?

Both.

Right. Have you thought about what the interface between them would be?

We need a new language. One thought I have is this: Imagine someone who can't speak. When they interact normally with the physical world, they aren't using System 2 or System 1, or thinking of language as System 2 or 1 to explain their own behavior. Right? Planning and acting might happen in some other space. But what that space is, I have no clue right now.

Maybe that space has to be learned from human data.

Exactly. We need to learn that space from scratch. I think with System 1 and System 2 right now, we're taking it quite literally because we have an LLM. But again, I think grounding an LLM in action is still too far away. I think if we had enough data, we could rebuild this from scratch.

In one of your slides, you mentioned a crow called Betty the Crow and its behavior. How far are today's robot systems from that level of intelligence?

Very far, extremely far. Betty the Crow is from a very famous experiment. There was a crow, and researchers found that it could use tools to reach things. There were several tubes of different lengths, with tools inside them. It would pull a tool from one tube and use it to reach a tool in another tube, then another tool in yet another tube, until it finally got the food out. It was seeing this setup for the first time. It had never seen that task before.

I think robot planning at that level is still very, very far away. If you really want it zero-shot, and we haven't done any pre-training on that system for that kind of task, then we're still extremely far away. Because I think problems like this still depend on the ability to use the components in your system to generate a new solution on the fly. Right now, the most I've seen is maybe cups in different colors. I can pick it up and pour water from it. Like that, right? So, I think we're still pretty far off.

And for a task like Betty the Crow, if we use RL to overfit one setup, that's definitely possible. You can definitely overfit it, but generalization is still a long way off, I think.

十六、作为教授：建立实验室文化

Danfei, to wrap up, I want to talk with you about your role as a professor at Georgia Tech, and your thoughts on the future. Now that you're a professor, what kind of advisor are you?

I think I'm somewhere between very hands-on and fairly hands-off, and I bounce back and forth. Some things I really want to do myself, and some things I really let the students handle. For some open-ended exploration, and I've only been doing this for less than four years, not even four years total as a professor. So that balance, I'm probably still figuring it out. Yeah. But there really are some things I really want to push forward, and I really will just do them myself.

For example, with EgoMimic, I really did that one myself.

You were the one who built that robot back then.

Yeah, and I even made myself a gripper. During the summer, I set up a 3D printer at home and designed a gripper myself. Because before that, the gripper had broken too many times. So I made one myself.

Built one myself.

Yeah. Then one day I just handed it to a student and told them to use this. Use this one.

But you're still very interested in hands-on engineering.

Certain parts of it. I think the more it involves hardware, the more interested I am. (Laughs) Yeah. I guess I'm drawn to two extremes. On one end, I'm interested in algorithms too. Some really top-level design work is genuinely fascinating to me. And things that are very tied to hardware, like doing control, or choosing hardware, I find that very interesting too. The stuff in the middle, I usually let the students handle more themselves.

Which do you enjoy more, being an IC and writing code yourself, or working more as a manager on the faculty side, in that kind of role?

I think what I probably enjoy more is well, the freedom. I can choose what I work on. It depends on the project, but I still prefer being able to pursue my own vision with some people helping me make it happen. In a way.

Then you've probably always enjoyed that. It feels like from undergrad to your PhD to now, you've always been doing what you wanted to do.

Yeah. And now there are people helping me do what I want to do, which is even better, right? (Laughs)

So it'd be hard for you to have a PhD student like yourself in your lab.

Right. That's something that I've discussed with my wife several times. I always say I'd be a terrible student. I absolutely wouldn't want to recruit myself. (Laughs)

Right. What values or standards do you insist on in your lab now?

I actually have a lab culture doc, and a few points in it are especially important. One is that I really dislike rigid division of labor. Because right now our projects are actually quite large, and a lot of people just focus only on their own part. I think that kind of thing isn't something I like. Because you need to care about the whole thing, and you need to understand everything. No work is beneath anyone. For example, one time a motor on our robot broke. I just went in, since nobody knew how to solder it, I soldered it myself. I want all my students to be truly full-stack. who are willing to be full-stack, or even enjoy it. That might be one thing about my lab that makes it pretty unique.

So when you pick and mentor students now, what trait matters most to you?

One bias I have is: you can't hate hardware. Haha.

Right. So you hate students who hate hardware?

I hate students who hate hardware.

十七、对学术界的看法与给年轻人的建议

Right haha. What is it about academia that really attracts you now?

I've always cared a lot about thought leadership. I value that a lot. This was basically what Fei-Fei told me early on: what academia should ultimately pursue is outsized impact. You need to use your ideas to influence others, and get other people to see that something is right, and be able to persuade them of it, so you can push it forward together.

If you were giving a young student or a young researcher one piece of advice, in today's industry and academia, in the current environment, about how to find their place,

This is what I want, but I don't know how to do it. I'd want that student to develop their own taste early on, know for themselves what's right, what isn't, and what they believe or don't believe. And then, as soon as possible, turn their understanding of the whole system into something concrete. In a project, don't just care about one thing. At the beginning, try to work on things that are as full-stack as possible. Then after the second year, you can start deciding whether you want to work on more open-ended problems, or whether you just want to take one problem and really solve it, or whether you want to, for example, collaborate with industry, and take some industry problems and make them more rigorous.

Right. I think you really need to build that foundation well.

For people starting a PhD this year, doing a robotics PhD in 2026, compared with your cohort ten years ago, doing robotics research around 2016, is it harder or easier?

Harder. It's because the FOMO is just too strong now. I think right now, if you're not connected to the mainstream, you really feel like you'll be left out. So, developing taste really means being able to really think through why something is worth doing, and think through the different paths, and why people do it or don't. Right? You need that patience.

Why did people have that patience? Because maybe there wasn't a winner-takes-all dynamic. But now, clearly, in industry, there may already be a winner.

There is a winner.

Right now, there is a winner. So people may not have as much patience to develop their own taste.

Right.

I think that makes it harder. But on the other hand, there are more resources, and more tools. Now you have tools like Claude Code. Those things can make research somewhat easier. So, it's harder to stay focused, but if you want to get something done, if you choose correctly, it's much easier to do it really well.

On taste, how do you judge, over this 10-plus-year journey, how do you tell whether a direction only looks important or will really change the field? What does your internal test actually look like?

I'm pretty evidence-based. I need to see something showing signs of life before I really believe in it. But that's not the whole story. I mean, there are really two possibilities. One is first-principles reasoning: this is definitely going to happen. The other is that it's already starting to emerge. I've already seen some early signs. That's when I start to believe it.

But taste is a bit different. I don't think it's quite the same. I think taste is really about what kinds of problems you choose to work on. I'm not saying I have great taste. I just think taste matters a lot. Right now, my taste is very practical. That was completely different during my PhD. I'm not saying my taste is great now. It's just that taste, when it comes to research, is incredibly important. Right. It determines what kind of research you do.

And for you personally, what are your career goals?

I don't really have a specific goal. I just hope that one day we'll see the field of robotics reach its GPT-3 moment. That's not a personal goal of mine. I just hope I can contribute in some small way to helping this move forward. I'd be very happy if that happens. But again, when it comes to research, I don't have much ego. It doesn't matter who gets it done.

You just mentioned robotics' GPT-3 moment. How would you define that moment?

In any setting, anything a person can do, a robot can do with about a 40 to 50 percent success rate.

If you were to give younger people some advice, a lot of students are entering robotics now, and AI too, right? what would you say to yourself ten years ago, when you were just starting out?

Look more at the paths of great researchers. Ask why. Try to understand the researchers you admire and why they do what they do, not just what they did. I think that's the most important thing. Because you're really learning their gradient, not their trajectory. And if you can really figure out why they do it, in a way that makes sense to you, you'll also gain a clearer view of your own research.

If you really want to do robotics, then treat robotics as a whole field. You can't just say "That's not robotics." For example, "Control isn't robotics," "hardware isn't robotics," or, as people used to say, "planning isn't robotics." Or that "object detection isn't robotics." All of those things are robotics. A lot of people confuse methods with problems. You're trying to solve a problem, and there are many different methods. Not everyone has to use the same method to solve that problem. That doesn't mean the method is the problem, or the problem is the method.

结语：时间胶囊

Danfei, thanks for your time. Well, at this point in time, we're recording this episode, and it will stay here. I hope, before we end, you can leave a few words on this podcast as a time capsule. Maybe five, ten, or even twenty years from now, you and the audience will come back to it. If you were to leave something here, what would you say?

I think being able to take part in a field that can change the world, at the right time, and in the right place, is a very lucky thing. I don't think that's because I've always wanted to change the world or accomplish something huge that I happened to be in the right place at the right time. Rather, I think that most of the time, for me, I think of myself as someone without extraordinary talent, just an ordinary person. I just think for most people, for the vast majority of people, doing what you actually want to do will probably make your career and your life a bit easier. And even if sometimes you're a little outside the mainstream, the consequences usually aren't that bad.

So be brave and do what you want to do. Don't carry so much baggage. What's to lose, right?

Okay, thanks for your time. We'll stop here for today. Thank you.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个反直觉的发现：他不是"选择"了机器人，而是"无法忍受"其他一切
整场访谈最让人意外的并不是 Danfei 对机器人的热爱，而是他反复提到的那种**"反向推力"**——"不想做某件事的力量比想做某件事的力量更强"。从中学时拒绝应试教育，到博士时拒绝 DeepMind 的自上而下，再到坚持不做行为克隆就"政治不正确"时仍然要做——他的整个职业生涯不是被"吸引"的，而是被"排斥"塑造的。
这是一个被严重低估的人生算法：**大多数励志叙事强调"找到你的热爱"，而 Danfei 的故事说明，更可靠的导航器其实是"识别你绝不能忍受的环境"**。前者需要漫长的自我探索，后者只需要一次糟糕的体验。从信号论的角度看，"厌恶"比"喜欢"更高频、更强烈、更早出现，因此更适合做决策梯度。
## 跨领域连接：人形机器人、人类数据与 LLM 的"基础设施寄生"现象
Danfei 反复提到一个关键张力——**LLM 之所以能起飞，是因为互联网、键盘、智能手机这些基础设施"恰好"产生了人类语言数据作为副产品**；而机器人却必须**专门去创造**人类数据。这本质上是"数据寄生"和"数据耕作"的差异。

item.title: [Business of Sport] The Craziest Promotion of All Time, F1 is Back & How the Sub-2 Marathon Shows Athletics’ Big Problem
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：体育的"金字塔顶端悖论"

这期节目暴露了一个几乎没人指出的结构性怪象——一项运动越接近"人类极限的纯粹证明"，运动员反而越穷。Sebastian Sawe 跑出史上最快马拉松，奖金 4 万英镑；一个无人知晓的 Challenger 网球巡回赛冠军也能拿到这个数。这不是市场的偶然失灵，而是一个系统性的"价值蒸发"机制：

田径越是"纯粹"（谁最快、谁最远、谁最高），它就越被吸入奥运会这个四年一度的引力黑洞——而 IOC 每年从田径上获得的约 10 亿美元收入，没有一分钱按比例下行到日常职业巡回赛。换句话说：田径的"纯粹性"恰恰是它的诅咒——它太容易被国家、被奥运、被业余主义传统所征用，反而失去了讨价还价的杠杆。NBA、NFL 50% 的收入分给球员的模式之所以可能，正是因为它们"不纯粹"——它们是商业 IP，不是民族图腾。

二、跨领域连接：Chelsea、LIV Golf、F1 共享同一个"叙事免疫系统"

表面上这是三件不相干的事，但 Charlie 无意间揭示了一个深层机制——一旦某个组织在公众心智中固化成某种叙事，相同事实会得到完全不同的处理：

  • F1 在三场比赛后就要紧急修改新规、20 岁车手撞墙 50G——本应是治理灾难，却被解读成"敏捷响应"；
  • Chelsea 解雇又一个没经验的主帅——本是常规操作，却被定性为"项目失败的存在主义证据"；
  • LIV Golf 推迟一站赛事——"推迟"这个词本身就在做叙事工作，掩盖资金链断裂。

这其实是认知科学里"确认偏差 × 制度声誉"的复利效应：叙事不是描述现实的工具，叙事本身就是过滤器。Liberty Media 因为前几年口碑积累，获得了"see no evil"的赦免券；Chelsea 则被锁进了"无论做什么都是 muppet"的反向叙事监狱。这跟金融市场里"高 PE 公司错失盈利仍涨"、"低 PE 公司超预期仍跌"是同一个结构。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：足球塔楼有"结构跃迁"，但没人愿意承认它是相变而非升级

Charlie 关于 National League 三升三降的反对，是这期节目最精妙的洞见——它揭示了一个英国体育界几乎从不公开讨论的事实：EFL 第二级到第一级是"线性升级"，但 National League 到 EFL 是"相变"（phase transition）。半职业 → 全职业、塑胶草皮 → 真草、无青训 → 必备 Academy、可有可无的社区慈善 → 必须的 Foundation。这不是数量级的改变，是物质形态的改变。

跨领域一类比就清楚了——这就像生物学里单细胞生物到多细胞生物的跃迁：你不能让一个变形虫"晋升"成一只老鼠，因为它的所有底层架构都要重写。Yo-yo clubs（升降机俱乐部）在英国低级联赛的悲剧，本质上是逼一个变形虫在一年内长出心脏、肺和脊椎，然后再让它退化回去——每次往返都伤筋动骨（挖掉塑胶草皮就回不去了，因为周中场地租赁的现金流没了）。

而最讽刺的是：National League 自己用六队季后赛来制造商业戏剧性，然后又抱怨"只升一队不公平"——他们要求 EFL 为他们的商业选择买单。这跟科技公司一边制造"成长焦虑"营销一边要求政府补贴新员工培训是同一个套路。

Now, just to be clear for people that don't know, those are what you call city tracks. They're

they are very close, tight walled races, whether the racetrack, you know, you normally have far long sweeping corners which give you some chance to decelerate, but still still hit it pretty hard.

The four to five changes that have been made without getting too technical against people, they're basically done to improve safety. What I did want to say is, you know, F1 has a history of regulation change. M

what's happened this year isn't new and sometimes it does take a long time for them to actually prove valuable why you know why they work

uh the ground effect cars in the 19 in the 1980s produced aerodynamic instability

um if you well I don't remember that but you probably will the refueling era which then came after that

created a serious fire hazard in a lot of the cars but I'm looking at this again from the part positive side of going well they've made a change relatively soon after implemented because it's been seen to be dangerous.

But I think Toto Wolf's and I want to leave this with you, Toto Wolf's key point on this seems about right because he said he basically called for a scalpel, not a baseball bat.

They've taken swift action. They haven't let the whole thing play out over 6 months, 8 months or even to the end of the season with with problems escalating left, right, and center. They've jumped in and they've gone, "Okay, we've taken it on board. We are changing it." And I guess you would say that is the sign of functionality within a sport and an industry. They haven't waited for the whole thing to become sort of, you know, too too dramatic, too bad. Yeah. They have applied their scalpel quite quickly and efficiently. Well, we hope efficiently. We haven't seen yet. We haven't seen what the outturn of it is, but we assume that they've got it right the second time around.

No. And we'll get a good we'll get a good grasp of it this weekend and see. And I'll be interested to see how the drivers speak about it as well. Drivers drivers interviewed throughout an entire weekend. Um,

yeah, it will. Well, I think the other point which you kind of touched on at the start of this conversation is we still don't know yet what the impact this is going to be on on on viewers. Yeah.

Um on on whether these changes actually are good for racing full stop.

Um because the outturn from an from an uneducated perspective, so from a normal viewer who just likes watching cars racing as opposed to the more technical level of people who's really interested in the intricacies of what's happening with the engines. Basically, what this has led to is more overtaking. So the question is is you know which something something that Max Vstappen is sneerary about and which Lewis Hamilton says he likes. We don't yet know whether the viewing public likes to see what I think it was Vstappen or somebody else called Mario Racing where it's literally backwards and forwards backwards and forwards backwards and forwards or if you know actually they prefer it when an overtaking maneuver is it happens but it's a rare event. So when it does happen, it's actually dramatic because I have to say when I've been live, been at Silverston or when I was working on the Bahrain Grand Prix and you're watching the race and you're aware like sort of it's it's a big battle and it's very hard to get past when the overtaking maneuver eventually happens, the whole place lifts off because they're in recognition that was an amazing piece of driving to be able to pull that off under the circumstances. So, I just am interested on whether making it a more common occurrence genuinely adds to the sport for the mass viewing public or it doesn't.

I have found the first few races an enjoyable consumer experience, but I also firmly understand that I don't want what we do not want to see is a return to days where drivers get in cars have major accidents and die.

Yeah, absolutely.

So, yeah,

you know, I want to enjoy it, but I don't want it to be dangerous to do so.

Toto Wolff 收购 Alpine？Zak Brown 的反对

Finally, just on Formula 1 before we before we move on, um have you seen some of the comments that Zack Brown's made about the potential Toto Wolf?

Yes. Yes. Now, this is something that we highlighted quite early on in our um breakdown journey um is that there was the possibility or in fact not the possibility there was a sort of corporate battle going on in terms of who um who would be able to buy um which which team was it is it Charlie the Alpine team and there were two contenders for this. one of which was a consortium led by Christian her coming back into the sport to introduce a new independent team that would be able to compete and the other one of which was Toto Wolf the uh the Mercedes boss effectively being allowed to buy up a second team um to help his his overall effort within within the industry and I think at the time our conclusion was that we wanted Horner back and we wanted another independent team we wanted more competition

what's Zach Brown said about it

well Zach's actually Zack Brown being McLaren boss

Zack Brown is the Yeah, the CEO of McLaren. Um, he said very something very similar. Uh, Zach's Zach's always been very consistent on on his position on this. So, I'm going to say this cuz he's he's treading a slightly careful line in saying that he doesn't want Toto and Mercedes to have it because Mercedes supply the power unit for McLaren. So, he needs to have a good relationship there, but I think he can say what he's said because he has always been very consistent. He does not agree with teams with an A and B team structure, should we say. He doesn't he doesn't agree in this day and age where Formula 1 is so strong. Two owners having two teams.

Yeah.

Sorry, the same owner having two teams.

Yes.

Um, Otro Capital and Renault own Alpine. Otro Capital is a financer. They have 24%. Renault is the car manufacturer. They have 76% but key a bit Jim Ratliffy and Man United. Otro Capital's 24% is uh like the should we say the sporting operational side. So, whoever owns that 24% will have a huge say of how the team is run.

Zach's key point, right, is that it creates unfair advantages for teams to have a direct relationship. And he's not wrong. Of course, he's not wrong. The reason it it exists in one part on the grid at the moment in Red Bulls and Racing Bulls. Now, the environment for Formula 1 is very, very different when that deal happened. It happened in 2005 where basically not valuable assets. They were being given away pretty much.

They weren't just not valuable, Charlie. There was there were seasons when you couldn't fill the whole grid up.

Mhm.

So

So anyone who wanted a team

Yeah.

you were kind of like come in and do it. So Red Bull taking two. Brilliant. That's one less we have to worry about.

Absolutely.

Now fast forward 21 years, the sport is in a completely different place and these are now multi-billion assets even at the at the back end of the grid. So Zach's point is this compromises sporting integrity and fairness. The sport is in the strongest it should has ever been. and his quote, "Can you imagine a Premier League game where you've got two teams competing owned by the same person?" Yeah. No. Couldn't. It's banned. Right. Can't have that.

So, he's basically trying to get he has previously he's been suggesting that, you know, FIA should force Red Bull to sell one of those teams.

Yeah.

Now,

that's of the existing landscape, let alone letting Mercedes purchase a well a very significant stake in another team.

And he, you know, previously has highlighted examples of why it's unfair. Um Daniel Ricardo, which we've mentioned before in 2024, took the fastest lap, basically changed his tires for the last lap of the race and took it off Lando Norris, which got an extra point at the time that had a say in the title in the championship race. But also, this is the other interesting one

which people, you know, must must consider and it's the movement of the executive between teams. So Red Bull when Christian Horner left just took Lauren Mecky's from um Racing Bulls a very respected and established engineer/

team principal. McLaren have just signed Vstappen's race engineer called Jeier Lambas from Red Bull but he can't join until 2028 because he has to see out his contract

at at McLaren. the free flowing movement of executives on one side versus we're getting a top level engineer from another and we have to pay compensation and wait for his contract to end. So there's a driver perspective and what happens on the track. There's a development perspective what happens behind the scenes. Toto very specifically to be clear said this is not a feeder team play. His quote is no and I'm telling you in big capital letters no we do not want to create a feeder team. Well but surely it will be.

Look, there's a very good reason why in football, not only you not allowed to own um two clubs in the same domestic ecosystem, even at UEFA level, as became clear with the whole controversy over Crystal Palace last summer, where the fact that John Texter had owned 40% of Palace but also owned Olympic Leon, that was enough basically to push um uh Palace out of the Europa League.

Okay. So that even though they were in totally different countries, even though the likelihood of them actually ending up facing each other was very very small, they were palace were pushed down and out of the Europa League because UEFA couldn't accept it. It's partially to do with reality. So let's just imagine that there was a situation where you were coming to the last race of the season and it was a pitch battle as you would want it to be between Mercedes and McLaren and there was a situation that happened out on the track where Toto Wolf being desperate for Mercedes to win that was in a position to influence how his other team acted during that race. No one can tell me that there wouldn't be a a more than a temptation, a major serious obvious incentive to try and control control things in that way as you mentioned with Ricardo um in the Red Bull group.

But then there's also perception as well. And perception is really important because ultimately it's all about the punters. It's all about that, the audience that drives the commercials and drives the TV audience. So even if Toto could put his hand on his heart and say, "No, I would never do that." the questions would be out there every race. Surely he must be directing what's going on. He controls the sporting operation there. He controls the sporting operation here that and that that is a major perception problem. You just can't get around it. It just is.

So I think you know you and I agreed on this 20 years ago. It was a question of needs must and that's when you had Red Bull and Toro Russo as it was then called and Bernie who was running the sport at the time needed to fill out the grid. Okay, if you guys want to have two teams, have two teams because I need to make sure we've got enough cars racing. Otherwise, we got a serious problem. They don't need that anymore. If there is a spare place on the grid for an expansion franchise, let's say, imagine what that would go for. It would go for absolutely vast amounts of money. So, there's no need to bend to somebody's desire to start monopolizing different parts of the grid. And I agree with Zack Brown. I think there should be a three-year um period when Red Bull are told right over the next three years you need you need to divest that team right also it's a sellers market why wouldn't you I mean make a lot of money out of it going to make a lot of money out of it but from the sporting perspective the more competitive teams you have on the grid the better

completely agree

let's move on

National League 史诗一战：York vs Rochdale

football finally quickly because we because we had the most incredible scenario at the weekend happen

now this is very domestic I think we have to say again to our foreign audiences we've been discussing F1 we've been discussing World Athletics. Those are genuine global things. This is quite domestic.

Strap in because it's amazing, right? It's domestic, but it's an incredible uh story.

Sporting contest.

Yeah. So, I lay it up and then you're going to smash it home for all the beliefs that that you have on this, which are genuinely different to anything I've heard. So, I cannot wait kind of wait for you to make me look silly. Right. So, this season

I I could never do that, Charles.

Only you do it yourself.

Very good.

Uh so the National League uh the the the top football league outside of um the well the EFL which is Championship League One League Two has just had the most incredible season. York City and Rodale have both had the most incredible run getting over 100 points a piece and this was neck and neck throughout the whole season. It was teed up perfectly because what was the final game of the season? The final game was Rodale versus York at Rodale.

And my god, it it it didn't disappoint. So going into the game, uh York run 107 points, Rodale run 105. Rodale win and they're up. York draw or win and they're up. Rodale scored a 95th minute goal and the stadium goes mental like pitch invasion.

The whole thing even though there's still time in the game left. Pitch invasion.

They think they're up. Of course they do. Um now there's only one team that goes up automatically which is what the root what's going to be grounded in this um in this conversation. One team goes up automatically and then there's a playoff underneath. So watchell score 105th pitch invasion. Finally they get the fans off etc. You know everyone calms down a bit. Game restarts 103rd minute. So 13 minutes into stoppage time York City score and York City go up.

And actually the most amazing thing about this, go and watch the game, guys. So literally go and take a look because I'm the the the linesman, the referee to give this goal is brilliant because it's a it's a line J. It doesn't hit the net. It's cleared just behind the line and it would no goal line technology.

No goal line technology. It would be so easy for that to have been missed and it wasn't in the most high pressure environment. Anyway, York City score

to equalize

to equalize 1-1. York City end on 108 points. Rodale end on 106. Now I'm looking at that going that is incredible. I'm also looking at it going that is ridiculous. How can a team get 106 points and not get promoted? What a joke. National league sorted out two automatic places. Well, how can the National League sort it out? Sorry. What you're asking National League, EFL, like whoever football governance, just sort that out. Two teams up automatically. That can't happen.

You asking for my opinion?

Yeah.

You You're actually looking like you got a slightly thick look on your face as if you're actually waiting to be slapped.

"Three Up" 之争与英国足球升降级历史

I know. I want you to So So Charlie is jumping on board a bandwagon which has been created by the National League. It's called Three Up.

Yeah.

Okay. So bandwagon. So at the well it's the National League as a whole but Jeff Selling is a very prominent pusher of this because his club Hartool is a National League club. Okay. So the National League just to reiterate what Charlie says is the fifth tier of English football. So it sits just outside the top four divisions and is the top tier of what we call the non-league game right the non-league pyramid. And the non-league pyramid starts at very very low levels almost at kind of village football level. and you can get promoted all the way up to the National League. And if you come either in first place in the National League or win the next six team playoff, as Charlie said, those two teams then progress to the bottom division of the football league and two teams from the football league get relegated back into the National League and they become what is called a non-league club. So the National League is the top of non-league and then you get the football league which is the the three division pyramid running up to the of the of the Premier League.

Okay. So, the National League earlier this year under the leadership of their chief executive Phil Alexander, who is a long-standing friend of mine, the former chief executive of Crystal Palace and a great man who I have nothing but respect for, but genuinely genuinely and I totally respect Phil's campaign on behalf of his clubs to get three promotion places to go up to the football league and therefore three relegation places from the football league to the National League. When sorry, can I just be clear? When you say three places, do you mean so one more automatic place plus the playoff place?

Exactly that. So two automatic positions plus the playoff places.

Um, now let me explain why I think this isn't a very good idea. And to understand this, we have to go back into history. So the National League was formed in 1979. Back in those days, it was called the conference. And those of my generation will grow up knowing it was sponsored by Voxil. So was known as the Vauxhall Conference. And the two leagues it was formed from were the Northern League and the Southern League. And those two that that that part of the game was regionalized effectively, as you can imagine, the Northern League and the Southern League. And the Northern League and the Southern League then have various divisions that ran below them which were also regionalized that fed into them. And at this point there was no automatic promotion of any kind between the non-league and the league.

Now I've got sort of personal knowledge of this albeit before I was born but I grew up with the history of it which is that the dominant southern league team the dominant non-league team in fact of the 1950s was a team called Hadington United. Hedington United were captained by a man who went on to become very famous called Ron Atkinson and their leading goal scorer was his brother Graeme Atinson and Hadington United was in the suburbs of Oxford but it's where Oxford Brooks University is right now. Um, and they were basically winning the Southern League every year during the 1950s. But there was no way of getting promoted to the football league unless you got elected. And fundamentally, you got elected when the football league clubs decided that one of their member clubs was no longer fit to be in the football league. And that was normally a function of bankruptcy. One of the football league clubs would go bankrupt. They would get chucked out of the football league. They lose their shareholding in the football league. And then the football league clubs would elect a non-league club to take their place. And that election would not necessarily just be who's currently the top performing non-league team. It would be who is the current top performing non-league team who would actually make a good football league club, one of our partners, one of our business partners, one of our co-shareholders.

So what happened was um that Hennington United applied for election when Aington Stanley went bust in 1961. and uh their directors had to go on a tour of the whole of the country trying to persuade the directors of football league clubs to elect them and eventually by a narrow margin they did elect Hennington United but they made a condition they said you have to change your name to Oxford United you can't be a shareholder and member of the football league whilst fundamentally being a village team you've got to make yourself into a proper city team you are now going to be a member of the football league that comes with responsibilities that comes with you know You have to be fully professional, previously not semi-professional. You have to be named after your city, not after your suburb, and so on and so forth.

Now, hold that thought that there were conditions attached. You had to be a certain type of club. You had to be ready for it. You had to be a responsible future shareholder of the EFL,

similar to what Prem Rugy's just done now.

Exactly that. Right now, automatic promotion first got introduced in 1987. That was when the first team got automatically promoted from what was then the Conference, now the National League, into the Football League. And it was Scarra who got promoted and Lincoln were the team who finished bottom of the whole football league who got relegated. Funny enough, just been promoted to the championship. Okay. So, the idea was in those days, if you finished bottom of the pile, 92nd out of 92nd, you've kind of stunk the place out. you kind of need to go and put a cold tow around your head. Sort yourself out because we don't really want to have teams stinking the place out in the football league. So, let's make it automatic. Let's not make it about an election. Let's make let's make sure that our worst member, our worst shareholder, the one who stinks the whole place out knows we're actually going automatically. We can't hang on just because we happen not to have gone bankrupt. And it also then gave a a pathway as it were to non-league clubs to say it's now automatic. We don't have to seek election. If we are the top non-league club, we will get the privilege and the right to become a shareholder in the football league. This is what it is. It's not just being promoted into a competition. You become a shareholder in the football league when you get promoted.

Go to 2003 and they say this is the football league saying right we think that the National League is stronger now. is better now and we want to see a bit. We're going to offer a second place. Okay. Now, the National League decided that they wanted that second place to be determined through a playoff. Now, this is a commercial move on behalf of the National League. Okay. Because what it means is is it means that more teams that are in the National League think that they've got a chance.

Makes sense,

right? As it does in other divisions. Okay? Now, if we're talking strictly about what your challenge is, which is, well, it's absolutely disgraceful that a team that gets 106 points shouldn't get promoted. The only reason why Rodell were not promoted this season was because of the National League, not the football league. Because the football league says you've got two spaces. And if the National League decided that actually first and second should go up, then they would go up. It's the National League that decided first of all to have a 14 playoff and then even more controversially to extend it to a six team playoff. So actually the jeopardy we're looking at here which is that Rodale who've got 106 points might not get promoted and a team who only got 80 85 points might get promoted is entirely because the National League have decided that that's how they want to operate things.

So what they're now saying is nice bit of cake that

I really enjoy that cake and I'm going to eat it. I want to take some cake as well.

半职业到职业的"相变"：为什么三升三降不合适

Three to four up in some going like through the leagues above. That's correct. There's four four up go from league to league one,

right? That that's consistent across those leagues. So if you're saying okay from two because it's two down from league two, two up from from National League. Really are we saying here that there would be a concern that the teams that could come up if it was three up three down wouldn't be able to survive well wouldn't wouldn't be able to sustain a football league

fundamental set of requirements

fundamentally yes that is my argument but I'm going to explain what I mean which is that um the jump from league 2 to league one within one sporting competition which is the football league is a very small jump right so the requirements the structural requirements for league two club are the same as the structural requirements for a league one club. You don't need to do anything different. You you need the same type of pitch. You need to have the same type of safety standards around your stadium. You need to have a community trust. That's a club charity. You're very likely to have a proper women's team. You're very likely within the academy system to have a proper academy that that brings all the young people from your area through. These are fundamentally requirements or if not requirements very close to being requirements of league clubs. The National League, not so. The National League doesn't have any of these requirements. There are clubs in the National League. There are competitive clubs in the National League. There are clubs in the top half of the National League that are semipro whose players are not full-time.

This is a totally different environment. Club charities, well, if you want, go for it. If you don't want, don't worry about it.

And a football league club has to have a foundation.

Yep. Exactly. Academy. M if you want one great if you don't want one not a problem right so this is really fundamentally different so what the challenge is here is when a national league club gets promoted to the football league it's going to have to quite fundamentally change itself in a number of different ways okay so if you look at Brmley for instance who recently got promoted from league 2 to league one going back 10 years Brmley is a semi-pro club operating with an all-weather pitch in a community club kind of Okay, they then start to do better on a consistent basis and they start to gear themselves up for we might get promoted to the football league. What does that mean? That means we'd have to dig our pitch up, get a grass pitch because you don't have all weather pitches in the football league. What does that mean? What that means is we can't rent out that pitch during the week and that's a massive part of our revenues. Okay. Okay. So, we're going to have to start thinking about our revenue base. If we don't have the revenues from the plastic pitch, then we we've got potential revenue problems. Okay. Are we eventually going to have an academy? Okay, we have to start having a look at that. Are we going to need to institute a Brmley charitable trust? Okay, we have to start looking at that so that by the time that they actually get into the football league, they are ready to be a football league club because the last thing you want is what I would call yo-yo clubs in this particular area. Okay? A club that gets on a nice little run in the National League, gets promoted to League Two, is in no sense ready to be in League Two, doesn't have an academy, has a plastic pitch, doesn't have the right safety requirements around the ground and so on so forth, and then struggles in League Two and comes straight back down, but then has dug up its plastic pitch, no longer has the revenues that it concerned, and so on and so forth.

So, my point is is I think that two up is adequate and correct. I think it is generous of the football league to allow this overall. Historically, it wasn't the case. Historically, it was a closed shop. It's now no longer a closed shop, and they've in fact even doubled the entry during my lifetime. They've doubled the number of clubs that can come in. But I don't see why if the National League is genuinely concerned from a sporting perspective about the problem that you posed, which I do have sympathy with, which is a team could have an outstanding season and only just miss out. Fine. Get rid of your playoff. Yeah,

get rid of your playoffs and just put two up.

Now, of course, what Phil Alexander would say to me is, "But those playoffs are crucial because they give hope to the mid-table and and they're also good from a TV perspective." I go, "Okay, Phil, that's great, right? Totally get it, but you're not talking about sporting integrity there, are you? You're asking the EFL to pay for you to have the luxury of having a playoff system when you don't need to have one."

That and the yo-yo club points, you've you've got me there. Okay, I get that. And and I completely agree. And it's easy to look at this just from the

how can that happen? But then also you had a team, you know, oh the poor old Liverpool, you know, give us a trophy for finishing second. You had a team that got like 97 98 points and didn't win the Premier League and some just like

Yeah,

that can happen. So

look, I I like this. Sorry, can I really like this with you putting that across because you know the point that is continually drilled into everyone is this is wrong and we need to have more place. we need to have that extra place. So to hear that side of it is great because that's grounded in rationale and that's grounded in the broader conversations we have in football which is sustainable product improvement. You know developing the right structures in the system that actually supports all of the participants in it. That sounds like a very sensible explanation of why this is actually in place.

It's a big jump, Charlie. There are fundamental differences and changes that come from moving from a semi-professional league into a professional league. Um and those need to be recognized and if at the end of it it's decided structurally do you know what actually there are three spaces here because the depth in the National League is now so deep of the clubs who could automatically translate there. So we're not talking about four or five clubs we're talking about 8 9 10 11 12 clubs who are league ready properly league ready with their foundations with themies with all this type of stuff. Okay have a proper look at it. Okay. But do make sure that's the case cuz what I'm seeing at the moment is in the upper mid table of the National League, you've got semi-pro teams. Whe Tamworth, you got semi-pro teams. And great for them by the way, but that's an awful long way away from league football.

Charlie, excellent as ever. Thank you. That was fascinating.

Thanks, CHZ.
